structural_units: SiO4 tetrahedra linked into a 3D framework
chemical_profile.atomic_structure_notes: Cryptocrystalline to microcrystalline silica. Like chalcedony, composed of intergrown microcrystalline quartz and moganite. Banding results from rhythmic variations in silica and impurity deposition during formation.
chemical_profile.chemistry_notes: Compositionally SiO2 with trace impurities. Color banding reflects periodic changes in impurity concentration (Fe, Mn, Al) or microporosity during hydrothermal or silica-gel precipitation.

crystallography.crystallography_notes: No macroscopic crystal faces. Banding is a growth phenomenon, not a crystallographic feature. Fibrous chalcedony microstructure underlies the banding.

physical_properties.physical_notes: Banding does not significantly alter physical properties from chalcedony baseline. Porosity varies between bands, enabling preferential dye uptake in different layers — the basis of most commercial agate dyeing.

color_profiles.name: classic-banded
color_profiles.cause: Alternating microporosity and impurity concentration in silica bands.
color_profiles.conditions: Baseline agate without significant metallic impurities.

color_profiles.name: iron-banded
color_profiles.cause: Iron oxides (hematite, goethite, limonite) concentrated in alternating bands.
color_profiles.conditions: Iron-bearing hydrothermal or weathering environment.

color_profiles.name: blue-lace-agate
color_profiles.cause: Scattering effects and trace impurities; very fine banding.
color_profiles.conditions: Delicately banded form; locality-specific (Namibia).

color_profiles.name: fire-agate
color_profiles.cause: Thin-film interference from layered iron oxide (goethite) inclusions within silica layers.
color_profiles.conditions: Southwestern US, Mexico hydrothermal deposits.

color_profiles.name: moss-agate
colors: colorless to white with green or brown moss-like inclusions
color_profiles.cause: Dendritic or filiform chlorite, hornblende, or manganese oxide inclusions.
color_profiles.conditions: Not strictly a banded agate; considered a type nonetheless.

color_profiles.name: onyx
color_profiles.cause: Straight parallel banding with black bands (carbon or manganese) and white bands.
color_profiles.conditions: Straight rather than curved banding pattern distinguishes from agate; definitions overlap historically.

common_forms: rounded nodules with hollow or drusy cores
appearance.visual_notes: Defining characteristic is banded or patterned appearance — concentric, plume, fortification, or other structured color patterns. The concentric fortification pattern in cross-section looks like aerial topography or castle walls.

optical_and_special_effects.fluorescence: Variable; some specimens show greenish or yellowish fluorescence under UV.
optical_and_special_effects.iridescence: Fire agate shows vivid iridescence from thin-film interference in iron oxide layers.
optical_and_special_effects.optical_notes: Iris agate (very thin banded agate) diffracts white light into spectral colors when held up to a light source.

geologic_environments: vesicles and amygdales in volcanic rocks (basalt, andesite, rhyolite)
notable_localities: Brazil (Rio Grande do Sul — world's largest commercial agate deposits)
Botswana (Botswana agate, concentric pink and gray bands)
United States (Montana, Oregon, Lake Superior agates)
India (Khambhat/Cambay — ancient trade center for carnelian and agate)
formation_and_occurrence.occurrence_notes: Agates form by slow deposition of silica-rich fluids into pre-existing cavities. Banding reflects periodic changes in chemistry or flow during infilling. Formation can take millions of years.

historical_uses: One of the oldest gem materials used by humans; Neolithic agate tools documented
Ancient Egyptian amulets and seals
Mesopotamian cylinder seals
Medieval European gem carving and intaglio
Idar-Oberstein, Germany: major agate-cutting industry since the 15th century
Brazilian agate exported globally since the 19th century for industry and gems
cultural_associations: Named from the Achates River (modern Dirillo) in Sicily, per Pliny the Elder
Associated with grounding, protection, and balance in many traditions
cultural_and_historical.folklore_notes: The name 'agate' is ancient, recorded by Theophrastus around 315 BCE. Believed to protect against storms, calm thirst, and bring luck in Greek and Roman traditions.

interpretive_vibe.vibe_notes: Agate is a mineral calendar — each band a record of deposition. In cross-section it reads like geologic stratigraphy compressed into a hand-held nodule. The fortification pattern is one of the most compelling natural geometries.

practical_notes.toxicity: low
practical_notes.handling_notes: Durable and safe for everyday handling. Fine silica dust hazard when cutting or grinding; use respiratory protection.
practical_notes.care_notes: Generally stable. Avoid harsh acids. Dyed agates can fade with prolonged UV exposure.
treatments_or_synthetics: Dyeing extremely common in trade — reds, greens, blues, blacks frequently artificial
Differential porosity between bands allows selective dye uptake for vivid color contrasts
Heat treatment can alter some colors
practical_notes.market_notes: Very large global market. Brazilian agate is the world's dominant commercial source. Dyed agates are ubiquitous and often sold without disclosure. High-quality specimens from Botswana, Montana, and Germany command premium prices.

structural_units: AlO6 octahedra and BeO4 tetrahedra in orthorhombic framework
chemical_profile.atomic_structure_notes: Chrysoberyl is an orthorhombic oxide with Be in tetrahedral and Al in two distinct octahedral sites. Alexandrite occurs when Cr3+ substitutes for Al3+ in sufficient quantity to produce the alexandrite effect. The dual absorption bands in the visible spectrum create the transmission window responsible for color change.
chemical_profile.chemistry_notes: The alexandrite effect requires Cr3+ in an orthorhombic field (different from trigonal corundum). Two broad absorption bands in the blue-yellow region and a transmission window in both the red and green-blue create a near-balance that shifts depending on illuminant. Tungsten light emphasizes red transmission; daylight/fluorescent light emphasizes blue-green. Vanadium also contributes to color change in some specimens.

crystallography.twinning: Polysynthetic twinning extremely common — chrysoberyl and alexandrite twins create the characteristic 'trillings' (cyclic triple twins) pseudohexagonal rosettes.
crystallography.crystallography_notes: Prismatic tabular crystals with rhombic cross section. Characteristic cyclic trillings (three intergrown crystals at 120° forming pseudohexagonal rosettes) are diagnostic of chrysoberyl. Crystals often have characteristic V-shaped striations. Heart- or leaf-shaped twins common.

physical_properties.physical_notes: High hardness (8.5 — between topaz and corundum); third hardest natural gemstone. High SG for a non-oxide gem. Very durable for jewelry use. No perfect cleavage in gemologically critical direction makes it suitable for cutting.

color_profiles.name: Classic Alexandrite Color Change
colors: emerald-green to bluish-green in daylight; red to purplish-red in incandescent light
color_profiles.cause: Cr3+ in BeAl2O4 orthorhombic field: dual transmission windows in red and blue-green of visible spectrum; daylight (blue-rich) emphasizes green transmission; incandescent (red-rich) emphasizes red transmission.
color_profiles.conditions: Strong color change requires high Cr content and balanced transmission. Ural Mountains (Russia) specimens show finest change. Modern sources (Brazil, Sri Lanka, Africa) variable.

color_profiles.name: Brazilian Alexandrite
colors: brownish-green to brownish-red; less vivid than Russian
color_profiles.cause: Same Cr3+ mechanism; slight Fe contamination shifts color toward brown.
color_profiles.conditions: Hematita and Malacacheta, Minas Gerais, Brazil.

color_profiles.name: Cat's Eye Alexandrite
color_profiles.cause: Color change from Cr3+ combined with chatoyancy from parallel tube inclusions or needle inclusions.
color_profiles.conditions: Very rare; Sri Lanka notably.

common_forms: faceted oval, cushion, or round gems
appearance.visual_notes: Alexandrite is unique — a single stone with two identities. Under northern daylight or fluorescent light it is distinctly green; under incandescent (candle, tungsten) light it turns vivid red-purple. The best Russian specimens shift from 'emerald' to 'ruby' — an impossible color change nicknamed 'the emerald by day, ruby by night' gem. No other natural gemstone produces such a dramatic and complete color change.

optical_and_special_effects.fluorescence: Moderate red fluorescence under UV (Cr3+ emission, same mechanism as ruby)
optical_and_special_effects.chatoyancy: Cat's eye alexandrite — strong chatoyancy from oriented needles; combines color change + cat's eye
optical_and_special_effects.asterism: Extremely rare four-rayed star alexandrite documented
optical_and_special_effects.pleochroism: Trichroic: red / orange / green (distinct in three crystallographic directions)
optical_and_special_effects.optical_notes: Biaxial positive. RI approximately 1.746–1.755. Birefringence ~0.009. Strong trichroism means orientation critical for color in faceting. The alexandrite effect is observable spectroscopically — characteristic chromium absorption bands.

geologic_environments: Cr-bearing metamorphic rocks (emerald-like conditions but with Be)
beryllium-bearing granitic pegmatites intersecting Cr-rich schist or ultramafic rocks
notable_localities: Tokovaya River, Ural Mountains, Russia (original discovery 1834 — finest quality)
Ratnapura, Sri Lanka (cat's eye alexandrite; smaller production)
Zimbabwe (Sandawana — small stones, excellent color change)
formation_and_occurrence.occurrence_notes: Alexandrite requires an improbable geological coincidence: beryllium-rich fluids must encounter chromium-rich host rocks — two elements that rarely coexist in the same geological setting. The Ural Mountain deposits (Tokovaya River) were the sole significant source for much of the 19th and early 20th centuries. Brazil is now the primary commercial source.

birthstone_status: June birthstone (modern — alongside pearl and moonstone)
historical_uses: Gemstone — discovered 1834 in Ural Mountains
Russian imperial gem during Romanov dynasty
Tsar Alexander II's stone — named on his 16th birthday (April 17, 1834)
Art Nouveau jewelry piece (late 19th century)
cultural_associations: Named for Tsar Alexander II of Russia — discovered on his birthday (April 17, 1834)
Colors of Imperial Russia: red and green (army uniform colors)
Considered a stone of good omen in Russia — wearing two gemstones in one
Modern rarity has elevated it to extreme collector status
symbolism: good luck and balanced opposing forces
cultural_and_historical.folklore_notes: Alexandrite was officially discovered on April 17, 1834 — the 16th birthday of Crown Prince Alexander II of Russia, who would later become Tsar. The stone was named in his honor by mineralogist Nils Gustaf Nordenskiöld. Its green-and-red color change matched the military colors of Imperial Russia, making it doubly auspicious. Russian jewelers favored it highly until the Revolution. Until the 1950s, alexandrite was one of the rarest colored gemstones; Brazilian deposits have made it more available, though fine Russian stones remain extreme rarities.

interpretive_vibe.vibe_notes: Alexandrite is the great paradox of gemology — one stone, two completely different identities, perfectly opposed. Green by day, red by night. The Russian gem named for a Tsar it would outlast by centuries. No other gem changes so completely.

practical_notes.toxicity: Non-toxic. Beryllium in mineral form is not hazardous when handled normally.
practical_notes.handling_notes: Very hard and durable — suitable for all jewelry use. No concerning cleavage for wear. Safe for daily use.
practical_notes.care_notes: Clean with warm soapy water. Ultrasonic generally safe. Store separately from harder/softer gems.
treatments_or_synthetics: Flux-grown synthetic alexandrite (Chatham, Creative Crystals): shows curved growth lines and flux inclusions
Pulled synthetic (Czochralski) alexandrite: nearly inclusion-free; requires spectroscopic testing
Color-change glass simulants (vanadium glass): show color change but lower RI and SG
Color-change sapphire sold as alexandrite (different RI and color shift direction)
Color-change spinel: similar effect; different RI
common_confusions: Synthetic alexandrite (requires inclusion and spectroscopic analysis)
practical_notes.market_notes: Natural fine alexandrite is extremely rare and commands very high prices. Russian Ural specimens are the rarest and most valuable. Brazilian is more available but still expensive for good color change. Cat's eye alexandrite is exceptionally rare. Synthetic is widely available and openly sold as synthetic. The color-change percentage (degree of shift) is the primary value factor.

prompt_fragments: alexandrite: green under daylight, turning deep red-purple under incandescent bulb
Ural Mountains alexandrite — Tsar's gem shifting colors like Imperial Russia's uniform
emerald by day, ruby by night — the chromium paradox of chrysoberyl

provenance.notes: Alexandrite is a variety of chrysoberyl (BeAl2O4). mindat_id 92 is the chrysoberyl/alexandrite entry.

structural_units: Complex polymerized terpenoid resin (mostly labdanoid diterpene polymers)
Succinic acid (diagnostic for Baltic amber / succinite): 3–8%
chemical_profile.atomic_structure_notes: Not a mineral — no crystalline structure. Amber is fossilized, polymerized tree resin, largely polyisoprene and terpenoid polymers, cross-linked over millions of years. The polymerization and cross-linking distinguish amber from copal (younger, incompletely polymerized resin).
chemical_profile.chemistry_notes: Composition varies by botanical origin and age. Baltic amber (succinite) contains 3–8% succinic acid — a diagnostic marker. Dominican and Burmese amber have different chemical profiles. Amber burns with a pleasant aromatic smell (organic material). Can be dissolved in organic solvents (ether, chloroform), unlike most minerals. Age range: several million years (copal) to over 100 million years (Burmese amber).

crystallography.crystallography_notes: Completely amorphous — organic polymer without crystalline structure. Sometimes confused with crystalline minerals due to apparent glassy fracture, but X-ray diffraction shows no crystal pattern.

physical_properties.streak: white (soft enough to produce streak but organic)
physical_properties.physical_notes: Extremely low SG (1.05–1.096) — barely denser than water. Floats in saturated salt water (the traditional test to distinguish from glass and copal). Warm to the touch (poor thermal conductor, unlike stone). Burns with aromatic resinous smell. Becomes electrostatically charged when rubbed (earliest-known demonstration of electricity — Greek 'elektron' = amber). Softens at 150–180°C.

color_profiles.name: Baltic Golden / Honey Amber
color_profiles.cause: Oxidation and polymerization of original resin; iron oxide traces may deepen color.
color_profiles.conditions: Most common Baltic amber color; Eocene-age (40–50 Ma) resin from extinct Pinaceae forest.

color_profiles.name: Butterscotch / Opaque Amber
color_profiles.cause: Micro-bubbles of water and air trapped during resin flow; scattering makes amber opaque.
color_profiles.conditions: Common variety of Baltic amber; some prized for uniform appearance.

color_profiles.name: Dominican Blue / Green Amber
colors: blue-green when viewed in reflected light; golden in transmitted light
color_profiles.cause: Fluorescent compounds (polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons from combustion in ancient forest fires) producing strong blue fluorescence that dominates reflected light view.
color_profiles.conditions: Dominican Republic amber — Miocene age (~15–40 Ma).

color_profiles.name: Red / Cherry Amber
color_profiles.cause: Prolonged oxidation of surface or ancient oxidized material.
color_profiles.conditions: Old or heat-treated Baltic amber; some natural red specimens.

color_profiles.name: Burmese Amber (Burmite)
color_profiles.cause: Age (100+ Ma — oldest significant amber) and specific resin chemistry of ancient Araucarian trees.
color_profiles.conditions: Myanmar (Burma) — Cretaceous; scientifically very important for inclusions.

appearance.visual_notes: Amber's most famous feature is inclusion preservation — ancient insects, spiders, plant fragments, feathers, and even vertebrate hair and skin have been preserved in extraordinary detail. The warm resinous golden color and organic warmth are unmatched by any mineral. Dominican blue amber appears an iridescent blue-green in reflected light — among the most extraordinary natural optical effects.

optical_and_special_effects.fluorescence: Significant fluorescence under UV — blue-white fluorescence common in Baltic amber; Dominican amber shows intense blue fluorescence (responsible for its blue color in daylight)
optical_and_special_effects.iridescence: Dominican blue amber shows strong blue-green iridescence from fluorescence; some amber shows interference colors in reflected light
optical_and_special_effects.optical_notes: RI approximately 1.539–1.545. Isotropic (amorphous). Characteristically warm resinous luster. Inclusions clearly visible in transparent material; can be magnified for extraordinary detail.

geologic_environments: Eocene lignite and clay deposits (Baltic — Sambia)
coastal cliffs and beaches (reworked from primary deposits)
notable_localities: Sambia (Kaliningrad) Peninsula, Russia/Baltic — world's largest amber deposit (Baltic amber)
Poland, Lithuania, Latvia, Estonia, Denmark (Baltic coast)
La Toca and Palo Quemado, La Vega, Dominican Republic (Dominican amber)
Hukawng Valley, Kachin State, Myanmar (Burmite — scientifically critical Cretaceous)
formation_and_occurrence.occurrence_notes: Baltic amber (succinite) from the Sambia deposit represents the world's largest amber source, derived from ancient Eocene forests (~40–50 million years ago). The amber was transported and reworked by glacial and wave action — hence rounded beach pebbles along Baltic coasts. Burmese amber (Burmite) at 98–100 Ma is critical for paleontology — it preserves Cretaceous organisms including dinosaur-era feathers.

historical_uses: Jewelry and ornament since the Neolithic (>8000 years of use)
Baltic trade — 'Amber Road' trading network from Baltic to Mediterranean
Perfume and incense (burning amber produces aromatic smoke)
Medicine (powdered succinic acid in folk medicine)
Electrical experiments — first material in which static electricity was systematically studied
Paleontological research — fossil inclusions in amber (paleoentomology, paleoecology)
Varnish and lacquer (amber dissolved in turpentine)
cultural_associations: The original 'electricity' substance — Greek 'elektron' = amber (electrostatic charge)
'Amber Road' ancient trade network linking Baltic to Mediterranean and Near East
Lithuanian amber tradition — 'Lithuanian gold'
Homer's Odyssey mentions amber necklaces as luxury gifts
Ancient Egyptian and Mycenaean graves contain Baltic amber
Viking amber jewelry and amulets
Chinese and Japanese use of amber in Buddhist prayer beads and carvings
Jurassic Park (1993) — Cretaceous DNA-from-amber concept (scientifically inaccurate but iconic)
cultural_and_historical.folklore_notes: Amber has been traded over long distances for at least 13,000 years — Baltic amber has been found in Egyptian and Mycenaean archaeological sites thousands of kilometers from its origin. The Greek myth explained amber as the tears of the Heliades (sisters of Phaëton) who were transformed into poplar trees and wept amber tears of grief. Romans so prized amber that it was more valuable than human slaves by some accounts. Thales of Miletus (~600 BCE) first documented amber's static electricity — planting the etymological seed of 'electricity.' The Amber Room of St. Petersburg (created 1701–1713) remains one of history's greatest lost treasures.

interpretive_vibe.vibe_notes: Amber is fossilized time — sunlight from a forest that no longer exists, trapped in a matrix that still holds a 100-million-year-old insect at your fingertips. It is the original electrical material, the original trade gem, and one of the most emotionally warm stones in the collection.

practical_notes.toxicity: Non-toxic in solid form. Burning amber produces smoke — avoid inhalation. Not a mineral dust hazard.
practical_notes.handling_notes: Soft (Mohs 2–2.5) — scratched by fingernail with effort. Warm to touch. Avoid solvents (acetone, chloroform, ether — dissolve amber). Avoid prolonged heat exposure (softens above 150°C). Charged by rubbing.
practical_notes.care_notes: Wipe with soft cloth; avoid harsh chemicals and solvents. Store away from direct sunlight (UV oxidizes surface). Do not expose to alcohol (perfumes, hand sanitizers). Keep away from heat sources. Polish with a soft cloth and a tiny bit of olive oil if surface becomes dull.
treatments_or_synthetics: Pressed/reconstituted amber — fragments fused under heat and pressure; identified by flow structures and bubble alignment
Copal — young resin (centuries to a few million years old) sold as amber; softer, different RI, dissolves in ether
Plastic simulants (celluloid, Bakelite) — common and widespread; hot needle test (plastic smells acrid, amber smells resinous)
Glass simulants — heavier, no UV fluorescence, cold to touch
Inclusions faked in reconstituted amber or plastic
common_confusions: Copal (young resin — dissolves in ether; sticky surface; similar appearance)
Plastic (higher SG; cold to touch; acrid smell when heated; no UV fluorescence)
Glass (much heavier; cold to touch; no fluorescence; no inclusions)
Pressed amber (flow structures in inclusions; bubble alignment)
Citrine quartz (much harder; different SG; no fluorescence)
practical_notes.market_notes: Natural Baltic amber remains the dominant market. Dominican amber more expensive due to fluorescence and rarity. Burmese amber scientifically priceless but ethically controversial (conflict-zone sourcing). Insect and vertebrate inclusions command extreme premiums depending on species. Pressed/reconstituted amber widespread in low-end market.

prompt_fragments: Baltic honey-amber — 50-million-year-old sunlight in polymerized resin
Burmese amber with Cretaceous insect inclusion — life suspended in ancient gold
Dominican blue amber glowing cerulean under fluorescent light

provenance.notes: Amber is organic/mineraloid — not a true mineral. Mindat includes it under organic minerals.

chemical_profile.atomic_structure_notes: Trigonal quartz framework. Color from Fe4+ color centers (radiation-induced conversion of Fe3+/Fe2+ impurities to Fe4+ in tetrahedral sites). Natural radiation (from host rock radioactivity) converts trace iron to the color-active state. Heat destroys the color center, producing citrine.
chemical_profile.chemistry_notes: The purple color requires both trace iron (as Fe3+ impurity in Al3+ sites) and natural gamma irradiation. Neither alone produces the color. This explains why geologically young, radiation-poor environments rarely produce amethyst. Amethyst-to-citrine color change by heat at ~300–500°C is the basis of commercial citrine production and ametrine (bicolor zones).

crystallography.twinning: Brazil-law twinning (optical twinning) common — responsible for color zoning
crystallography.crystallography_notes: Prismatic hexagonal crystals with rhombohedral and trapezoidal terminations (like all quartz). Color zoning — often in angular zones following crystal faces — is characteristic. Color concentration typically in pyramidal terminations. Druse coatings in geodes are the classic occurrence.

physical_properties.physical_notes: Standard quartz properties. Hardness 7 makes it reasonably durable for jewelry (some care needed against harder gems). No cleavage is an advantage. Color fades if heated above ~300°C or prolonged strong UV exposure.

color_profiles.name: Deep Siberian Amethyst
color_profiles.cause: High Fe4+ color center density; optimal iron content and irradiation history.
color_profiles.conditions: Historically Ural Mountains, Russia ('Siberian amethyst'); now also Uruguay and Zambia material matches this grade.

color_profiles.name: Standard Amethyst
color_profiles.cause: Fe4+ color centers at moderate concentration.
color_profiles.conditions: Most commercial material; Brazil, Uruguay, Zambia, Madagascar.

color_profiles.name: Rose de France Amethyst
color_profiles.cause: Low Fe4+ color center density.
color_profiles.conditions: Light-colored amethyst; often from Brazil.

color_profiles.name: Ametrine
color_profiles.cause: Temperature and oxidation state gradient during crystal growth produces amethyst (cooler zones) and citrine (warmer/more oxidizing zones) in one crystal.
color_profiles.conditions: Anahi mine, Santa Cruz, Bolivia — virtually the only natural ametrine source.

macro_patterns: geode cavity fill — crystal points radiating inward
angular color zoning parallel to rhombohedral faces
appearance.visual_notes: Amethyst geodes from Brazil and Uruguay are among the most recognizable mineral display specimens globally. Hollow cavite filled with violet crystals pointing inward. The finest deep Siberian-grade material shows a red flash under incandescent light. Color zoning in angular patterns is characteristic and diagnostic.

optical_and_special_effects.fluorescence: Typically none to very weak; some pale specimens show weak bluish or greenish fluorescence
optical_and_special_effects.optical_notes: Uniaxial positive. RI = 1.544–1.553. Standard quartz optical properties. Color fades with prolonged UV exposure and heat — avoid extended sunlight display.

geologic_environments: volcanic geodes in basalt and rhyolite flows (primary — Brazil/Uruguay)
notable_localities: Rio Grande do Sul, Brazil (Ametista do Sul — largest geode source globally)
Anahi Mine, Santa Cruz Department, Bolivia (ametrine and amethyst)
Zambia (Kariba area — deep violet, minimal zoning)
Ural Mountains, Russia ('Siberian' amethyst — largely depleted)
formation_and_occurrence.occurrence_notes: Amethyst is the most commercially important quartz variety. Brazilian and Uruguayan amethyst forms in secondary silica-filled vesicles in Paleocene-Eocene flood basalts of the Paraná Basin — the world's largest geode deposit. Zambian amethyst comes from veins in schists and is typically darker with less zoning.

historical_uses: Gemstone since Egyptian antiquity — found in Pharaonic jewelry
Greek and Roman wine goblets (believed to prevent intoxication)
Medieval Christian bishops' rings and episcopal jewelry
Catherine the Great's major amethyst collection
Victorian and Art Nouveau jewelry
Crown jewels of many European monarchies
cultural_associations: Dionysus/Bacchus association — Greek myth: Amethystos = 'not drunk' (a-methystos)
Catholic Church: episcopal and papal rings traditionally amethyst
Associated with royalty and spirituality across cultures
Scottish Highlanders wore amethyst as a protective stone
Until amethyst deposits found in Brazil (19th century), was as valuable as ruby and sapphire
cultural_and_historical.folklore_notes: The name 'amethyst' comes from Greek 'amethystos' (not intoxicated). Greek myth: Dionysus, god of wine, pursued a maiden named Amethystos who prayed to the goddess Artemis for protection; Artemis turned her to white quartz. Dionysus, repentant, poured wine over the crystal — turning it purple. Ancient Greeks and Romans wore amethyst and carved wine goblets from it believing it prevented drunkenness. Until large Brazilian deposits were found in the 19th century, amethyst was priced alongside diamond, ruby, and emerald in the crown jewels of Europe. Discovery of massive deposits in Brazil dramatically crashed the price.

interpretive_vibe.vibe_notes: Amethyst is the purple of wine, prayer, and power — used by bishops, kings, and Dionysus worshippers alike. A geode of amethyst is a cathedral floor inside a stone egg: thousands of violet crystals pointing inward at nothing and everything.

practical_notes.toxicity: Non-toxic.
practical_notes.handling_notes: Color fades with prolonged UV exposure and heat above ~300°C. Avoid extended sunlight display. Safe for most jewelry use.
practical_notes.care_notes: Clean with warm soapy water. Ultrasonic generally safe. Avoid prolonged sunlight display. Do not steam-clean (heat risk). Store in dark when not displayed.
treatments_or_synthetics: Heat treatment produces citrine from amethyst (commercial practice for citrine production)
Irradiation can deepen pale amethyst color (less common)
Synthetic amethyst (hydrothermal) widely produced — identified by inclusions and growth patterns
Glass simulants common in costume jewelry
common_confusions: Purple fluorite (softer — Mohs 4; cubic; different RI; cleavage)
Kunzite (very strong pleochroism; biaxial; perfect cleavage)
practical_notes.market_notes: Extremely abundant and affordable. Fine deep Zambian and deep Uruguayan material commands modest premium. 'Siberian' grade (deep with red flash) most prized. Mass market driven by Brazilian geodes. Synthetic widely produced but market not heavily affected (natural so cheap).

prompt_fragments: amethyst geode cross-section — violet crystal cathedral in basalt egg
deep Siberian amethyst with red flash under incandescent light
Dionysus purple: iron-radiation alchemy crystallized in quartz

provenance.notes: Amethyst is a variety of quartz. mindat_id 1332 is the amethyst variety listing.

chemical_profile.atomic_structure_notes: Cyclosilicate structure identical to all beryl. Fe2+ in channel sites (L sites) primarily responsible for blue color; Fe2+/Fe3+ charge transfer also contributes. Heated stones have Fe3+ reduced to Fe2+, producing cleaner blue.
chemical_profile.chemistry_notes: Blue color from Fe2+ ions in the structural channel sites. Unheated aquamarine often has a greenish tint from Fe3+ mixing with Fe2+ — heat treatment converts Fe3+ to Fe2+, producing a purer blue. Deep pure-blue specimens (Santa Maria) are most valued. The deepest-blue 'Santa Maria' color (from Santa Maria de Itabira, Brazil) is a benchmark color grade.

crystallography.twinning: Rare
crystallography.crystallography_notes: Well-developed hexagonal prisms, often long, with flat basal terminations. Crystals commonly striated parallel to c-axis. Aquamarine can form spectacular large crystals in pegmatites — gem-quality crystals of many kilograms are known (Dom Pedro, 26 kg, carved obelisk in Smithsonian).

physical_properties.physical_notes: Good hardness and durability for jewelry use. Typically cleaner (fewer inclusions) than emerald. Large clean crystals are relatively common in pegmatites. Heat treatment (producing or deepening blue from greenish stones) is standard and undisclosed in trade.

color_profiles.name: Santa Maria Aquamarine
color_profiles.cause: Higher Fe2+ content; optimum Fe2+/Fe3+ ratio for deepest blue.
color_profiles.conditions: Santa Maria de Itabira, Minas Gerais, Brazil — benchmark color grade; also applied to matching stones from other origins.

color_profiles.name: Classic Aquamarine (Pale Blue)
color_profiles.cause: Fe2+ at moderate concentration.
color_profiles.conditions: Most aquamarine on market; widespread.

color_profiles.name: Blue-Green Aquamarine
color_profiles.cause: Mixed Fe2+ (blue) and Fe3+ (yellowish) contributions.
color_profiles.conditions: Unheated material; common in rough form; converted to pure blue by heat treatment.

color_profiles.name: Maxixe (Deep Blue — Unstable)
color_profiles.cause: Color centers from natural irradiation (not Fe); color fades in light
color_profiles.conditions: Maxixe mine, Minas Gerais; also irradiated-treated material sold as maxixe-type

appearance.visual_notes: Aquamarine is the sea captured in hexagonal beryl — pale to deep blue with the clarity of tropical water. The Dom Pedro aquamarine (Smithsonian, carved by Bernd Munsteiner) is the largest faceted aquamarine in the world (10,363 ct). Long prismatic crystal form with striated faces is characteristic of large rough specimens.

optical_and_special_effects.fluorescence: None typically
optical_and_special_effects.pleochroism: Dichroic: pale-blue or near-colorless / blue (moderate); orientation critical for color in faceting
optical_and_special_effects.optical_notes: Uniaxial negative. RI approximately 1.567–1.590. Birefringence ~0.005–0.009. Lower RI than most colored gemstones. Characteristically clean — few inclusions compared to emerald.

notable_localities: Santa Maria de Itabira, Minas Gerais, Brazil (benchmark deep-blue — largely depleted)
Marambaia and Nova Era, Minas Gerais, Brazil (major production)
formation_and_occurrence.occurrence_notes: Aquamarine is the most widely distributed beryl variety — found in granitic pegmatites worldwide. Brazil is the dominant commercial producer. Large single crystals are more common in aquamarine than in any other beryl variety, as aquamarine grows in Be-rich pegmatite environments with fewer inclusions. Heat treatment to remove greenish tint is standard and universally accepted in the trade.

historical_uses: Sailor's talisman for protection at sea — Roman and Greek traditions
Glasses / magnifying lenses (beryl lenses used in medieval Europe)
Jewelry since antiquity
Carving and decorative arts (large crystals)
cultural_associations: Sailor's gemstone — believed to calm seas and protect against drowning
Mermaid's treasure in folklore
Roman belief: aquamarine from the sea-god's treasury
German word for spectacles ('Brille') possibly derived from 'Beryll'
Victorian revival as a fashion gem
cultural_and_historical.folklore_notes: Ancient mariners believed aquamarine was the sacred gemstone of mermaids, cast up from the sea floor. Carrying aquamarine on a sea voyage was thought to bring calm seas, prevent seasickness, and protect against drowning. In Roman tradition it was sacred to Neptune. The association with water, clarity, and truth persists in contemporary use. The 'Dom Pedro Aquamarine' (Smithsonian's National Gem Collection) — a 10,363 ct carved obelisk — is the largest faceted aquamarine in the world.

interpretive_vibe.vibe_notes: Aquamarine is the color of shallow tropical sea over white sand — pale to deep blue-green, vitreous clear, the hexagonal form of ocean. The Dom Pedro obelisk in the Smithsonian is an 18-inch tower of the stuff. Sailors carried it for calm and protection. It still has that quality: cool, honest, clarifying.

practical_notes.toxicity: Beryllium-bearing; dust from grinding hazardous. Finished gems safe.
practical_notes.handling_notes: Relatively durable and clean — good for jewelry. Less included than emerald. Heat treatment is standard and accepted.
practical_notes.care_notes: Clean with warm soapy water. Ultrasonic generally safe (no fractures to fill). Avoid strong acids and physical impact.
treatments_or_synthetics: Heat treatment: standard and universally accepted; converts blue-green to pure blue
Irradiation (maxixe type): unstable; color fades in light — not standard
Synthetic aquamarine: uncommon commercially; identified by inclusions
common_confusions: Blue topaz (biaxial; different SG and RI; no pleochroism in same way)
Tanzanite (trichroic; much softer; different color profile)
practical_notes.market_notes: Most affordable of the major colored gemstones. Santa Maria deep-blue is most valued but largely depleted at source. Large clean crystals command premium. Treatment (heat) universal and accepted. Wide commercial availability worldwide.

prompt_fragments: aquamarine hexagonal prism — tropical sea-blue iron beryl from Brazil
Santa Maria deep-blue: compressed Atlantic light in beryllium silicate
sailor's gemstone — blue water clarity in Mohs-8 crystal

provenance.notes: Aquamarine is a variety of beryl. mindat_id 421 is the aquamarine variety listing.

structural_units: CuO4 square planar and CuO6 octahedral units (Jahn-Teller distorted)
chemical_profile.atomic_structure_notes: Monoclinic structure with strongly distorted copper coordination due to Jahn-Teller effect on Cu2+. Two crystallographically distinct Cu sites, each with different geometry. Copper hydroxycarbonate.
chemical_profile.chemistry_notes: Secondary copper mineral forming in oxidized zones of copper deposits. Chemical partner to malachite Cu2(CO3)(OH)2 — azurite forms where CO2 activity is higher, malachite where H2O activity is higher. Azurite converts to malachite (pseudomorphic replacement) under moist surface conditions over geologic time.

crystallography.twinning: Uncommon
crystallography.crystallography_notes: Forms tabular, prismatic, or equant crystals with excellent terminations. Also found as radiating aggregates, rosettes, nodular masses, and earthy crusts. Crystal faces often show deep azure-blue color with vitreous to adamantine luster.

physical_properties.physical_notes: Notably heavy for a non-metallic mineral (SG ~3.8). Perfect cleavage produces clean faces. Individual crystals can be transparent and brilliantly blue. Earthy azurite is much less lustrous.

color_profiles.name: Azurite Blue
color_profiles.cause: Cu2+ d-d electronic transition in distorted Jahn-Teller coordination; same chromophore mechanism as other blue copper minerals.
color_profiles.conditions: All azurite specimens; color intensity varies with crystal size and purity.

color_profiles.name: Azurmalachite
color_profiles.cause: Intimate intergrowth of azurite (blue) and malachite (green).
color_profiles.conditions: Interface zones where azurite is actively converting to malachite.

appearance.visual_notes: Among the most intensely colored blue minerals in existence. The deep azure color is immediately striking. Azurmalachite — the intergrowth with green malachite — produces some of the most visually dramatic natural patterns in mineralogy.

optical_and_special_effects.fluorescence: None typical
optical_and_special_effects.pleochroism: Weak to moderate; crystals may show pale-blue / deep-blue variation
optical_and_special_effects.optical_notes: Biaxial negative. RI approximately 1.730–1.838. Strongly pleochroic in some orientations.

geologic_environments: oxidized zones of copper sulfide deposits
cavities and fractures in copper-bearing rocks
contact zones with carbonate country rocks
notable_localities: Chessy-les-Mines (Chessy), Lyon, France (type locality for 'chessylite' historic name)
Bisbee, Cochise County, Arizona, USA (Bisbee Blue — famous specimens)
Broken Hill, New South Wales, Australia
Siziwang Banner, Inner Mongolia, China (recent fine crystals)
formation_and_occurrence.occurrence_notes: Strictly secondary, forming by oxidation of primary copper sulfides (chalcopyrite, chalcocite, bornite) in the presence of CO2-bearing groundwater. Typically found above the water table in the gossanous oxidized cap of copper deposits. Thermodynamically unstable — converts progressively to malachite with increasing moisture over time.

historical_uses: Pigment (azurite ground to blue pigment — used in Egyptian, Greek, and medieval European painting)
Substitute for lapis lazuli in medieval manuscripts and panel paintings
Copper ore (minor; too small quantities)
Ornamental use in beads and carvings (ancient Egypt, pre-Columbian Americas)
Blue pigment known as 'mountain blue' (German: Bergblau) in European painting through 17th century
cultural_associations: Used alongside lapis lazuli in Egyptian manuscripts and decorations
Medieval European painters used azurite for blue skies until replaced by smalt and then ultramarine
The blue in many Renaissance paintings (Vermeer, Van Eyck workshops) is partly azurite that has greened to malachite over centuries
Aztec and other Mesoamerican cultures used copper minerals as pigments and ornaments
cultural_and_historical.folklore_notes: In medieval Europe, azurite was called 'azzurrum' and was ground to produce blue paint; it was one of the few ways to achieve true blue before synthetic pigments. Many old paintings now show greenish skies where azurite has converted to malachite due to moisture exposure over centuries. Ancient Egyptians used azurite in ornaments and burial goods alongside lapis lazuli.

interpretive_vibe.vibe_notes: Azurite is blue fire from copper chemistry — secondary, unstable, perpetually converting to malachite. The medieval painters who ground it to pigment knew it would fade and green. That fragility is part of its identity.

practical_notes.toxicity: Copper-bearing mineral — avoid ingesting powder or dust. Fine dust from cutting or grinding is hazardous. Handle finished specimens normally; wash hands after.
practical_notes.handling_notes: Relatively brittle with perfect cleavage — handle crystals gently. Soft matrix specimens may be delicate. Avoid water immersion as it may accelerate conversion to malachite.
practical_notes.care_notes: Keep dry; avoid acids (acetic acid in vinegar will dissolve). Do not use ultrasonic cleaners. Dust gently with a soft brush. Avoid high humidity storage.
treatments_or_synthetics: Some azurite specimens stabilized with resin before cutting
Synthetic azurite exists but rarely in gem market
Azurmalachite may be resin-stabilized for lapidary use
common_confusions: Lapis lazuli (aggregate rock with pyrite; different chemistry; lazurite is the blue mineral)
Shattuckite (blue copper silicate hydroxide; different structure)
Cavansite (blue vanadium silicate; different SG and habit)
practical_notes.market_notes: Premium collector mineral. Fine crystallized specimens from Tsumeb and Bisbee are highly prized. Azurmalachite nodules popular as cabochon material. Earthy specimens affordable; excellent crystals can be expensive. Copper mine source-linked.

prompt_fragments: deep azure radiating rosettes on copper-stained gossan matrix
azurmalachite swirl — blue fire meeting green venom
medieval painter's pigment ground from vivid copper-heaven blue

structural_units: Si6O18 six-membered rings of SiO4 tetrahedra (cyclosilicate)
chemical_profile.atomic_structure_notes: Cyclosilicate (ring silicate). Six SiO4 tetrahedra form closed hexagonal rings stacked along the c-axis, creating channels that can accommodate large alkali cations and water molecules.
chemical_profile.chemistry_notes: Beryllium is the critical essential element. Color varieties arise from trace chromophores in the channels and substituting for Al: Cr3+ and V3+ produce emerald green, Fe2+/Fe3+ yield aquamarine blue-green and heliodor yellow, Mn2+/Mn3+ cause morganite pink, Cs gives maximum rose in pezzottaite.

crystallography.twinning: Rare; polysynthetic twinning occasionally observed
crystallography.crystallography_notes: Typically forms well-developed hexagonal prisms with flat or slightly striated prism faces. Terminations range from simple basal pinacoid to weak dipyramidal faces. Tabular crystals occur in some metamorphic settings. Crystals can reach enormous sizes in pegmatites (multi-meter specimens recorded).

physical_properties.physical_notes: High hardness and relatively low SG for a gemstone. SG varies with composition; Cs-rich varieties (pezzottaite) reach higher values. Emerald commonly has many inclusions (the 'jardin') making it more brittle in practice.

color_profiles.name: goshenite
color_profiles.cause: Absence of chromophore impurities; near-ideal Be3Al2Si6O18 composition.
color_profiles.conditions: Pure beryl in pegmatites and granites.

color_profiles.name: emerald
color_profiles.cause: Cr3+ and/or V3+ substituting for Al3+ in the octahedral site.
color_profiles.conditions: Hydrothermal and metamorphic beryl in Cr/V-bearing host rocks; Colombia (hydrothermal veins in black shale), Zambia, Zimbabwe, Brazil.

color_profiles.name: aquamarine
color_profiles.cause: Fe2+ in channel sites (and Fe2+→Fe3+ charge transfer) produces blue hues.
color_profiles.conditions: Pegmatites worldwide; heat treatment deepens blue by converting Fe3+ to Fe2+.

color_profiles.name: morganite
color_profiles.cause: Mn2+/Mn3+ substituting in the structure; sometimes Cs-enrichment.
color_profiles.conditions: Li-Cs-rich granitic pegmatites; Madagascar, Brazil, California.

color_profiles.name: heliodor
color_profiles.cause: Fe3+ in channel sites producing yellow; combined Fe2+/Fe3+ gives yellow-green.
color_profiles.conditions: Pegmatites; Ukraine, Namibia, Brazil.

color_profiles.name: red-beryl
color_profiles.cause: Mn3+ substituting for Al3+ in the octahedral site.
color_profiles.conditions: Extremely rare; topaz-bearing rhyolites of Utah (Wah Wah Mountains) and New Mexico.

common_forms: long hexagonal prisms with flat basal terminations
appearance.visual_notes: Hexagonal cross-section is diagnostic in hand specimens. Prismatic crystals can be enormous in pegmatites (multi-kilogram). Emerald is densely included compared to other varieties. Color zoning (core-to-rim or sector) occurs in some crystals.

optical_and_special_effects.fluorescence: Weak to none in most varieties; emerald may show weak red fluorescence under LW UV (Cr-bearing); synthetic emerald often stronger red
optical_and_special_effects.chatoyancy: Rare cat's-eye beryl known; requires parallel tube inclusions
optical_and_special_effects.pleochroism: Moderate in colored varieties: aquamarine shows pale blue / near colorless (dichroic); emerald shows blue-green / yellow-green; morganite shows pink / pale pink
optical_and_special_effects.optical_notes: Uniaxial negative. RI approximately 1.565–1.602. Birefringence low (~0.005–0.009). Strong pleochroism useful in gem identification.

geologic_environments: hydrothermal veins in carbonate and black-shale host rocks
notable_localities: Muzo and Chivor, Boyacá, Colombia (premier emerald)
Itabira and Nova Era, Minas Gerais, Brazil (aquamarine, heliodor)
Wah Wah Mountains, Utah, USA (red beryl)
formation_and_occurrence.occurrence_notes: Requires Be enrichment in melts or fluids, which makes it less common than other silicates. Emerald specifically needs intersection of Be-rich fluids with Cr/V-bearing host rocks — a geologically rare coincidence. Pegmatites are the dominant environment globally.

historical_uses: Emeralds mined in Egypt at Cleopatra's Mines (Sikait) since at least 300 BCE
Prized by Aztec and Inca civilizations; large specimens taken to Spain during conquest
Aquamarine used as a talisman by sailors for safe sea voyages in antiquity
Beryl lenses used as early magnifying glasses in medieval Europe
The German word for eyeglasses ('Brille') may derive from 'Beryll'
cultural_associations: Emerald associated with Venus, love, and rebirth across many cultures
Mughal emperors engraved verses onto large emeralds as talismans
Aquamarine linked to the sea, clarity, and calm in European tradition
Morganite named after financier J.P. Morgan in 1911 (via G.F. Kunz)
cultural_and_historical.folklore_notes: Ancient legend held that wearing an emerald could reveal truth — placing it under the tongue would supposedly make a person speak truthfully. Aquamarine was believed to be the treasure of mermaids.

interpretive_vibe.vibe_notes: Beryl is a color-variety powerhouse — a single species that spans the gem rainbow from green to blue to pink to gold, all from trace chromophore swaps in the same hexagonal prism lattice.

practical_notes.toxicity: Beryllium-bearing dust is hazardous if inhaled (berylliosis risk). Finished gems and intact specimens are safe to handle. Do not grind or sand without proper respiratory protection.
practical_notes.handling_notes: Emerald is relatively brittle due to heavy inclusions; avoid impact. Other gem varieties more durable. Conchoidal fracture means chips are sharp.
practical_notes.care_notes: Avoid ultrasonic and steam cleaners (especially for emerald, which is often oil-treated). Clean with warm water and mild soap. Store separately to prevent scratching softer gems.
treatments_or_synthetics: Emerald: routine oiling/resin filling of fractures (F1–F3 on GIA scale) is industry standard
Aquamarine: heat treatment to remove green/yellow tint and produce pure blue is common and stable
Synthetic emerald (hydrothermal and flux): widely available; distinguished by inclusions and spectroscopy
Synthetic aquamarine: less common commercially
Irradiation: used on some goshenite and heliodor to produce maxixe-type deep blue (unstable, fades)
common_confusions: Emerald vs. green tourmaline (tsavorite garnet, demantoid garnet)
Aquamarine vs. blue topaz, blue glass
Morganite vs. rose quartz, pink topaz, kunzite
practical_notes.market_notes: Emerald commands premium prices; Colombian emeralds with strong saturation are among the most valuable colored stones per carat. Treatment disclosure essential. Aquamarine and morganite are more affordable and widely available.

prompt_fragments: hexagonal beryl prism in forest green
aquamarine sea-glass clarity
morganite rose-peach pegmatite gem
emerald jardin vivid green

provenance.notes: All varieties, color causes, and localities are scientifically verified. Berylliosis toxicity note applies to industrial dust exposure, not normal specimen handling.

chemical_profile.atomic_structure_notes: Trigonal structure with alternating layers of Ca2+ cations and planar CO3 groups perpendicular to the c-axis. The CO3 triangles are oriented oppositely in alternate layers, creating the rhombohedral lattice.
chemical_profile.chemistry_notes: Most stable polymorph of CaCO3 at surface conditions. Mn substitution produces pink mangano-calcite; Co produces cobaltoan calcite (pink-purple). Fe and Mg substitutions common in sedimentary and metamorphic settings. Complete solid solution to rhodochrosite (MnCO3) via kutnahorite.

crystallography.twinning: Extremely common; {0001} and {01-12} twin laws produce butterfly, contact, lamellar, and penetration twins.
crystallography.crystallography_notes: Displays over 300 known crystal forms — the most morphologically diverse common mineral. Scalenohedral (dogtooth), rhombohedral (nailhead), prismatic, tabular, and acicular habits all well-documented. Perfect rhombohedral cleavage in three directions is diagnostic.

cleavage: perfect rhombohedral {10-14} — three directions forming a rhombohedron
physical_properties.physical_notes: Defines Mohs hardness 3. Perfect cleavage in three directions at ~75° (not 90°, unlike halite). Reacts visibly with dilute HCl (fizzes vigorously) — the definitive field test for calcite. Iceland spar exhibits extreme birefringence (double refraction) that visibly doubles images seen through the crystal.

color_profiles.name: Colorless / Iceland Spar
color_profiles.cause: Absence of chromophore impurities; pure CaCO3.
color_profiles.conditions: Optical-grade Iceland spar used historically for Nicol prisms.

color_profiles.name: White / Chalk / Limestone
color_profiles.cause: Microcrystalline or fine-grained aggregate; scattering of light.
color_profiles.conditions: Common in chalk, limestone, travertine, marble.

color_profiles.name: Mangano-Calcite
color_profiles.cause: Mn2+ substituting for Ca2+ in the octahedral site.
color_profiles.conditions: Hydrothermal veins and secondary deposits.

color_profiles.name: Orange / Amber Calcite
color_profiles.cause: Iron oxides and organic matter inclusions.
color_profiles.conditions: Common in sedimentary and cave deposits.

color_profiles.name: Green Calcite
color_profiles.cause: Chlorite inclusions or trace impurities.
color_profiles.conditions: Hydrothermal or sedimentary.

color_profiles.name: Blue Calcite
color_profiles.cause: Trace impurities or structural defects; exact mechanism varies.
color_profiles.conditions: Relatively rare; Argentina notable source.

color_profiles.name: Black Calcite
color_profiles.cause: Organic matter inclusions (bitumen).
color_profiles.conditions: Bituminous limestone environments.

appearance.visual_notes: Calcite's extraordinary morphological diversity makes it among the most recognizable minerals despite no single defining habit. Clear Iceland spar shows remarkable double refraction — images appear doubled. Dogtooth clusters are among the most architecturally beautiful mineral specimens.

optical_and_special_effects.fluorescence: Highly variable — many specimens fluoresce red, pink, orange, yellow, or blue under UV; mangano-calcite often fluoresces vivid pink-red under SW UV
optical_and_special_effects.phosphorescence: Some specimens phosphoresce after UV; triboluminescent when cleaved
optical_and_special_effects.chatoyancy: Fibrous satin-spar calcite shows cat's eye effect
optical_and_special_effects.iridescence: Rare iridescence from thin-film interference in some specimens
optical_and_special_effects.optical_notes: Uniaxial negative. Extreme birefringence (Δn ≈ 0.172) — highest of any common mineral. nω = 1.658, nε = 1.486. Clear Iceland spar crystals display striking double refraction visible to the naked eye.

geologic_environments: veins cutting virtually all rock types
notable_localities: Helgustaðir, Reyðarfjörður, Iceland (optical Iceland spar — historical source)
Elmwood Mine, Smith County, Tennessee, USA (spectacular dogtooth clusters)
Sweetwater Mine, Reynolds County, Missouri, USA
San Luis Potosí, Mexico (amber and orange crystals)
formation_and_occurrence.occurrence_notes: Among the most abundant carbonate minerals; constitutes limestone (a major sedimentary rock type), marble, and chalk. Present in virtually every geological environment. The second most common mineral in Earth's crust after quartz and feldspar. Biogenic calcite builds shells, coral reefs, and deep-sea ooze.

historical_uses: Building stone (limestone, marble) since antiquity
Iceland spar used in Nicol prisms for polarized light research
Marble carving (Greek and Roman sculpture, Italian Renaissance)
Agricultural lime (soil amendment)
Cement and concrete (Portland cement)
Chalk for writing
Acid neutralization (water treatment, mine drainage)
Glass making (limestone flux)
Steel manufacturing flux
Statuettes and carvings (alabaster calcite)
cultural_associations: White marble as the material of classical civilization (Parthenon, Michelangelo's David)
Calcite veining in marble symbolic of Roman luxury and imperial power
Iceland spar possibly used by Vikings as a 'solar stone' sunstone for navigation
Cave calcite (stalactites) central to underground spiritual sites globally
cultural_and_historical.folklore_notes: Iceland spar's double refraction fascinated early scientists — it was central to Huygens' (1690) wave theory of light and was used in early polarimetry. Some researchers have proposed Viking navigators used Iceland spar to locate the sun in cloudy Arctic skies, though evidence remains debated. Calcite is the mineral of civilization — every major stone-building culture from Egypt to Rome to the Aztecs used it.

interpretive_vibe.vibe_notes: Calcite is the mineral that built civilization's monuments and floors its caves with crystal cathedrals. It reacts with acid — a rare dramatic chemistry demonstration in geology. The most morphologically diverse mineral on Earth; no two habits look alike.

practical_notes.toxicity: Non-toxic; major component of antacids (calcium carbonate). Chalk dust inhalation in quantity warrants caution but calcite is not a hazardous mineral.
practical_notes.handling_notes: Soft (Mohs 3) — scratched by a fingernail with effort; easily scratched by a coin. Handle crystal specimens carefully to protect terminations. Cleavage fragments have sharp edges.
practical_notes.care_notes: Avoid acids — even mild acids (vinegar, fruit juices) will dissolve calcite surfaces. Do not soak in water for extended periods. Clean with dry or barely-damp cloth. Do not store with harder minerals that will scratch.
treatments_or_synthetics: Dyed calcite slabs common in the ornamental stone market (blue, green, purple)
Synthetic calcium carbonate (precipitated) used industrially
Heat treatment converts some calcite colors (fluorescence tests sometimes useful)
common_confusions: Quartz (harder — cannot be scratched by coin; does not effervesce in HCl)
Dolomite (does not effervesce in cold dilute HCl; effervesces only in warm/powdered)
Aragonite (CaCO3 polymorph — slightly harder, denser; acicular habit more common)
Gypsum (softer; can be scratched by fingernail easily; no effervescence)
practical_notes.market_notes: Abundant and affordable. Collector specimens of complex crystal forms (dogtooth, rhombohedral) command moderate prices. Iceland spar optical grade historically important but now mostly synthetic alternatives used. Mangano-calcite popular in metaphysical market. Marble is a major commercial stone market.

prompt_fragments: dogtooth scalenohedral calcite — pale gold clusters on dark matrix
Iceland spar rhombohedron doubling every letter beneath it
cave stalactite drip-built calcite — slow geometry
mangano-calcite pink in hydrothermal veins

chemical_profile.atomic_structure_notes: Orthorhombic structure isostructural with barite (BaSO4). Sr occupies 12-coordinate sites. Complete solid solution toward barite (BaSO4) and incomplete solution toward anglesite (PbSO4). The pure Sr endmember is the mineral 'celestite' (also called 'celestine').
chemical_profile.chemistry_notes: Pure SrSO4 is colorless to white; the characteristic pale blue color comes from color centers related to trace impurities or radiation effects. Strontium is one of the elements that gives a brilliant crimson red in flame tests and fireworks. Celestite is the primary commercial source of strontium compounds.

crystallography.twinning: Contact twins on {110} occasionally
crystallography.crystallography_notes: Commonly forms well-developed tabular or prismatic orthorhombic crystals. Tabular {001} with prominent pinacoidal faces typical. Also fibrous, granular, and massive forms. Geode fillings in limestone (Madagascan geodes) are the most famous collector form — clusters of blue crystals in hollow limestone cavities.

physical_properties.physical_notes: High SG for a non-metallic light-colored mineral (nearly 4.0). Perfect basal cleavage. Soluble in concentrated H2SO4. Flame test: strontium produces vivid crimson-red flame — useful field test.

color_profiles.name: Blue Celestite
color_profiles.cause: Color centers from trace impurities and/or radiation effects; mechanism not fully established.
color_profiles.conditions: Sedimentary and evaporitic deposits worldwide; most famous from Madagascar geodes.

color_profiles.name: Colorless / White Celestite
color_profiles.cause: Absence of chromophore; pure SrSO4.
color_profiles.conditions: Common in evaporitic and hydrothermal settings.

color_profiles.name: Orange / Yellow Celestite
color_profiles.cause: Iron oxide staining or trace impurities.
color_profiles.conditions: Sedimentary deposits.

common_forms: tabular crystal clusters in limestone geode cavities (Madagascar)
appearance.visual_notes: Madagascar celestite geodes — hollow limestone nodules lined with pale-blue tabular crystals — are among the most widely sold mineral specimens globally. The celestial blue color is gentle and consistent. Crystals often have a translucent quality with pearly luster on cleavage surfaces.

optical_and_special_effects.fluorescence: Some specimens fluoresce pale blue or white under SW UV
optical_and_special_effects.chatoyancy: Fibrous material may show weak cat's eye
optical_and_special_effects.optical_notes: Biaxial positive. RI approximately 1.619–1.631. Low birefringence. Pearly cleavage luster from structural reflection.

notable_localities: Sakoany, Mahajanga (Majunga), Madagascar (world's premier source of geode specimens)
Put-in-Bay (South Bass Island), Lake Erie, Ohio, USA (historically important)
Strontian, Argyllshire, Scotland (historical — strontium named here)
formation_and_occurrence.occurrence_notes: Celestite is the primary ore of strontium. Madagascar's Sakoany deposit produces spectacular geodes and is the world's most important source for collector material. The mineral 'strontianite' (SrCO3) and celestite were both first found near Strontian, Scotland — giving the element strontium its name. Industrial celestite mining in Poland, Germany, and Spain produces strontium for fireworks, glass TV tubes (historical), and medical uses.

historical_uses: Primary strontium ore — industrial source of strontium compounds
Strontium in fireworks (brilliant crimson-red flame)
Strontium for red traffic lights and signal flares
Strontium carbonate for CRT television glass (historical)
Medical uses (strontium ranelate for osteoporosis)
Collector mineral specimens
cultural_associations: Name from Latin 'caelestis' (heavenly) — referring to celestial-blue color
Madagascar geode trade sustains local mining communities
Common in crystal healing market — 'stone of celestial communication'
Strontian, Scotland — namesake of strontium (element 38)
cultural_and_historical.folklore_notes: The name 'celestite' or 'celestine' comes from Latin 'caelestis' (heavenly) — a reference to the pale sky-blue color. The element strontium was first isolated from celestite and strontianite found near Strontian, a village in Argyll, Scotland — giving the element its name. Madagascar's Sakoany celestite geodes have supplied the global collector market for decades and are among the most recognizable mineral specimens in gift shops worldwide.

interpretive_vibe.vibe_notes: Celestite holds the color of open sky inside a limestone cave. The name is fitting — pale blue, pearly, translucent, quiet. Madagascar geodes are small sky-caverns. A gentle mineral with a celestial aspiration.

practical_notes.toxicity: Low toxicity. Strontium in mineral form is relatively insoluble and not hazardous. Strontium salts in solution have some mild health concerns but not relevant to normal specimen handling.
practical_notes.handling_notes: Soft (Mohs 3–3.5) and brittle with perfect cleavage — handle crystal specimens carefully. Heavy for apparent size. Geode specimens need protection for crystal points.
practical_notes.care_notes: Avoid water immersion (partially soluble over time). Clean with dry or barely damp soft brush. Do not expose to acids. Handle with care to avoid cleavage damage.
treatments_or_synthetics: Dyeing of pale specimens to enhance blue color (uncommon)
No significant synthetic market
common_confusions: Barite (BaSO4 — higher SG; similar habit but heavier)
Blue calcite (effervesces in HCl; cubic cleavage)
practical_notes.market_notes: Affordable collector mineral. Madagascar geodes widely available at low prices. Larger crystals and exceptional specimens from Sicily and Ohio command moderate premium. Industrial material is commodity (strontium ore).

prompt_fragments: Madagascar celestite geode — hollow limestone cave of sky-blue tabular crystals
pale celestial-blue orthorhombic clusters — heaven's palette in strontium sulfate
pearly-blue celestite tabular crystal with pearlescent cleavage

structural_units: SiO4 tetrahedra linked into a 3D framework
chemical_profile.atomic_structure_notes: Cryptocrystalline to microcrystalline silica; individual crystal fibers too small to resolve without electron microscopy.
chemical_profile.chemistry_notes: Essentially pure SiO2 with trace impurities governing color. Composed of intergrown microcrystalline quartz and moganite in variable proportions.

crystallography.unit_cell_notes: Microcrystalline; unit cell as quartz but crystals are submicroscopic.
crystallography.crystallography_notes: Masses of interlocking microfibers without macroscopic crystal faces. Fibrous chalcedony has fibers oriented perpendicular to growth surfaces.

physical_properties.physical_notes: Lacks distinct cleavage due to microcrystalline structure. Conchoidal fracture typical. More porous than coarse quartz; can absorb water and dyes.

color_profiles.name: chalcedony-blue
color_profiles.cause: Rayleigh scattering of light through the microporous fibrous structure.
color_profiles.conditions: Pure or low-impurity chalcedony.

color_profiles.name: carnelian
color_profiles.cause: Iron oxide (hematite/goethite) inclusions.
color_profiles.conditions: Iron-bearing chalcedony; color often heat-enhanced in the trade.

color_profiles.name: chrysoprase
color_profiles.cause: Nickel (as nickeloan talc or willemseite) imparting green.
color_profiles.conditions: Nickel-bearing chalcedony from lateritic weathering zones.

color_profiles.name: onyx
color_profiles.cause: Parallel straight bands; black from carbon or manganese inclusions.
color_profiles.conditions: Banded form with straight parallel layers; commonly dyed in trade.

color_profiles.name: bloodstone
color_profiles.cause: Green from chlorite inclusions; red spots from iron oxide (hematite/jasper).
color_profiles.conditions: Specific microinclusion pattern.

appearance.visual_notes: Characteristic waxy luster on polished surfaces. Translucent with a faint glow in thin sections. Smooth, ghost-like quality distinguishes it from coarse quartz.

optical_and_special_effects.fluorescence: Variable; some specimens weakly fluorescent under UV.
optical_and_special_effects.chatoyancy: Weak; fibrous varieties can show faint cat's eye in cabochon cut.
optical_and_special_effects.optical_notes: Blue chalcedony color is partly a scattering effect, not a true absorption color.

formation_and_occurrence.occurrence_notes: One of the most widespread silica minerals globally. Fills vesicles, veins, and voids across a huge range of geological settings.

historical_uses: Prehistoric tool-making (flint/chert, which are varieties)
Seal stones and signet rings in antiquity
Carnelian used extensively in ancient Egyptian, Mesopotamian, and Greek jewelry
Bloodstone used as a healing amulet in medieval Europe
cultural_associations: Carnelian: ancient Egyptian protection and vitality
Bloodstone: associated with the crucifixion in medieval Christian tradition
Chrysoprase: prized by Alexander the Great; carried as a talisman
cultural_and_historical.folklore_notes: Carnelian (a chalcedony variety) was one of the most important gem materials of the ancient world, used in amulets, seals, and jewelry across Egypt, Mesopotamia, and Rome.

interpretive_vibe.vibe_notes: Chalcedony reads as quiet, cool, and internally lit. The waxy surface has a specific tactile quality — smooth and almost skin-like. The blue form evokes fog, still water, and negative space.

practical_notes.toxicity: low
practical_notes.handling_notes: Durable and safe for handling. Fine silica dust hazard if grinding or cutting; use respiratory protection.
practical_notes.care_notes: Avoid harsh acids or strong alkalies. Porous specimens can absorb chemicals and stains. Ultrasonic cleaning safe for most specimens.
treatments_or_synthetics: Dyeing very common in trade (especially black 'onyx', blue chalcedony)
Heat treatment of carnelian widespread to intensify color
Synthetic SiO2 materials exist but not common as chalcedony simulants
common_confusions: turquoise (blue-dyed chalcedony frequently sold as turquoise)
practical_notes.market_notes: Huge and diverse market. Carnelian, chrysoprase, onyx, and bloodstone are major trade categories. Dyed blue 'chalcedony' is pervasive. Some blue chalcedony from Namibia is among the most valuable.

prompt_fragments: waxy translucent microcrystalline silica
smooth ghost-white blue chalcedony surface
botryoidal cave-milk formations

chemical_profile.atomic_structure_notes: Chrysocolla has a disordered, poorly crystalline to amorphous structure. It is often described as a copper-bearing phyllosilicate mineraloid. The structure resembles allophane (amorphous aluminosilicate) with Cu replacing Al. Water content is variable and affects physical properties. Some authorities describe it as a mixture of copper hydroxide and amorphous silica.
chemical_profile.chemistry_notes: The formula is highly variable and debated; Cu content, Al content, and water vary significantly between specimens. The vivid cyan/blue-green color is entirely due to Cu2+. Pure chrysocolla without impurities is relatively soft and light; when intimately intergrown with quartz or chalcedony it forms the harder gem-silica material.

crystallography.unit_cell_notes: Not well defined; essentially amorphous to nanocrystalline
crystallography.crystallography_notes: Chrysocolla is essentially amorphous or at best nanocrystalline. It does not form macroscopic crystals. All occurrences are massive, botryoidal, earthy, or as crusts. X-ray diffraction shows only broad, diffuse peaks. It is best classified as a mineraloid.

physical_properties.physical_notes: Hardness and SG vary with water content and admixed silica. Gem-silica (chrysocolla in chalcedony) reaches Mohs 6.5–7 and SG ~2.58. Pure earthy chrysocolla is very soft (2–2.5) and light (SG ~1.9–2.0). Water loss on heating causes cracking and color change.

color_profiles.name: cyan-chrysocolla
color_profiles.cause: Cu2+ in the structure; high Cu content with low Fe.
color_profiles.conditions: Oxidized zones of copper deposits; intense cyan is most prized.

color_profiles.name: blue-green-chrysocolla
color_profiles.cause: Cu2+ with minor Fe or Mn shifting hue.
color_profiles.conditions: Common color across most occurrences.

color_profiles.name: gem-silica
color_profiles.cause: Cu2+ in chalcedony host; silicification increases hardness and translucency.
color_profiles.conditions: High-silica environments; Arizona USA (Superior, Globe-Miami), Philippines.

color_profiles.name: green-chrysocolla
color_profiles.cause: High Cu with slight oxidation state shift; mixing with malachite.
color_profiles.conditions: Often intergrown or mixed with malachite in copper oxidation zones.

common_forms: earthy blue-green coatings on copper ore
appearance.visual_notes: The vivid cyan to turquoise-blue color is unmistakable. Chrysocolla is often the most visually striking mineral in copper oxidation zones. It frequently occurs with malachite (green), azurite (deep blue), and cuprite (red), creating spectacular polychrome specimens.

optical_and_special_effects.fluorescence: Generally inert
optical_and_special_effects.optical_notes: RI approximately 1.46–1.57 (variable; amorphous). Gem-silica RI closer to chalcedony (~1.53–1.54). Isotropic to weakly anisotropic.

geologic_environments: oxidation zones of copper sulfide deposits
notable_localities: Bisbee and Globe-Miami, Arizona, USA (gem-silica and massive chrysocolla)
Shaba Province (Katanga), Democratic Republic of Congo
Broken Hill, New South Wales, Australia
formation_and_occurrence.occurrence_notes: Chrysocolla is one of the most characteristic secondary copper minerals, forming wherever copper sulfides are weathered in the presence of silica-rich waters. It forms relatively early in the oxidation sequence and may be replaced over time by more stable copper minerals. Gem-silica forms when silica-rich groundwater simultaneously deposits chalcedony and chrysocolla.

historical_uses: Chrysocolla was described by Theophrastus (~315 BCE) and named from Greek chrysos (gold) + kolla (glue) — it was used as a flux for soldering gold in antiquity
Egyptian blue pigment and some green-blue decorative materials in ancient contexts may involve chrysocolla
Cleopatra reportedly wore chrysocolla jewelry; Pliny the Elder describes it in Natural History
Used as a blue-green pigment in ancient Egypt, Rome, and South America
Timna Valley, Israel: one of the world's earliest copper mining sites (active from Chalcolithic through Roman periods); chrysocolla abundant
cultural_associations: Associated with copper-working cultures worldwide — wherever copper was mined, chrysocolla occurred as a vivid blue-green indicator mineral
cultural_and_historical.folklore_notes: The ancient Greek name means 'gold solder' — reflecting its actual historical use as a flux material in gold soldering. In modern crystal markets it is often conflated with turquoise due to color similarity.

interpretive_vibe.vibe_notes: Chrysocolla is the color of patinated copper — a vivid, slightly decayed cyan that evokes wet tropical ruins and ocean-floor chemistry. Its softness and mineraloid nature give it an impermanence that contrasts with its brilliant color.

practical_notes.toxicity: Moderate. Contains significant copper; avoid ingesting dust. Do not use in gem elixirs. Handle with normal hygiene (wash hands after handling).
practical_notes.handling_notes: Soft and brittle; handle carefully. Sensitive to dehydration — avoid prolonged heat or dry conditions. Some specimens crack or fade as water is lost.
practical_notes.care_notes: Avoid ultrasonic, steam, and prolonged water immersion (can cause dehydration cracking). Wipe with damp cloth only. Store away from heat and very dry environments.
treatments_or_synthetics: Wax or resin impregnation common for stabilization in jewelry use
Often sold mixed with or as 'gem-silica' when chalcedony-hosted
Dyed howlite or magnesite sometimes sold as chrysocolla
common_confusions: Turquoise (harder, phosphate not silicate, different streak)
Gem-silica is chrysocolla in chalcedony — more durable variety
practical_notes.market_notes: Massive chrysocolla is common and affordable. Gem-silica (chrysocolla chalcedony) is rare and commands high prices as a cabochon gem. Mixed chrysocolla-malachite-azurite specimens from Bisbee and Congo are popular collector pieces.

prompt_fragments: vivid cyan chrysocolla crust on copper ore
earthy blue-green mineraloid in oxidation zone
gem-silica translucent aqua cabochon
copper patina mineral decay blue

provenance.notes: Formula and structural description reflect ongoing debate in mineralogical literature; the amorphous/mineraloid classification is appropriate. Copper toxicity note is precautionary and standard.

chemical_profile.atomic_structure_notes: Trigonal quartz framework. Color from Fe3+ impurities in tetrahedral Al3+ sites — these create color centers absorbing in blue-violet range, transmitting yellow-orange. Natural citrine is uncommon; most commercial 'citrine' is heat-treated amethyst or smoky quartz (Fe4+ color centers destroyed/rearranged by heating).
chemical_profile.chemistry_notes: Natural citrine is rare — far rarer than amethyst. Most commercial citrine is produced by heating amethyst (destroys purple Fe4+ centers, leaving Fe3+ yellow) or smoky quartz. Natural citrine tends to be paler and less saturated than heat-treated material. The famous 'Madeira' deep orange-red citrine is effectively always heat-treated amethyst.

crystallography.twinning: Brazil-law twinning common
crystallography.crystallography_notes: Identical structure to all quartz. Prismatic hexagonal crystals. Natural citrine crystals indistinguishable in form from other quartz. Heat-treated material takes the form of whatever starting material (amethyst or smoky quartz) was used.

physical_properties.physical_notes: Standard quartz properties. Color stable to heat beyond the temperatures already used in treatment (commercial citrine has already been heated). UV exposure may gradually lighten color. Stable for normal jewelry use.

color_profiles.name: Pale Yellow Citrine
color_profiles.cause: Fe3+ at low concentration; may be natural.
color_profiles.conditions: Natural citrine from pegmatites and hydrothermal veins; also lightly treated.

color_profiles.name: Golden Citrine
color_profiles.cause: Fe3+ at moderate concentration; most heat-treated amethyst.
color_profiles.conditions: Most common commercial citrine; Brazilian heated material.

color_profiles.name: Madeira Citrine
color_profiles.cause: Elevated Fe3+ at high temperature; always heat-treated (amethyst originally).
color_profiles.conditions: Heat-treated Brazilian amethyst at higher temperatures — named for Madeira wine color.

color_profiles.name: Ametrine
color_profiles.cause: Natural zoning in crystal growth; amethyst zones (Fe4+) and citrine zones (Fe3+) coexist.
color_profiles.conditions: Natural: Anahi mine, Bolivia only. Heat-treated bicolor also produced.

macro_patterns: color zoning (faint in natural; uniform in heat-treated)
common_forms: faceted oval, cushion, round, fancy gems
appearance.visual_notes: Citrine spans from pale lemon-yellow to deep Madeira orange-cognac. The warm golden tones made it popular in Art Deco jewelry. Large clean specimens are readily available and affordable. Heat-treated material has uniform color; natural citrine often has patchy or pale color with visible phantoms.

optical_and_special_effects.fluorescence: None typically
optical_and_special_effects.optical_notes: Uniaxial positive. RI = 1.544–1.553. Standard quartz optics. Color stable to light but may fade slightly with prolonged UV in extreme cases.

genesis: produced from amethyst by natural geothermal heating
notable_localities: Rio Grande do Sul, Brazil (largest producer of heat-treated citrine from amethyst)
formation_and_occurrence.occurrence_notes: Natural citrine is genuinely rare — estimated less than 1% of the commercial market is natural (unheated). Most commercial citrine originates as Brazilian amethyst geode material that is heated. The heat treatment is permanent, stable, and widely accepted in the trade, though disclosure is technically required.

historical_uses: Used as a gem since antiquity (ancient Greek and Roman jewelry)
Art Deco jewelry (1920s-30s — large yellow stones fashionable)
Victorian jewelry
Called 'topaz' historically — much confusion between citrine and topaz in old records
cultural_associations: The 'merchant's stone' in modern crystal traditions
November birthstone
Citrine and topaz long confused — 'Spanish topaz' was often citrine from Brazil
Art Deco Hollywood: large yellow/orange gems in bold settings
cultural_and_historical.folklore_notes: Citrine has been confused with topaz throughout most of gem history — 'Spanish topaz,' 'Brazilian topaz,' and 'Madeira topaz' were all actually citrine. The confusion was only definitively resolved in the early 20th century. In contemporary crystal culture, citrine is heavily associated with prosperity and abundance — 'the merchant's stone.'

interpretive_vibe.vibe_notes: Citrine is bottled autumn sunlight — the yellow-to-amber warmth of October afternoons in crystalline quartz. Madeira citrine burns like cognac held up to candlelight. The Art Deco period knew what to do with it.

practical_notes.toxicity: Non-toxic.
practical_notes.handling_notes: Durable for jewelry. Color stable under normal conditions. Avoid prolonged direct UV in extreme cases.
practical_notes.care_notes: Clean with warm soapy water. Ultrasonic safe. Store away from extreme heat or prolonged sunlight if cautious.
treatments_or_synthetics: Heat treatment of amethyst or smoky quartz: ubiquitous in commercial market; permanent and stable
Synthetic citrine: quartz is commercially synthesized but sold as synthetic/lab
practical_notes.market_notes: Inexpensive and widely available. Large clean faceted stones at low per-carat prices. Madeira-grade (deep orange) most valued. Art Deco pieces featuring large citrine have collector market. November birthstone alternative to topaz.

prompt_fragments: Madeira citrine: cognac-amber hexagonal quartz — warm iron-glow
golden citrine faceted cushion — Art Deco sunshine in quartz
November amber warmth in SiO2 — heated amethyst becomes solar

provenance.notes: Citrine is a variety of quartz. mindat_id 1099 is the citrine variety listing. Most commercial citrine is heat-treated amethyst.

chemical_profile.atomic_structure_notes: FCC metallic structure isostructural with gold and silver.
