chemical_profile.chemistry_notes: Softest of the three noble native metals in practice. Forms natural alloys with silver. Excellent electrical and thermal conductor; third-highest electrical conductivity of any element after silver and gold.

crystallography.twinning: Spinel-law twins occasionally reported.
crystallography.crystallography_notes: Well-formed crystals uncommon; cubic and tetrahexahedral crystals known from some hydrothermal and supergene localities. Most commonly forms arborescent, dendritic, or wire masses.

physical_properties.physical_notes: Distinctive copper-red color when fresh, developing characteristic green-blue verdigris patina over time. Excellent electrical and thermal conductor.

color_profiles.name: Fresh Native Copper
color_profiles.cause: metallic copper surface — characteristic d-band optical absorption
color_profiles.conditions: freshly exposed, unoxidized

color_profiles.name: Oxidized / Tarnished
color_profiles.cause: formation of cuprite (Cu2O) or tenorite surface layer
color_profiles.conditions: mild oxidation

color_profiles.name: Patinated / Weathered
color_profiles.cause: formation of malachite, azurite, chrysocolla, or brochantite on surface
color_profiles.conditions: prolonged weathering with moisture, CO2, or sulfate

appearance.visual_notes: Unmistakable copper-red when fresh. Slow oxidation spectrum from red through brown and black to vivid blue-green patina is one of nature's most beautiful chemical sequences. Keweenaw Peninsula specimens show exceptional arborescent habits.

optical_and_special_effects.fluorescence: none

geologic_environments: oxidation zones of copper sulfide deposits
basaltic lava vesicles and amygdules (Lake Superior type)
notable_localities: Keweenaw Peninsula, Michigan, USA (world's finest arborescent specimens)
Broken Hill, New South Wales, Australia
formation_and_occurrence.occurrence_notes: Most common in the oxidation and secondary enrichment zones of copper sulfide deposits. The Lake Superior basaltic copper deposits are unique volcanogenic occurrences producing the world's finest dendritic and arborescent specimens.

historical_uses: first metal systematically worked by humans (Chalcolithic, ~9000 BCE)
bronze production (copper + tin alloy, ~3300 BCE)
coins (Roman aes, US penny historically copper)
electrical wiring and electronics
plumbing
roofing and architectural cladding (develops green patina)
cookware and utensils
cultural_associations: Venus/Aphrodite (copper associated with Cyprus — Latin 'cuprum' from 'Cyprium')
Chalcolithic civilization transition
Statue of Liberty (copper exterior with patina)
Aztec metalwork
Celtic decorative arts
Old Copper Complex (5000–1000 BCE, Lake Superior region)
cultural_and_historical.folklore_notes: The word 'copper' derives from Latin 'cuprum', itself from Greek 'Kuprios' meaning 'of Cyprus', where copper was extensively mined in antiquity. Copper was the first metal systematically worked by humans, defining the Chalcolithic period. The Lake Superior copper deposits in Michigan supported one of the world's earliest known mining cultures (Old Copper Complex, ~5000–1000 BCE).

aesthetic_roles: tonal contrast with blue-green secondary minerals
interpretive_vibe.vibe_notes: Copper is the first chapter of human metallurgy — warm, workable, transformative. Its slow oxidation into blues and greens is one of nature's most beautiful chemical reactions.

practical_notes.toxicity: metallic copper generally low toxicity; copper dust and fumes can cause respiratory irritation; copper compounds vary widely in toxicity; excessive oral intake can cause liver damage
practical_notes.handling_notes: Handle normally. Skin contact may leave temporary greenish discoloration. Avoid prolonged inhalation of copper dust when cutting or grinding specimens.
practical_notes.care_notes: Store dry. Patina (green-blue surface) is often desirable and protective; do not aggressively clean if patina is part of specimen aesthetics. Can clean with dilute acids if needed, then neutralize.
treatments_or_synthetics: copper electroforming used in art and manufacture
electrolytic copper is refined synthetic copper — not a specimen mineral
bronze and brass are copper alloys — distinct from native copper
common_confusions: cuprite (Cu2O — darker red, harder, streak red-brown)
bismuth (similar metallic luster but different color and streak)
practical_notes.market_notes: Industrial metal; commodity price globally traded. Collector specimens — especially arborescent/dendritic from Keweenaw Peninsula, Michigan and Corocoro, Bolivia — command significant premiums. Museum-quality Keweenaw specimens are among the most prized native metal mineral specimens.

prompt_fragments: copper-red arborescent mass with blue-green malachite bloom
wiry dendritic copper on basalt matrix
warm metallic red fading to verdigris teal

structural_units: AlO6 octahedra sharing faces and edges in a hexagonal close-packed oxygen framework
chemical_profile.atomic_structure_notes: Trigonal structure (rhombohedral); Al occupies 2/3 of octahedral sites in a hexagonal close-packed oxygen lattice.
chemical_profile.chemistry_notes: Pure corundum is colorless; trace elements produce gem varieties. Cr3+ gives ruby red; Fe2+/Ti4+ gives blue sapphire; other traces yield yellows, greens, purples, and pink.

crystallography.twinning: Contact and penetration twins on {0001} and {10-11} common.
crystallography.crystallography_notes: Crystals typically prismatic, tabular, or barrel-shaped with striated basal faces. Sometimes as steep bipyramids.

physical_properties.physical_notes: Second hardest natural mineral after diamond. Extreme hardness makes it highly abrasion-resistant; used industrially as emery abrasive.

color_profiles.name: colorless
color_profiles.cause: Absence of chromophore trace elements.
color_profiles.conditions: Pure Al2O3.

color_profiles.name: ruby
color_profiles.cause: Cr3+ substituting for Al3+; d-d electronic transitions.
color_profiles.conditions: Typically in marble-hosted or basaltic metamorphic environments.

color_profiles.name: blue-sapphire
color_profiles.cause: Intervalence charge transfer between Fe2+ and Ti4+ on adjacent octahedral sites.
color_profiles.conditions: Common; widely distributed.

color_profiles.name: yellow-sapphire
color_profiles.cause: Fe3+ or color centers.
color_profiles.conditions: Variety of sapphire.

color_profiles.name: padparadscha
color_profiles.cause: Combined Cr3+ and iron-related centers.
color_profiles.conditions: Rare; Sri Lanka most notable.

color_profiles.name: pink-sapphire
color_profiles.cause: Low Cr3+ concentrations; boundary with ruby is debated.

macro_patterns: silk (needle inclusions of rutile producing asterism)
appearance.visual_notes: Gem-quality crystals prized as ruby and sapphire. Non-gem material is gray-brown and often massive. Silk inclusions produce asterism in star rubies and sapphires.

optical_and_special_effects.fluorescence: Variable; rubies often fluoresce strong red under UV; blue sapphires typically inert.
optical_and_special_effects.chatoyancy: Present in star corundums when cut en cabochon.
optical_and_special_effects.asterism: Six-rayed stars in star ruby and star sapphire from intersecting rutile needle (silk) inclusions along {0001}.
optical_and_special_effects.pleochroism: Dichroic; ruby shows red/orange-red; blue sapphire shows blue/blue-green to violet-blue.
optical_and_special_effects.optical_notes: Uniaxial negative. RI approximately 1.760–1.778. Birefringence ~0.008.

notable_localities: Queensland and New South Wales, Australia (sapphire)
formation_and_occurrence.occurrence_notes: Gem-quality corundum is scarce relative to industrial corundum. Ruby and sapphire localities often placer-based. Emery (massive corundum + magnetite + spinel) used as abrasive.

historical_uses: Abrasive (emery) for grinding and polishing
Gemstones; ruby and sapphire used in jewelry for millennia
Watch bearings and precision instruments (synthetic corundum)
Laser medium (ruby laser, first working laser 1960)
cultural_associations: Ruby: symbol of passion, royalty, and protection in South and Southeast Asian cultures
Sapphire: symbol of wisdom, fidelity, and heaven in Western traditions
cultural_and_historical.folklore_notes: Rubies were called 'ratnaraj' (king of gems) in Sanskrit texts. Sapphire was associated with divine favor in medieval European Christianity.

interpretive_vibe.vibe_notes: Corundum occupies the extreme end of mineral hardness and gem value. Ruby and sapphire represent fire and sky — the two poles of corundum's chromatic identity.

practical_notes.toxicity: low
practical_notes.handling_notes: Very hard; will scratch most surfaces and other gemstones. Durable in almost all conditions.
practical_notes.care_notes: Stable to heat, acids, and weathering. Safe for ultrasonic cleaning. Avoid thermally shocking high-clarity stones.
treatments_or_synthetics: Heat treatment: extremely common for ruby and sapphire to improve color and clarity
Beryllium diffusion: used in some sapphires to produce yellow or orange
Lead glass filling: occasionally used in low-quality rubies
Synthetic corundum: grown by Verneuil (flame fusion) and Czochralski methods; widely used in industry and jewelry
common_confusions: Ruby confused with: red spinel, garnet (pyrope, almandine), red tourmaline
Sapphire confused with: aquamarine, blue topaz, tanzanite, iolite, synthetic sapphire
practical_notes.market_notes: Fine ruby and sapphire among the most valuable gemstones per carat. Kashmir sapphire and Burmese pigeon-blood ruby command extreme premiums. Treatment disclosure critical. Industrial corundum (emery) is commodity.

prompt_fragments: barrel-shaped trigonal corundum crystals
chromium fire of ruby red
midnight blue of sapphire iron-titanium charge transfer
six-rayed asterism from silk inclusions

provenance.notes: Ruby and sapphire will have separate variety files. Color mechanism data based on established mineralogical literature.

structural_units: sp3-hybridized carbon in tetrahedral framework — each C bonded to 4 C neighbors
chemical_profile.atomic_structure_notes: All carbon atoms tetrahedrally bonded in a rigid 3D covalent network (diamond cubic structure). No delocalized electrons — hence transparency and electrical insulation (contrast with graphite).
chemical_profile.chemistry_notes: Nitrogen is most common impurity — creates yellow color (Type Ia/Ib). Boron impurity creates blue color (Type IIb). Type IIa diamonds are near-pure carbon with exceptional transparency. Brown color from plastic deformation and vacancy clusters.

crystallography.twinning: Macles (spinel-law twins producing flat triangular crystals) common in alluvial diamonds.
crystallography.crystallography_notes: Octahedral habit most common. Cubic and dodecahedral forms also occur. Alluvial stones typically rounded and frosted. Trigon etch pits on octahedral faces are diagnostic. Perfect octahedral cleavage in four directions is a critical property despite extreme hardness.

physical_properties.streak: none (too hard to leave a streak)
physical_properties.physical_notes: Hardest known natural mineral (Mohs 10). Highest thermal conductivity of any natural material. Despite extreme hardness, has perfect octahedral cleavage — can be cleaved or broken with a single skilled blow. High refractive index and dispersion produce characteristic 'fire' (spectral color flashes).

color_profiles.name: Colorless (D–F on GIA scale)
color_profiles.cause: structural purity; absence of nitrogen and other chromophores
color_profiles.conditions: Type IIa — nitrogen-free or negligible

color_profiles.name: Cape Yellow / Fancy Yellow
color_profiles.cause: nitrogen impurities substituting for carbon (Type Ia / Type Ib)
color_profiles.conditions: N substitution in crystal lattice; Cape series refers to historical South African yellows

color_profiles.name: Brown Diamond
color_profiles.cause: plastic deformation and vacancy clusters along crystal planes
color_profiles.conditions: most common natural fancy color; now marketed as 'chocolate' or 'cognac'

color_profiles.name: Blue Diamond
color_profiles.cause: boron impurities (Type IIb)
color_profiles.conditions: extremely rare; semiconducting electrical behavior

color_profiles.name: Pink / Red Diamond
color_profiles.cause: structural defects / plastic deformation (precise mechanism still debated)
color_profiles.conditions: Argyle Mine, Australia was historically dominant source; now closed
color_profiles.variety_name: Argyle Pink, Fancy Pink, Red Diamond

color_profiles.name: Green Diamond
color_profiles.cause: natural radiation exposure (alpha particle irradiation of crystal lattice)
color_profiles.conditions: typically affects surface layer only; cutting often removes color

color_profiles.name: Black Diamond (Carbonado)
color_profiles.cause: polycrystalline aggregate with inclusions of graphite, amorphous carbon, and sulfides
color_profiles.conditions: distinct from single-crystal diamond; possibly extraterrestrial origin debated

textures: smooth adamantine luster on fresh crystal faces
frosted or etched on alluvial grains
trigon etch pits on octahedral faces
appearance.visual_notes: Unmistakable adamantine luster and exceptional brilliance in cut stones. Rough diamonds often appear greasy or frosted. Dispersion produces fire — flashes of spectral color — unmatched by any other colorless gemstone except moissanite.

optical_and_special_effects.fluorescence: blue fluorescence common (~25–35% of gem diamonds) under shortwave UV; some fluoresce yellow, orange, or green; many inert
optical_and_special_effects.phosphorescence: some diamonds phosphoresce after UV exposure
optical_and_special_effects.optical_notes: Highest refractive index of any colorless natural gemstone (~2.417). Very high dispersion (0.044) produces fire. Totally internally reflective — basis of brilliant cut design. Thermally cold to touch due to extreme thermal conductivity.

genesis: mantle origin (150–200+ km depth, extreme P-T conditions)
transported to surface via kimberlite or lamproite pipe eruptions
notable_localities: Kimberley, Northern Cape, South Africa (kimberlite type locality)
Cullinan Mine, South Africa (world's largest gem diamond, 3106 ct)
Argyle Mine, Western Australia (world's finest pink diamonds — now closed)
Jwaneng and Orapa, Botswana (highest volume producers)
Mirny and Jubilee pipes, Yakutia, Russia
Golconda, India (historic — source of Hope, Koh-i-Noor, Regent diamonds)
Crater of Diamonds State Park, Arkansas, USA (only public diamond mine)
formation_and_occurrence.occurrence_notes: Diamonds form in the mantle at depths >150 km and are transported to the surface in kimberlite and lamproite eruptions. Secondary placer deposits form downcurrent of eroded primary pipes. Superdeep diamonds from >660 km provide unique windows into the deep Earth.

historical_uses: gemstone in jewelry (ancient India through present)
cutting and grinding tools (diamond abrasives, saws, drills)
industrial abrasive (grinding wheels, polishing)
windows and optics for high-pressure experimental cells
heat spreaders in electronics
scalpel blades (medical)
diamond anvil cells (high-pressure research)
cultural_associations: engagement ring tradition (De Beers 'A Diamond is Forever' campaign, 1947)
ultimate luxury gemstone globally
Hope Diamond (Smithsonian Institution)
Koh-i-Noor (British Crown Jewels — contested provenance)
Cullinan Diamond fragments in British Crown Jewels
blood and conflict diamonds (ethical sourcing concerns)
Golconda diamonds (Indian sultanic tradition)
cultural_and_historical.folklore_notes: Diamond's name comes from Greek 'adamas' (unconquerable). Ancient Indians recognized diamond's hardness millennia before European contact. Hindu mythology associates vajra (diamond thunderbolt) with Indra's weapon. Medieval Europeans believed diamonds were tears of gods or splinters of stars. The De Beers 'A Diamond is Forever' (1947) is considered the most successful advertising slogan in history — it created the modern engagement ring tradition in Western culture. Lab-grown diamonds (chemically identical to natural) have disrupted the market since the 2010s.

interpretive_vibe.vibe_notes: Diamond is crystallographic perfection distilled from the Earth's mantle. It holds light like a prison — refracting, dispersing, returning it transformed. The hardest thing on Earth, yet cleavable with a single skilled blow.

practical_notes.toxicity: non-toxic in mineral and gemstone form; diamond dust (very fine particles) is a serious inhalation hazard; not a concern for handled specimens or jewelry
practical_notes.handling_notes: Extremely hard but brittle — avoid impacts and dropping on hard surfaces (perfect octahedral cleavage can cause chips at girdle of faceted stones). Use padded surfaces for gemological handling.
practical_notes.care_notes: Clean jewelry with warm soapy water and soft brush; ultrasonic cleaners generally safe for pure diamonds (avoid if inclusions are present). Store separate from other gemstones — diamond will scratch all other gem materials.
treatments_or_synthetics: HPHT color enhancement treatment of natural yellow-brown diamonds to colorless or fancy colors
laser drilling and fracture filling of natural inclusions
irradiation and annealing for color enhancement
CVD (Chemical Vapor Deposition) lab-grown diamond — chemically identical, requires specialized testing to distinguish
HPHT lab-grown diamond — also chemically identical to natural
diamond simulants (moissanite, cubic zirconia, white sapphire) are NOT diamond
common_confusions: cubic zirconia (zirconium oxide — softer, heavier, no cleavage)
moissanite (silicon carbide — double refraction, higher dispersion; now synthetic)
lab-grown diamond (chemically identical — requires spectroscopic testing)
practical_notes.market_notes: Most valuable gemstone market globally. Graded by 4Cs: cut, color, clarity, carat. Colorless D–F / IF–VS1 most prized traditionally. Fancy colored diamonds — especially blue, red, and pink — reach highest per-carat auction prices. Lab-grown diamonds have caused major price disruption in melee and lower-grade natural markets since 2018. Kimberley Process certification addresses conflict diamond concerns.

polymorphs: lonsdaleite (hexagonal diamond — rare, impact and meteoritic)

kimberlite blue ground yielding crystallographic perfection

chemical_profile.atomic_structure_notes: Cyclosilicate (ring silicate). Cr3+ and/or V3+ substitute for Al3+ in octahedral sites, creating the vivid green color. The channel cavities in the beryl structure may contain H2O, CO2, and Na. High Cr content (>0.1%) or V content defines emerald from green beryl.
chemical_profile.chemistry_notes: Chromium is the primary chromophore in Colombian emeralds; vanadium dominates in some Brazilian (Salininha) and some African sources. The GIA separates emerald (Cr or V dominantly colored green beryl) from 'green beryl' (Fe-colored green beryl). Colombian emeralds typically have three-phase inclusions (fluid+gas+solid crystal) in characteristic fingerprint patterns — the 'jardin.'

crystallography.twinning: Rare
crystallography.crystallography_notes: Well-developed hexagonal prisms, typically with flat basal terminations. Prismatic faces often striated parallel to c-axis. Colombian emerald crystals from black shale-hosted hydrothermal veins often hexagonal and doubly terminated. Inclusions ('jardin') are characteristic and numerous — fine emerald is described as a garden (jardin) within.

physical_properties.physical_notes: Lower SG than most colored gemstones due to light beryllium composition. High inclusion density ('jardin') makes emerald the most included major gemstone — often more brittle in practice than hardness suggests. Colombian emerald jardin is characteristic and expected; overly clean emerald raises treatment concerns.

color_profiles.name: Colombian Emerald
color_profiles.cause: Cr3+ dominant chromophore; some V3+. Low Fe keeps color pure without brownish or yellowish cast.
color_profiles.conditions: Hydrothermal veins in black shale (Cretaceous flysch), Boyacá, Colombia.

color_profiles.name: Zambian Emerald
color_profiles.cause: Cr3+ + some Fe2+; slightly deeper/darker than Colombian.
color_profiles.conditions: Mica schist, Kafubu area, Zambia.

color_profiles.name: Brazilian Emerald
color_profiles.cause: V3+ dominant in some deposits; Fe adds yellowish-green component.
color_profiles.conditions: Pegmatitic and hydrothermal, Minas Gerais; various types.

color_profiles.name: Zimbabwe Emerald
color_profiles.cause: High Cr3+ in very small stones from mica schist.
color_profiles.conditions: Sandawana, Zimbabwe — noted for small but intensely colored stones.

appearance.visual_notes: Emerald's vivid green is the benchmark for lush saturated green in the gem world. The jardin (inclusion garden) is not a flaw in fine emerald — it is expected, accepted, and sometimes considered a signature of natural origin. The 'emerald cut' (rectangular step cut) was designed to showcase emerald's color while minimizing mechanical stress on the included stone.

optical_and_special_effects.fluorescence: Weak to moderate red fluorescence under LW UV (Cr3+); varies by origin — Colombian often weakly red, synthetic usually stronger red, Fe-rich specimens often inert
optical_and_special_effects.optical_notes: Uniaxial negative. RI approximately 1.565–1.602. Birefringence ~0.005–0.009. Characteristic three-phase inclusions (fluid cavities with bubble and daughter crystals) in Colombian material are diagnostic of natural origin. UV fluorescence distinguishes natural from synthetic.

geologic_environments: Cr-bearing mica schists and phlogopite-bearing rocks (metamorphic type)
Li-Be rich granitic pegmatites with Cr-bearing host
notable_localities: Muzo, Boyacá, Colombia (world's finest — Colombian type)
Kafubu / Musakashi, Copperbelt, Zambia (second largest production)
Itabira (Nova Era) and Salininha, Minas Gerais, Brazil
Sikait, Eastern Desert, Egypt (Cleopatra's Mines — one of world's oldest gem mines)
formation_and_occurrence.occurrence_notes: Emerald requires a very unusual geological coincidence: beryllium-rich granitic fluids or magmas must interact with chromium or vanadium-bearing rocks (black shale, peridotite, or ultramafic lithologies). This convergence is geologically rare, explaining emerald's scarcity relative to other beryls. Colombia's unique black-shale/carbonate hydrothermal system produces the finest material.

historical_uses: Gemstone — Egyptian mining at Sikait since ~330 BCE ('Cleopatra's Mines')
Aztec and Inca sacred stone — 'esmeralda' — Spanish conquest looted enormous quantities
Mughal engraved emeralds (verses from Quran on large crystals)
Roman, Byzantine, and medieval European jewelry
Oil lamp lanterns in Egyptian temples
Magnification lenses (Nero reportedly viewed gladiatorial combat through an emerald lens)
cultural_associations: Associated with Venus, fertility, and rebirth in Greco-Roman tradition
Inca: sacred to Pachacamac, offered to gods
Mughal emperors used large emeralds as talismans engraved with sacred texts
Duchess of Devonshire's emerald parure
The Tropic of Cancer passes through Colombia — emerald country
cultural_and_historical.folklore_notes: Cleopatra's Mines in Egypt's Eastern Desert were the world's primary emerald source for Roman civilization. The Aztec god Quetzalcoatl was said to reside in emeralds; large emeralds were among the most sacred objects in pre-Columbian Mesoamerica — the Spanish conquest of Peru looted crystals the size of ostrich eggs. Mughal emperors had large emeralds engraved with verses from the Quran as talismans — these 'Mughal emeralds' are among the most prized objects in Islamic art history. Pliny the Elder in Historia Naturalis said that no other green is as intense as that of emerald.

interpretive_vibe.vibe_notes: Emerald is living green crystallized — the color of growth, deep jungle, and spring. Its jardin is not imperfection but biography — three-phase Colombian inclusions tell the story of the hydrothermal fluid that made it. The finest stones have a depth that no synthetic can replicate.

practical_notes.toxicity: Beryllium-bearing; dust from grinding is hazardous (berylliosis risk). Finished gems and specimens safe to handle.
practical_notes.handling_notes: Relatively brittle due to inclusions and fractures. Avoid impact, especially on edges. The emerald cut was specifically designed to reduce mechanical stress on the stone.
practical_notes.care_notes: Avoid ultrasonic and steam cleaners — both can damage oil/resin-filled fractures and may cause stone fracture. Clean with warm water and soft brush only. Oil treatment is routine; never soak in water for extended periods or use harsh cleaners.
treatments_or_synthetics: Oiling / resin filling: industry standard F1-F3 scale (GIA); must be disclosed; all commercial emerald is assumed treated unless certified otherwise
Synthetic emerald (flux and hydrothermal): Chatham, Gilson, Biron brands; distinguished by inclusions under magnification
Triplet simulants: quartz or beryl top/bottom + green glass core — common in historical jewelry
common_confusions: Green tourmaline (tsavorite garnet if red-included green; tourmaline biaxial)
Tsavorite garnet (no inclusions typical; isometric; different RI)
Green beryl (Fe-colored, not Cr/V — less vivid; different classification)
Synthetic emerald (requires inclusion and spectroscopic analysis)
practical_notes.market_notes: Emerald is the most valuable colored transparent gemstone per carat at top quality, often exceeding sapphire and rivaling ruby. Colombian origin commands premium. Treatment disclosure essential and affects value significantly. Unheated, unoiled Colombian fine-quality emerald is extremely rare. Synthetic widely available and openly sold.

prompt_fragments: Colombian emerald hexagonal prism — vivid chromium green with jardin inclusions
Muzo mine: hydrothermal green from black shale, spring-vivid Cr3+
Cleopatra's mine: 2000 years of green from Egypt's Eastern Desert

structural_units: Amorphous or paracrystalline SiO2 spheres with water
chemical_profile.atomic_structure_notes: Amorphous hydrated silica identical to other opal varieties. Water content typically lower than precious opal — 3–10%. Formed in volcanic (rhyolitic) host rock rather than sedimentary environments.
chemical_profile.chemistry_notes: Orange-red-yellow body color caused by iron oxide inclusions (hematite/goethite) and possibly organic compounds within the silica matrix. Some fire opals (precious fire opals) also show play-of-color in addition to the vivid body color.

crystallography.crystallography_notes: Amorphous like all opal. Occurs as nodular masses, veins, and botryoidal forms within rhyolite cavities and veins. Sometimes crystal-clear and transparent (facetable).

physical_properties.physical_notes: Often more transparent than Australian precious opal due to lower water content and volcanic formation. Can be faceted as a transparent gem when clear. More stable than hydrophane (Ethiopian) opal due to lower water content in some specimens.

color_profiles.name: Classic Fire Opal (Orange)
color_profiles.cause: Iron oxide inclusions (hematite/goethite) and structural/organic chromophores in amorphous silica.
color_profiles.conditions: Querétaro and other Mexican rhyolitic deposits.

color_profiles.name: Yellow Fire Opal
color_profiles.cause: Lower iron oxide content; other trace chromophores.
color_profiles.conditions: Less common than orange variety.

color_profiles.name: Red Fire Opal
color_profiles.cause: Higher iron oxide concentration.
color_profiles.conditions: Rarest and most prized fire opal color.

color_profiles.name: Precious Fire Opal
color_profiles.cause: Combination of iron-oxide chromophore AND ordered silica sphere diffraction.
color_profiles.conditions: Rare; found when sphere-size uniformity is achieved in volcanic opal.

appearance.visual_notes: Fire opal's vivid orange-to-red body color makes it the only facetable opal variety (when transparent). The color evokes live flame, molten metal, and sunset. In Mexico it is called 'piedra de fuego' (stone of fire) and was sacred to the Aztecs as a symbol of Huitzilopochtli.

optical_and_special_effects.fluorescence: Some specimens fluoresce green or yellow-green under LW UV
optical_and_special_effects.iridescence: Present in precious fire opal; absent in common fire opal
optical_and_special_effects.optical_notes: RI approximately 1.37–1.47. Isotropic. Transparent gem-quality material faceted as brilliant or cushion cuts. Fire opal without play-of-color is valued purely for body color intensity.

geologic_environments: rhyolite and ignimbrite cavities and veins
late-stage silica precipitation in cooling volcanic flows
notable_localities: Querétaro, Mexico (world's finest and most famous fire opal)
formation_and_occurrence.occurrence_notes: Fire opal is almost exclusively volcanic in origin, forming in rhyolitic volcanic rocks. Mexico accounts for the vast majority of production; Querétaro is the world's most famous fire opal locality. The iron-rich volcanic environment provides the chromophores that give fire opal its distinctive body color.

birthstone_status: October birthstone (as a variety of opal)
historical_uses: Sacred stone of the Aztec civilization — 'quetzalitzlipyollitli' (stone of fire hummingbird)
Used in Aztec ritual objects and jewelry
Pre-Columbian Mesoamerican lapidary work
Modern gem and jewelry use — faceted gems
cultural_associations: Aztec: associated with Huitzilopochtli (god of sun, war, and fire) and the hummingbird
Aztec name translates roughly to 'stone of the fire bird'
Mexican national gem identity — especially Querétaro fire opal
European explorers brought fire opal to Europe as exotic New World curiosities
cultural_and_historical.folklore_notes: Aztec civilization held fire opal sacred — the 'stone of the fire bird' associated with the hummingbird avatar of Huitzilopochtli. The Spanish conquest brought fire opal to Europe, where it was considered an exotic curiosity but never achieved the commercial success of the later Australian market. Mexico's fire opal tradition continues today — Querétaro produces the finest material and it is embedded in Mexican cultural identity.

interpretive_vibe.vibe_notes: Fire opal is concentrated solar energy in amorphous silica — orange-red volcanic glass that holds the color of fire without being consumed by it. It has none of the spectral mystery of black opal; instead it confronts you with pure warm chromatic intensity.

practical_notes.toxicity: Non-toxic.
practical_notes.handling_notes: More stable than Australian hydrophane opal due to lower water content, but still sensitive to dehydration. Avoid heat, prolonged direct sunlight, and dry conditions. Handle transparent faceted gems normally but avoid hard impacts.
practical_notes.care_notes: Clean with damp soft cloth. Avoid ultrasonic cleaners and steam. Avoid extreme heat and dryness. Store in consistent humidity.
treatments_or_synthetics: Sugar-acid treatment: darkening background of common fire opal (rare)
Synthetic opal: fire opal simulants in glass and synthetic resin
Imitation: amber, orange glass, carnelian sometimes confused
common_confusions: Orange glass (heavy; bubble inclusions; higher RI)
practical_notes.market_notes: Transparent faceted fire opal is distinctly priced by clarity, color saturation, and whether play-of-color is present. Red fire opal commands premiums. Mexican (Querétaro) material is most valued. Play-of-color fire opals are very rare and exceptionally valuable. Cabochon material widely available at moderate prices.

chemical_profile.atomic_structure_notes: FCC metallic structure with Au atoms at cube corners and face centers.
chemical_profile.chemistry_notes: Naturally forms alloys with silver (electrum) and copper. High purity specimens have SG near 19.3; silver-rich electrum may be below 16.

crystallography.twinning: Spinel-law twins occasionally reported; generally rare.
crystallography.crystallography_notes: Crystals rare; usually forms dendritic, arborescent, or wire habits. Well-formed octahedra occasionally found in hydrothermal veins.

physical_properties.physical_notes: Extremely malleable and ductile — can be beaten into leaf thinner than 100 nm. Does not tarnish or corrode under normal conditions. Highest malleability of any metal.

appearance.visual_notes: Unmistakable warm golden-yellow in fresh specimens. Arborescent and dendritic forms are among the most prized mineral specimens globally.

optical_and_special_effects.fluorescence: none

formation_and_occurrence.occurrence_notes: Found on every continent. Primary deposits in Precambrian greenstone belts and quartz veins; secondary (placer) deposits in alluvial gravels downcurrent of primary deposits.

historical_uses: currency and coinage (earliest coins ~600 BCE, Lydia)
jewelry and adornment (prehistoric to present)
Egyptian funerary objects (death masks, sarcophagi)
Byzantine icon art (gold leaf backgrounds)
electronics and technology (modern)
dentistry

divine perfection and immortality in alchemy
wealth and power universally

El Dorado myth
cultural_and_historical.folklore_notes: Gold has been considered sacred across virtually every ancient civilization. The alchemical pursuit of turning base metals into gold (chrysopoeia) dominated medieval science. Ancient Egyptians considered gold the flesh of the gods. In Hindu tradition, gold is associated with Lakshmi and prosperity. The California Gold Rush (1848–1855) and Klondike Gold Rush (1896–1899) shaped modern North America.

interpretive_vibe.vibe_notes: Gold carries the weight of ten thousand years of human desire. It is simultaneously primordial and modern, terrestrial and cosmic.

practical_notes.toxicity: non-toxic in metallic form; gold salts (used in medicine) can be toxic in excess; generally considered biocompatible
practical_notes.handling_notes: Soft and malleable — specimens should not be stored with harder minerals. Avoid dropping.
practical_notes.care_notes: Clean with warm water and mild soap. Does not tarnish. Store separately from harder minerals.

gold-filled and gold-vermeil jewelry distinct from solid gold

chalcopyrite (brassy yellow, harder, often iridescent tarnish)

structural_units: sp2-hybridized carbon sheets (graphene layers) stacked in ABAB sequence
chemical_profile.atomic_structure_notes: Layered hexagonal structure with strong covalent bonding within sheets and weak van der Waals forces between sheets. Delocalized pi electrons within layers account for metallic luster and electrical conductivity.
chemical_profile.chemistry_notes: Carbon polymorph stable at surface conditions. Chemically identical to diamond but structurally completely different. Impurities (Fe, Si, Al) common in natural specimens. Layer structure gives extreme anisotropy in physical properties.

crystallography.crystallography_notes: Typically forms foliated, platy, or scaly aggregates rather than well-terminated crystals. Large hexagonal plates known from Sri Lanka and Madagascar. Perfect basal cleavage is the dominant structural expression.

physical_properties.physical_notes: Extremely soft and greasy feel. Perfect basal cleavage produces thin flexible flakes. Conducts electricity due to delocalized electrons in graphene planes. Excellent solid lubricant. Writes on paper — pencil 'lead' is graphite, not lead metal.

color_profiles.name: Crystalline Graphite
color_profiles.cause: carbon layers with metallic-character electron delocalization
color_profiles.conditions: well-crystallized platy habit

color_profiles.name: Massive / Amorphous Graphite
color_profiles.cause: fine-grained or poorly crystallized carbon aggregate
color_profiles.conditions: microcrystalline or amorphous habit

macro_patterns: massive dark streaks in host rock
common_forms: scaly disseminations in marble or schist
appearance.visual_notes: Unmistakable greasy feel and extremely dark matte-black color. Marks paper and skin readily. Steel-gray metallic luster on fresh cleavage faces. Often found as disseminated flakes or foliated masses in metamorphic rocks.

optical_and_special_effects.fluorescence: none
optical_and_special_effects.optical_notes: Opaque. Metallic reflectance in reflected light microscopy.

geologic_environments: high-grade regional metamorphic terranes (granulite, amphibolite facies)
notable_localities: Borrowdale, Cumbria, England (discovery of pencil use; world's only massive graphite deposit historically)
Tioga, Sri Lanka (finest large crystalline plates)
formation_and_occurrence.occurrence_notes: Forms by metamorphic recrystallization of organic carbon in sedimentary protoliths. The Borrowdale deposit was exceptional in producing massive pure graphite directly workable as a writing material — all other significant deposits are disseminated or foliated.

historical_uses: pencils ('lead pencils' — graphite in clay matrix, since 16th century)
dry lubricant (machinery, locks)
electrodes in batteries and electrolysis
crucibles for high-temperature metalwork
nuclear reactors (neutron moderator)
carbon brushes in electric motors
brake linings and clutch pads
paint and protective coatings
ancestor material for graphene research
cultural_associations: Borrowdale discovery (1560s) transformed writing technology
pencil as tool of literacy and art
industrial revolution lubricant
nuclear age moderator
cultural_and_historical.folklore_notes: Graphite was initially called 'plumbago' (black lead) when discovered in Borrowdale in the 1560s — mistaken for a form of lead. The Borrowdale deposit was so pure and massive it could be sawn into sticks directly for writing, and so valuable that miners were searched leaving the mines. Graphite and diamond are chemically identical (both pure carbon) but could not be more different in structure, hardness, and appearance.

interpretive_vibe.vibe_notes: Graphite is diamond's humble alter ego — same element, opposite outcome. Where diamond captures and refracts, graphite absorbs and slides. It is carbon in its most pragmatic, terrestrial form.

practical_notes.toxicity: generally non-toxic; inhalation of graphite dust in industrial settings can cause graphitosis (pneumoconiosis); not hazardous as a handled specimen; fine graphite powder is combustible in bulk
practical_notes.handling_notes: Leaves black marks on hands and surfaces easily. Greasy texture is diagnostic. Avoid over-handling crystalline specimens as soft flakes detach readily.
practical_notes.care_notes: Store in sealed container to prevent graphite dust transfer and staining. Clean with soft dry brush. Avoid moisture in storage.
treatments_or_synthetics: synthetic graphite produced by Acheson process (high-temperature graphitization of petroleum coke)
graphene (single atomic layer of graphite) is a derived industrial and research material
artificial graphite electrodes widely used in industry and batteries
common_confusions: molybdenite (similar habit, color, and feel — but heavier SG ~4.7, and greenish streak)
stibnite (silvery metallic, higher luster, different habit)
coal (not a mineral species — no crystal structure)
practical_notes.market_notes: Industrial graphite is a significant commodity (battery, steel, lubricant industries). Natural crystalline specimen graphite from Madagascar and Sri Lanka collected by mineral enthusiasts. Prices modest relative to gemstone minerals.

polymorphs: lonsdaleite (hexagonal diamond — rare, meteoritic and impact)

chemical_profile.atomic_structure_notes: Isostructural with corundum (Al2O3). Fe3+ occupies 2/3 of octahedral sites in a hexagonal close-packed oxygen framework.
chemical_profile.chemistry_notes: Pure ferric iron oxide. The red-brown color of soils and many rocks worldwide reflects trace hematite. Used as pigment (ochre) since prehistoric times.

crystallography.twinning: Penetration twins on {0001} giving iron roses; contact twins possible.
crystallography.crystallography_notes: Well-formed crystals tabular to platy. Specular variety forms bright mirror-like plates. Botryoidal forms (kidney ore) show no visible crystallinity at hand-specimen scale.

physical_properties.physical_notes: Red-brown streak is highly diagnostic even when specimen appears black and metallic. Specular hematite is mirror-bright. Earthy red varieties (ochre) are soft-feeling but still test at 5.5–6.5 on Mohs scale when compact.

color_profiles.name: earthy-red

color_profiles.name: iron-rose

appearance.visual_notes: One of the most visually variable oxides. Specular hematite is mirror-silver; earthy hematite is brick-red. Red streak unites all forms. Iron roses are among the most dramatic mineral specimens.

optical_and_special_effects.fluorescence: None.
optical_and_special_effects.iridescence: Iridescent tarnish colors sometimes visible on specular plates.
optical_and_special_effects.optical_notes: Opaque. Strongly anisotropic in reflected light under the microscope (ore microscopy). Uniaxial negative when transparent in very thin section.

notable_localities: Minas Gerais, Brazil (large iron ore deposits; iron roses)
Lake Superior region, USA and Canada (BIF iron ore)
Pilbara region, Western Australia (world's largest iron ore)
formation_and_occurrence.occurrence_notes: One of the most abundant iron minerals in Earth's crust. Major iron ore mineral. Red soils worldwide owe their color to fine-grained hematite.

historical_uses: Red ochre pigment; used by humans for at least 300,000 years (oldest known pigment)
Cave paintings (Lascaux, Altamira)
Body and ritual paint in many ancient cultures
Iron ore; primary feedstock for steel production
Polishing stone (bloodstone, rouge)
Jewelry and beads
cultural_associations: Ancient Egypt: 'blood of Isis'; protective amulets
Roman gladiators wore hematite for protection
Indigenous cultures: red ochre in ceremony and burial
cultural_and_historical.folklore_notes: Name from Greek 'haima' (blood) for the red streak and powder. Ancient amulets believed to stop bleeding. Ochre use predates all other known pigments.

interpretive_vibe.vibe_notes: Hematite bridges the prehistoric and the industrial — the first pigment and a global iron ore. Red streak as diagnostic clue is an unforgettable mineralogical moment.

practical_notes.toxicity: low
practical_notes.handling_notes: Dense and brittle. Powdery ochre forms may stain hands and surfaces red-brown. Specular plates can have sharp edges.
practical_notes.care_notes: Very stable; resistant to acids compared to carbonates. No special handling needed.
treatments_or_synthetics: Synthetic hematite produced commercially
'Hematite' jewelry beads are often synthetic or compressed powder
Magnetic hematite is actually synthetic magnetite or ferrite, not true hematite
common_confusions: Magnetite (black, strongly magnetic; hematite is not or only weakly magnetic)
Ilmenite (FeTiO3; similar appearance but different streak)
practical_notes.market_notes: Iron-rose specimens from Elba and Brazil command high prices as collectors pieces. 'Magnetic hematite' sold in the gem trade is almost always synthetic. Bulk hematite is major industrial commodity.

structural_units: SiO4 tetrahedra linked into single chains (inosilicate)

chemical_profile.atomic_structure_notes: Single-chain inosilicate (pyroxene). Chains of corner-sharing SiO4 tetrahedra repeat every two tetrahedra (two-tetrahedra repeat unit Si2O6). Al occupies the smaller M1 octahedral site; Na occupies the larger M2 site. The high-pressure stability of jadeite relative to its low-pressure equivalent (albite + nepheline) reflects the dense packing of the pyroxene structure.
chemical_profile.chemistry_notes: Jadeite is the Na-Al end member of the clinopyroxene solid solution series. It grades toward omphacite (with Ca-Mg aegirine substitution) and augite. Chromium substituting for Al3+ in the M1 octahedral site produces the vivid emerald-green of Imperial jade. Fe3+ (aegirine component) produces black. Mn produces lavender in some varieties.

crystallography.twinning: Simple and polysynthetic twinning on {100} and {001} common
crystallography.crystallography_notes: Although jadeite is monoclinic and forms distinct prismatic crystals in laboratory samples, natural jade as used in carving is almost always a fine-grained interlocking mosaic of jadeite crystals — a rock texture that is responsible for its extraordinary toughness. Prismatic crystals with two cleavage planes meeting at ~87° and ~93° (characteristic pyroxene cleavage angle) are occasionally found.

cleavage: good prismatic {110} at ~87° and ~93° (two directions, pyroxene cleavage)
physical_properties.tenacity: tough (fine-grained interlocking crystals provide exceptional toughness)
physical_properties.physical_notes: Jadeite has one of the highest SG values among commercially important jade-type materials (~3.24–3.43), distinguishing it from nephrite (~2.90–3.03) and most simulants. Its toughness in massive form is due to interlocking crystal texture, not high hardness alone. The waxy to vitreous luster on polished surfaces is immediately distinctive.

color_profiles.name: imperial-jade
color_profiles.cause: Cr3+ substituting for Al3+ in the M1 octahedral site, producing a chromium absorption spectrum analogous to emerald.
color_profiles.conditions: Highest quality jadeite from Myanmar; colour, transparency, and texture must all be optimal. The finest material is semi-transparent vivid green ('apple green' to 'emerald green').

color_profiles.name: fei-cui-green
color_profiles.cause: Cr3+ for vivid greens; Fe2+/Fe3+ for duller or bluish greens.
color_profiles.conditions: General term for gem jadeite (fei-cui in Mandarin) encompassing a range of green to greenish stones.

color_profiles.name: lavender-jadeite
color_profiles.cause: Mn2+ and/or Fe2+–Fe3+ charge transfer in the structure; exact mechanism debated.
color_profiles.conditions: Myanmar; prized in Chinese market; second most valuable color after imperial green.

color_profiles.name: white-jadeite
color_profiles.cause: Low chromophore content; fine-grained texture scatters light.
color_profiles.conditions: Called 'mutton-fat' when semi-translucent white; often used as carving material.

color_profiles.name: black-jadeite
color_profiles.cause: High Fe3+ (aegirine component) or graphite/carbonaceous inclusions.
color_profiles.conditions: Myanmar and Guatemala; used in modern jewelry.

appearance.visual_notes: Jadeite's waxy polish and semi-transparency in the finest green material ('glassy' or 'icy') is visually unmistakable. The fei-cui terminology in Chinese trade describes a full quality spectrum. A water-drop test (drops stand high on polished surface) and the cold, greasy feel are tactile identifiers used by experienced traders.

optical_and_special_effects.fluorescence: Inert (untreated) to weak white or pale green under LW UV; heavily treated (B-jade) may show blue-white fluorescence
optical_and_special_effects.optical_notes: RI approximately 1.654–1.667 (spot reading on refractometer gives ~1.66). Birefringence ~0.012–0.020 (rarely measured due to aggregate nature). 'Dip dyed' or B+C jade (impregnated and coated) can be detected by Chelsea filter, spectroscopy, or by the resin fluorescence.

notable_localities: Tawmaw (Hpakan), Kachin State, Myanmar — world's primary and premier source of gem jadeite since at least the 13th century
Motagua Valley, Guatemala — second major world source; Mesoamerican jade (Olmec, Maya)
Kotaki River area, Niigata Prefecture, Japan (itoigawa-type jadeite)
formation_and_occurrence.occurrence_notes: Jadeite forms at high pressure (>1 GPa) and relatively low temperature (~200–500°C), conditions typical of subducted oceanic crust. It is brought to the surface in serpentinite mélanges along paleo-subduction zones. Most gem jadeite has been transported to secondary alluvial and residual deposits, where rounded boulders with weathered crusts (called 'skin' in the trade) are characteristic.

birthstone_status: Not a traditional Western birthstone, but recognized in some systems for March or August
zodiac_associations: Virgo, Taurus (in Chinese and some Western systems)
historical_uses: Chinese jade carving tradition spans more than 7,000 years; Neolithic cultures (Hongshan, Liangzhu) produced ritual jades including bi discs and cong tubes, though many early 'jade' artifacts are nephrite

Jadeite from Myanmar only became the dominant Chinese imperial jade material after the 18th century, when Burmese trade routes opened under Qing dynasty patronage — Empress Dowager Cixi was a particularly devoted collector

The Olmec civilization of Mesoamerica (1500–400 BCE) and the Maya (250 BCE–900 CE) worked Guatemalan jadeite into masks, pendants, and celts of great ritual significance; jade was more valuable than gold in Mesoamerican cultures
Aztec mosaic masks incorporating jadeite and other green stones were among the most prized gifts sent to Hernán Cortés, who dismissed them in favor of gold — a historic cultural misvaluation

Itoigawa jadeite in Japan (Niigata Prefecture) has been worked since the Jomon period (~5000 BCE), making it one of the earliest jade-working traditions; Itoigawa jade has been found in Jomon-period sites across Japan

Spanish conquistadors observed Mesoamerican peoples pressing jade stones to their sides to cure 'piedra de ijada' (loin pain/kidney pain) — the origin of the Spanish word 'jade' and the belief in jade's kidney-healing properties
cultural_associations: In Chinese philosophy and culture, jade (yu) is the most revered stone, embodying the Confucian virtues: humanity, justice, wisdom, courage, and purity. The phrase 'precious as jade and gold' reflects its status.
Imperial jade bangles, pendants, and carvings are central to Chinese bridal tradition and family heirlooms across the Chinese diaspora
The Chinese phrase 'jade-like' (如玉, rú yù) is a supreme compliment for skin, character, or beauty
Guatemalan jadeite was rediscovered by archaeologists in the 20th century; a major revival of Maya jade carving began in the 1970s and continues today
Ritual jade bi discs (flat circular discs with central holes) may represent heaven in ancient Chinese cosmology; cong (square outer, circular inner tubes) may represent earth
cultural_and_historical.folklore_notes: In ancient China, the deceased were sometimes buried with jade pieces placed on bodily orifices to preserve the body — jade burial suits (玉衣) of the Han dynasty, made from thousands of jade plaques sewn with gold, silver, or bronze wire, represent the apex of this tradition. Jade was believed to prevent decomposition and ensure immortality.

interpretive_vibe.vibe_notes: Jadeite carries perhaps the heaviest cultural weight of any mineral on Earth — the civilizational stone of China and Mesoamerica simultaneously, formed in the crushing pressure of subduction zones and revered for millennia. Imperial jade's vivid semi-transparent green is one of the most coveted colors in human history.

practical_notes.toxicity: low
practical_notes.handling_notes: Tough and durable; safe to handle. Avoid sharp impacts that could chip carved surfaces or thin sections.
practical_notes.care_notes: Clean with warm water and mild soap; a soft brush for carvings. Avoid chemicals that could attack surface treatments. Genuine untreated jadeite is stable; B-jade (impregnated) is sensitive to cleaning chemicals.
treatments_or_synthetics: A-jade: completely untreated; highest value; authenticated by absence of polymer or bleaching
B-jade: bleached with acid to remove brown Fe-staining, then impregnated with colorless polymer/wax to improve transparency; unstable over time, may yellow or crack
C-jade: dye-treated to add or enhance color; dyes may fade
B+C jade: both impregnated and dyed
Assembled doublets and triplets: thin jade over dyed cement or glass backing
FTIR spectroscopy and UV-Vis are standard gemmological detection methods
common_confusions: Nephrite (the other 'jade' — softer SG ~2.95, amphibole rather than pyroxene)
Maw-sit-sit (a Cr-jadeite-rich rock, not pure jadeite)
practical_notes.market_notes: Imperial jadeite is among the world's most valuable gem materials — top-quality translucent vivid-green pieces routinely exceed diamond prices per carat at Hong Kong auction. Myanmar controls the vast majority of supply; the trade is politically sensitive. Gemmological certification of treatment status (A/B/C jade) is essential.

prompt_fragments: imperial jade vivid green semi-transparent glow
waxy jadeite polish cool to touch
subduction-zone mineral carved to perfection
lavender jadeite whisper of violet

provenance.notes: Cultural history sections are accurate. Jadeite formation conditions (HP-LT metamorphism) are well-established. Trade terminology (A/B/C jade) follows GIA standards.

structural_units: SiO4 tetrahedra linked into a 3D framework
microcrystalline quartz aggregate with abundant inclusions
chemical_profile.atomic_structure_notes: Microcrystalline to cryptocrystalline SiO2 matrix with high proportion of non-silica impurities (iron oxides, clay minerals, organic matter). Impurity content can be high enough to classify some jaspers as silicified mudstones or cherts.
chemical_profile.chemistry_notes: Broadly SiO2 with significant impurity content (up to 20% or more by weight in some specimens) from iron oxides, clay minerals, and other inclusions. Specific gravity varies accordingly.

crystallography.unit_cell_notes: Microcrystalline aggregate; no macroscopic crystal structure.
crystallography.crystallography_notes: No macroscopic crystal faces. Jasper is effectively a rock material — a silicified sedimentary or volcanic deposit — rather than a single mineral.

physical_properties.physical_notes: Opaque is the defining characteristic distinguishing jasper from chalcedony and agate. Specific gravity varies with impurity load. Accepts a very good polish due to microcrystalline structure.

color_profiles.name: red-jasper
color_profiles.cause: Hematite (iron oxide) inclusions distributed through silica matrix.
color_profiles.conditions: Iron-rich formation environment; most common jasper type.

color_profiles.name: yellow-brown-jasper
color_profiles.cause: Goethite and limonite (hydrous iron oxides).
color_profiles.conditions: Varying degrees of iron hydration.

color_profiles.name: green-jasper
color_profiles.cause: Chlorite, actinolite, or iron silicate inclusions.
color_profiles.conditions: Metamorphic or iron silicate-bearing formation.

color_profiles.name: picture-jasper
color_profiles.cause: Differential impurity banding and inclusion patterns creating landscape-like imagery.
color_profiles.conditions: Sedimentary jasper with complex layering; Idaho and Oregon are notable sources.

color_profiles.name: ocean-jasper
color_profiles.cause: Orbicular spherulitic structure in rhyolite-hosted chalcedony/jasper.
color_profiles.conditions: Locality-specific (Madagascar coastal deposits).

color_profiles.name: mookaite
color_profiles.cause: Radiolarite-origin silicite with varied iron and organic impurities.
color_profiles.conditions: Windalia Radiolarite formation, Mooka Station, Western Australia.

appearance.visual_notes: Opaque throughout — this is the key visual identifier. Takes a good polish showing vivid matte-to-waxy colors. Picture jasper is among the most visually complex natural stone materials.

optical_and_special_effects.optical_notes: No notable optical effects. Opacity is consistent and non-negotiable.

notable_localities: United States (Idaho — picture jasper; Oregon — various; South Dakota)
formation_and_occurrence.occurrence_notes: Jasper forms wherever silica-rich fluids permeate and replace iron-bearing sedimentary or volcanic rocks. Many jaspers are essentially silicified mudstones, cherts, or iron formations.

historical_uses: Prehistoric tool-making (flint/chert/jasper flaked tools)
Ancient Egyptian and Mesopotamian seal stones and amulets
Classical Greek and Roman intaglio carving
Medieval European gem carving
Native American ceremonial use (red jasper)
cultural_associations: Red jasper: associated with blood, strength, and protection in many ancient cultures
From Greek 'iaspis'; corresponds to the Hebrew 'yashfeh' in Exodus breastplate
One of the foundation stones in Revelation's heavenly Jerusalem
cultural_and_historical.folklore_notes: One of the oldest named gemstones; 'jasper' appears in texts from ancient Babylonia, Egypt, and the Hebrew Bible. Red jasper was associated with blood and warrior energy across many ancient cultures.

interpretive_vibe.vibe_notes: Jasper is the most opaque and earthy of the silica minerals. It reads as matter, ground, oxidized earth — the base layer beneath everything. Its matte warmth is fundamentally different from the translucent glow of chalcedony.

practical_notes.toxicity: low
practical_notes.handling_notes: Durable and safe for handling. Fine silica dust hazard when cutting; use respiratory protection.
practical_notes.care_notes: Generally stable. Avoid strong acids. Most jasper is stable and durable for jewelry and decorative use.
treatments_or_synthetics: Some jaspers dyed to enhance or alter colors
Impregnation with resins occasionally done on porous specimens
Synthetic jasper-like materials not common
common_confusions: chalcedony (translucent vs jasper's opacity is the key difference)
practical_notes.market_notes: Enormous and diverse market. Many jasper trade names for locality-specific materials. Ocean jasper and mookaite command higher prices. Much material sold as jasper is technically chert or silicified rock.

prompt_fragments: opaque iron-oxide-stained microcrystalline silica
canyon-floor earth-body matte mineral
picture jasper landscape imagery in stone

structural_units: corner-sharing TO4 tetrahedra in a 3D feldspar framework
chemical_profile.atomic_structure_notes: Framework silicate (tectosilicate). Labradorite is the intermediate member of the plagioclase solid-solution series between albite (NaAlSi3O8, An0) and anorthite (CaAl2Si2O8, An100), with composition An50–An70. The Al/Si ordering and Ca/Na distribution on specific sites define the structure. Lamellar intergrowths of Ca-rich and Na-rich feldspar domains on a nanometer scale are responsible for labradorescence.
chemical_profile.chemistry_notes: Plagioclase series member. The labradorescence (schiller) effect arises from thin-film interference in alternating Ca-rich and Na-rich exsolution lamellae. Lamellar spacing of approximately 100–300 nm produces the interference colors observed.

crystallography.unit_cell_notes: a ≈ 8.17 Å, b ≈ 12.87 Å, c ≈ 7.11 Å; α ≈ 93.5°, β ≈ 116.6°, γ ≈ 90.5° (approximate; varies with composition)
crystallography.twinning: Polysynthetic twinning common (Carlsbad, Albite, and Pericline twin laws); lamellar twinning is related to exsolution and produces labradorescence.
crystallography.crystallography_notes: Triclinic symmetry. Polysynthetic albite and pericline twinning visible microscopically and sometimes macroscopically. The exsolution lamellae responsible for labradorescence are crystallographically controlled.

physical_properties.physical_notes: Two cleavage directions typical of feldspars. Cleavage at nearly right angles (86° and 94°). Transparent gem-quality labradorite exists but is uncommon. Most specimens are translucent to opaque.

color_profiles.name: labradorescence-blue-green
color_profiles.cause: Thin-film interference from Ca-rich and Na-rich exsolution lamellae at ~100–300 nm spacing. Shorter lamellar spacing produces blue; longer spacing shifts toward green and gold.
color_profiles.conditions: Most common labradorescence colors; seen in standard gray labradorite.

color_profiles.name: labradorescence-gold-copper
color_profiles.cause: Longer-spacing exsolution lamellae shifting interference toward yellow-orange wavelengths.
color_profiles.conditions: Specific lamellar spacing; can coexist with blue-green in the same specimen.

color_profiles.name: spectrolite
color_profiles.cause: Full-range lamellar spacing distribution on a very dark ground mass, producing all visible colors.
color_profiles.conditions: Locality-specific to Ylämaa district, Finland. Requires the dark opaque ground color to display full spectrum.

color_profiles.name: transparent-gem
color_profiles.cause: Body color from iron or other trace impurities; transparent facetable material.
color_profiles.conditions: Rare; some sources in Ethiopia, Mexico (andesine controversy), Tanzania.

appearance.visual_notes: The labradorescence appears as a floating, depth-inhabiting iridescence that shifts with viewing angle and light direction. Unlike surface iridescence, it appears to come from within the stone. The dark gray to near-black base color intensifies the color display. Best specimens show full-spectrum color shift across a single face.

optical_and_special_effects.iridescence: Labradorescence: intense thin-film interference iridescence showing blue, green, gold, copper, orange — and in spectrolite, the full visible spectrum — from exsolution lamellae oriented parallel to {010}. The effect is directional and changes with both viewing angle and light angle.
optical_and_special_effects.optical_notes: Labradorescence is caused by thin-film interference (Bragg reflection/constructive interference) from alternating Ca-rich (anorthite-component) and Na-rich (albite-component) exsolution lamellae, typically 100–300 nm thick, parallel to the {010} cleavage plane. The phenomenon is sometimes called schiller or adularescence in related feldspars, but labradorescence is the precise term for this plagioclase effect.

geologic_environments: basalt and gabbro as primary phenocrysts or groundmass
notable_localities: Labrador, Canada (Paul's Island — type locality; first scientifically described 1770)
Finland (Ylämaa district — spectrolite; near-black with full-spectrum labradorescence)
Ukraine (larvikite — a nepheline syenite with labradorite, used as architectural stone)
formation_and_occurrence.occurrence_notes: Labradorite is an essential and very common mineral in mafic and intermediate igneous rocks worldwide. Gem-quality material with strong labradorescence is less common and associated with specific geological settings. The plagioclase in anorthosite massifs is often labradorite-composition.

historical_uses: First described scientifically from Paul's Island, Labrador, Canada in 1770 by Moravian missionaries
Adopted as a gemstone and ornamental stone in the 18th–19th century
Finnish spectrolite quarried since the 1940s; national gemstone of Finland
Larvikite (Norwegian labradorite-bearing rock) used as architectural stone worldwide
cultural_associations: Inuit peoples of Labrador have stories of the Northern Lights being trapped in stone — labradorescence the cause
Associated with magic, transformation, and hidden knowledge in modern crystal traditions
National gemstone of Finland (spectrolite variety)
cultural_and_historical.folklore_notes: Inuit legend describes the aurora borealis as frozen light trapped inside labradorite. Moravian missionaries encountering the stone in Labrador in 1770 were reportedly struck by the play of color. The Finnish spectrolite variety was not discovered until World War II when a German bomb crater revealed an extraordinary deposit.

interpretive_vibe.vibe_notes: Labradorite carries its color inside — it is not a surface phenomenon but a depth effect. The dark ground color against the shifting iridescence creates a sense of something luminous moving beneath a dark surface. Spectrolite, with its near-black matrix and full-spectrum display, is one of the most visually complex single mineral specimens on earth.

practical_notes.toxicity: low
practical_notes.handling_notes: Safe for handling. Cleavage in two directions means it can chip or cleave if dropped or struck. Avoid sharp impacts.
practical_notes.care_notes: Avoid ultrasonic cleaners (can shatter along cleavage planes). Clean with damp cloth. Avoid prolonged exposure to harsh chemicals.
treatments_or_synthetics: Some transparent faceted 'andesine-labradorite' from China/Congo subject to copper-diffusion treatment controversy; treated material sold as natural
Surface coatings sometimes applied to enhance labradorescence on lower-quality material
common_confusions: rainbow-moonstone (actually labradorite with adularescence-like effect; sold as moonstone in trade)
spectrolite vs standard labradorite (Finnish spectrolite is locality-specific and more valuable)
practical_notes.market_notes: Madagascar provides most commercial gem-quality material. Spectrolite from Finland is premium. Rainbow moonstone (labradorite) is pervasive in the jewelry market. Strong full-spectrum labradorescence commands significant premiums.

prompt_fragments: floating prismatic aurora iridescence in dark gray feldspar
thin-film interference labradorescence shifting blue-green-gold
northern lights imprisoned in polished stone

provenance.notes: Tier 1 entry. Rainbow moonstone in the trade is actually labradorite — noted in confusions.

structural_units: Si4O10 (tetrahedral) sheet units of SiO4 and AlO4 tetrahedra
Octahedral layer (Li, Al) sandwiched between two tetrahedral sheets
Interlayer K+ cations binding the 2:1 sheets
chemical_profile.atomic_structure_notes: Phyllosilicate (sheet silicate), 2:1 mica structure. Two tetrahedral silicate sheets sandwich one octahedral sheet in each layer. K+ occupies the interlayer, weakly bonding layers — responsible for perfect basal cleavage. Li occupies octahedral sites alongside Al, making lepidolite a trioctahedral-to-dioctahedral transitional mica. The high F content (F replacing OH) is typical of Li-bearing pegmatitic micas.
chemical_profile.chemistry_notes: Lepidolite is the most common lithium-bearing mica. Li content (reported as Li2O) is typically 3–7 wt%. It also concentrates Rb and Cs, which substitute for K in the interlayer — making lepidolite a potential indicator mineral and minor source of these rare alkali metals. Mn2+/Mn3+ produces the characteristic lavender/pink/purple color. The F:OH ratio varies; more fluorine-rich lepidolite tends to be whiter. IMA now recognizes lepidolite as a series between polylithionite and trilithionite.

crystallography.twinning: Polysynthetic twinning on {001} common; produces stacking polytypes (1M, 2M1, 3T, etc.)
crystallography.crystallography_notes: Lepidolite occurs in several polytypes (1M, 2M1, 3T most common) due to different stacking sequences of the 2:1 layers. The 1M polytype (monoclinic) is most common in pegmatites. Crystals typically form thin pseudo-hexagonal plates or tablets. Fine-grained scaly masses ('lepidolite' derives from Greek lepidos, scale) are the most common occurrence. Individual books or stacks of platy crystals can reach several centimeters.

cleavage: perfect basal {001} — thin flexible, elastic cleavage flakes
physical_properties.tenacity: flexible and elastic (cleavage flakes); brittle when massive
physical_properties.physical_notes: Perfect basal cleavage into flexible, elastic sheets is characteristic of all micas, including lepidolite. The pearly luster on cleavage surfaces results from internal reflections within the thin sheets. Hardness varies; individual cleavage flakes measure lower (2.5), while massive aggregates feel harder (up to 4). The relatively high SG for a mica reflects the combined weight of Li, Al, K, and F.

color_profiles.name: lavender-lepidolite
color_profiles.cause: Mn2+ and/or Mn3+ substituting in octahedral sites; trace amounts produce pastel lavender.
color_profiles.conditions: Most common lepidolite color; widespread in Li-rich pegmatites globally.

color_profiles.name: pink-lepidolite
color_profiles.cause: Higher Mn content shifting from lavender toward pink.
color_profiles.conditions: Minas Gerais, Brazil; Pala, California; Madagascar.

color_profiles.name: purple-lepidolite
color_profiles.cause: Elevated Mn3+ content; sometimes enhanced by inclusions.
color_profiles.conditions: Less common; vivid purple from specific Brazilian and Afghan localities.

color_profiles.name: colorless-white-lepidolite
color_profiles.cause: Absence of Mn or other chromophores; high F content typical.
color_profiles.conditions: Fluorine-rich lepidolite in pegmatites.

color_profiles.name: yellow-lepidolite
color_profiles.cause: Minor Fe3+ or specific structural defects.
color_profiles.conditions: Uncommon; some localities in Brazil and Russia.

common_forms: fine-grained lavender scaly masses in pegmatite
platy books up to several centimeters
appearance.visual_notes: Lepidolite is one of the most visually distinctive micas due to its lavender/pink color — the only common lavender mica. In pegmatites, it often forms spectacular masses of pale lavender scales intergrown with pink tourmaline (rubellite) and rose quartz. The pearly luster on cleavage surfaces gives a soft, shimmering quality.

optical_and_special_effects.fluorescence: Weak cream to yellow under SW UV in some specimens; generally weak
optical_and_special_effects.iridescence: Subtle adularescence-like pearly shimmer on cleavage surfaces
optical_and_special_effects.pleochroism: Weak; colorless to pale lavender in different orientations
optical_and_special_effects.optical_notes: Biaxial negative. RI approximately 1.525–1.587 (varies with composition). Birefringence ~0.018–0.038. Perfect basal cleavage makes conventional RI measurement difficult; usually measured on cleavage flake.

notable_localities: Minas Gerais, Brazil (Resplendor, Governador Valadares, Araçuaí — world's largest specimens)
Pala district, San Diego County, California, USA (with tourmaline and kunzite)
Nuristan and Kunar, Afghanistan (with elbaite tourmaline)
Alabashka and Murzinka, Ural Mountains, Russia
formation_and_occurrence.occurrence_notes: Lepidolite is a characteristic mineral of highly evolved (LCT-type) granitic pegmatites that are enriched in Li, Cs, Ta, and F. It is a late-stage crystallization product, forming after most feldspars and quartz, often in the intermediate and core zones of zoned pegmatites. Its presence is an indicator of economic Li-mineral potential. Lepidolite itself is occasionally mined as a lithium ore (Li2O: 3–6 wt%), though spodumene and amblygonite are typically preferred. It is a major flux mineral in pegmatite systems.

historical_uses: Lepidolite was an early commercial lithium mineral; lithium was first isolated from petalite in 1817, but lepidolite was one of the main industrial Li sources in the late 19th and early 20th centuries before spodumene deposits became dominant
Used as a flux in glass and ceramic glazes; its high F content and flux properties are commercially relevant
In the 20th century, large masses of lepidolite were mined in Brazil and California as a minor Li ore and as a fluxing agent in specialty glasses
Modern lithium demand (EV batteries, pharmaceuticals) has renewed interest in all Li-bearing pegmatite minerals including lepidolite
cultural_associations: In contemporary crystal trade, lepidolite is marketed as a 'calming' or 'anti-anxiety' stone, possibly a reflection of its lavender color association with calm and its name connection to lithium (a real pharmaceutical mood-stabilizing element)

Note: while lepidolite does contain lithium, contact with a specimen does not deliver pharmacologically relevant doses — this is a popular but scientifically unfounded claim in crystal healing contexts
cultural_and_historical.folklore_notes: The lavender color and lithium-bearing nature have combined in popular imagination to make lepidolite a stone of 'calm and peace' in New Age traditions. The real lithium connection — as one of the oldest lithium mineral sources — gives it an unexpected industrial modernity beneath its gentle pastel appearance.

interpretive_vibe.vibe_notes: Lepidolite is the gentlest common pegmatite mineral — a pastel lavender mica shimmer against stark feldspar and quartz. Its lithium content gives it a surprising industrial dimension hidden beneath its soft aesthetic. In combination with pink tourmaline and rose quartz, it creates some of the most feminine-coded pegmatite assemblages.

practical_notes.toxicity: Low. Lepidolite itself is not toxic in normal handling. Lithium in the structure is not bioavailable through skin contact. Fine dust from cutting should be avoided (general silicate dust precaution).
practical_notes.handling_notes: Soft (Mohs 2.5–4); easily scratched. Platy cleavage means thin pieces can split. Handle massive pieces normally; avoid pressing thin cleavage flakes.
practical_notes.care_notes: Avoid abrasives. Clean with soft damp cloth. Avoid acid soaking (can attack cleavage surfaces). Store separately from harder minerals to prevent scratching.
treatments_or_synthetics: No common treatments in the mineral trade
Sometimes stabilized with resin for use in jewelry when massive
Synthetic lithium micas are produced industrially but not sold as gem materials
common_confusions: Muscovite (white to silvery — lacks lavender color)
Lavender fluorite (isometric, no cleavage in sheets)
Sugilite (purple but massive, no mica cleavage)
Kunzite (spodumene — more vitreous, different crystal form)
practical_notes.market_notes: Large scaly masses of lavender lepidolite from Brazil and Afghanistan are widely available and relatively affordable. Fine individual crystals or books are more collected. Popular in tumbled stone and crystal healing markets due to lavender color. Industrial interest in Li content is distinct from the gem/mineral trade.

polymorphs: Various polytypes: 1M, 2M1, 3T (stacking polytypes, not true polymorphs)

prompt_fragments: lavender scaly lepidolite mica in pegmatite
soft lilac pearly shimmer mineral
lithium-bearing pink mica books
lepidolite intergrown with pink tourmaline

provenance.notes: IMA now treats lepidolite as a series name between polylithionite and trilithionite end members, but the traditional species name lepidolite is retained here per mindat and common usage. Li pharmacology note in cultural section is a responsible correction of a common misconception.

chemical_profile.atomic_structure_notes: Structure described with strongly distorted CuO6 octahedra.
chemical_profile.chemistry_notes: Secondary copper mineral.

crystallography.crystallography_notes: Single crystals exist but are much less common than aggregate forms.

physical_properties.physical_notes: Best known visually for rich green surfaces and patterned growth.

color_profiles.name: classic-malachite-green
color_profiles.cause: Copper-bearing chemistry.
color_profiles.conditions: Common across many specimens.

appearance.visual_notes: One of the strongest 'ornamental pattern' minerals in existence.

formation_and_occurrence.occurrence_notes: Typically develops as a secondary copper mineral in oxidized environments.

interpretive_vibe.vibe_notes: Perfect for anything ornate, hypnotic, green, dangerous, or ceremonial.

practical_notes.toxicity: Copper-bearing mineral; avoid ingesting dust and use sensible handling.
practical_notes.handling_notes: Can be softer and more delicate than people expect.
practical_notes.care_notes: Avoid harsh abrasives and acid exposure.
practical_notes.market_notes: Frequently used in carvings, beads, cabs, slabs, and decorative objects.

prompt_fragments: botryoidal copper-green domes
banded malachite concentric eyes
ornamental toxic-jewel swirl pattern

provenance.notes: Starter example entry.

other_ids.note: Moonstone is a variety of orthoclase/adularia; mindat_id references parent orthoclase entry

structural_units: corner-sharing TO4 tetrahedra in a 3D feldspar framework
alternating orthoclase and albite exsolution lamellae
chemical_profile.atomic_structure_notes: Framework silicate (tectosilicate). Moonstone consists of intergrown orthoclase (KAlSi3O8) and albite (NaAlSi3O8) layers produced by exsolution during slow cooling. These alternating nanoscale lamellae (typically 10–300 nm thick) scatter and interfere with light to produce adularescence. Thinner lamellae produce blue adularescence; thicker lamellae produce white or peach glow.
chemical_profile.chemistry_notes: Parent mineral is orthoclase (monoclinic K-feldspar) or the low-temperature polymorph adularia. Albite lamellae are exsolved from the original homogeneous K-feldspar during slow cooling. The finest Sri Lankan blue moonstone has very thin, evenly spaced lamellae.

crystallography.unit_cell_notes: a ≈ 8.56 Å, b ≈ 13.00 Å, c ≈ 7.17 Å; β ≈ 116.0° (orthoclase; approximate)
crystallography.twinning: Carlsbad twinning common; fine-scale albite twinning in adularia.
crystallography.crystallography_notes: Moonstone is cut as cabochon to maximize adularescence; the stone must be oriented with the exsolution lamellae parallel to the base of the cabochon. Incorrect orientation eliminates the adularescent effect entirely.

physical_properties.physical_notes: Two cleavage directions intersecting at near-right-angles, typical of feldspars. Mohs 6–6.5 makes moonstone susceptible to surface scratching with daily use. Transparency varies from near-transparent (finest Sri Lankan) to translucent (most commercial material).

color_profiles.name: blue-moonstone
colors: colorless to white body; blue floating glow
color_profiles.cause: Thin albite exsolution lamellae (10–100 nm) produce Rayleigh/Tyndall scattering and constructive interference for blue wavelengths.
color_profiles.conditions: Finest quality moonstone from Sri Lanka; requires very thin, evenly distributed lamellae. Body must be near-colorless to transparent for the blue to appear.

color_profiles.name: white-moonstone
colors: white body; white to milky-white floating glow
color_profiles.cause: Thicker lamellae scatter all visible wavelengths approximately equally, producing white adularescence.
color_profiles.conditions: Most common commercial moonstone; broader range of sources.

color_profiles.name: peach-moonstone
colors: warm-beige body; peach to white glow
color_profiles.cause: Iron impurities impart warm body color; adularescence still present.
color_profiles.conditions: India (Rajasthan) is major source of peach moonstone.

color_profiles.name: rainbow-moonstone
colors: colorless to white body; multicolor iridescence — blue
color_profiles.cause: Trade name for colorless to white labradorite showing adularescence-like labradorescence. Not orthoclase moonstone.
color_profiles.conditions: This material is actually labradorite (plagioclase feldspar), not orthoclase. The 'rainbow moonstone' trade name is widespread but mineralogically inaccurate.

color_profiles.name: gray-moonstone
colors: gray body; blue to white floating glow
color_profiles.cause: Iron or other inclusions giving gray body color; adularescence retained.
color_profiles.conditions: India; common commercial material.

color_profiles.name: cat-eye-moonstone
color_profiles.cause: Needle-like inclusions aligned with exsolution lamellae create chatoyancy in addition to adularescence.
color_profiles.conditions: Rare; Sri Lanka.

appearance.visual_notes: Adularescence appears as a floating, glowing light phenomenon that rolls or shifts as the stone is moved — unlike surface iridescence, it appears to emanate from within the stone. The finest blue moonstone from Sri Lanka has an electric, almost liquid blue light that rolls across the interior.

optical_and_special_effects.fluorescence: Weak bluish or yellowish fluorescence in some specimens under longwave UV.
optical_and_special_effects.chatoyancy: Rare; cat's eye moonstone exists with needle inclusions.
