optical_and_special_effects.iridescence: Adularescence: the defining optical phenomenon. A floating, billowing blue or white light glow from alternating orthoclase/albite exsolution lamellae. Caused by Rayleigh/Tyndall scattering and constructive thin-film interference. Blue adularescence (from thin lamellae) is most prized. Effect is direction-dependent and orientation-critical in cutting.
optical_and_special_effects.optical_notes: Adularescence is distinct from labradorescence (tiger-stripe directional flash in labradorite) and from aventurescence (metallic glitter from platelets). Adularescence is a soft, rolling, internal glow — sometimes described as 'billowing light trapped underwater.' The phenomenon was named after adularia, the low-temperature orthoclase polymorph.

notable_localities: Sri Lanka (finest blue moonstone; Meetiyagoda — near Galle; internationally benchmark quality)
India (Rajasthan — peach and 'rainbow moonstone'/labradorite; large commercial source)
formation_and_occurrence.occurrence_notes: Sri Lankan moonstone deposit supply is declining; finest blue moonstone from Meetiyagoda area has become increasingly scarce. Indian material dominates the commercial market but is often the labradorite 'rainbow moonstone' variety.

birthstone_status: June birthstone (traditional; along with pearl and alexandrite)
Modern June birthstone (along with pearl and alexandrite)
historical_uses: Used in ancient Roman and Greek jewelry; Romans believed it contained the image of Diana, goddess of the moon
Revered in Hindu tradition as a sacred stone of the moon god Chandra
Art Nouveau jewelry movement (1890–1910) featured moonstone prominently — René Lalique designs
June birthstone designation formalized by the American National Retail Jewelers Association in 1912
cultural_associations: Deeply associated with the moon, lunar cycles, femininity, and water across many cultures
Roman myth: moonstone formed from solidified moonbeams
Hindu sacred stone; used in religious jewelry and ritual objects
Associated with love and fertility in many traditions
Art Nouveau era symbol of the Romantic and the mysterious
cultural_and_historical.folklore_notes: Pliny the Elder wrote that moonstone's appearance changed with the phases of the moon. Roman legend held that it was formed from moonlight solidified. In India, moonstone is considered a sacred stone and a dream stone — sleeping with it is said to bring beautiful visions. The Art Nouveau jewelers of the late 19th century elevated moonstone to iconic status in Western fine jewelry.

interpretive_vibe.vibe_notes: Moonstone has one of the most evocative optical effects in mineralogy — the adularescence gives it a liquid, otherworldly quality as if light is floating just beneath the surface. The blue variety in particular has an electric, cold, lunar quality. It is the mineral most consistently associated with water, night, and the feminine across unconnected cultures.

practical_notes.toxicity: low
practical_notes.handling_notes: Safe for handling. Two cleavage directions and moderate hardness (6–6.5) mean it can chip or scratch with daily jewelry wear. Avoid knocking against hard surfaces.
practical_notes.care_notes: Do not use ultrasonic or steam cleaners (cleavage risk). Clean with damp cloth and mild soap. Remove before physical activity. Store cushioned to prevent scratching.
treatments_or_synthetics: Rainbow moonstone (actually labradorite) is widely sold as moonstone — not treated, but taxonomically misleading
Synthetic orthoclase exists but is not commercially common as moonstone simulant
Blue color in some material may be surface-enhanced (coatings)
Glass imitations (opalescent glass) common at low price points
practical_notes.market_notes: Fine blue moonstone from Sri Lanka has become increasingly rare and commands high prices. Most commercial moonstone is Indian peach/white or 'rainbow moonstone' (labradorite) from India and Madagascar. Trade naming confusion with rainbow moonstone (labradorite) is pervasive and not expected to be resolved commercially.

prompt_fragments: floating blue light billowing under translucent feldspar surface
lunar milk adularescence in near-colorless K-feldspar cabochon
night-water cool glow of Sri Lankan blue moonstone

provenance.notes: Tier 1 entry. Rainbow moonstone trade name clarified as labradorite — important commercial confusion documented.

chemical_profile.atomic_structure_notes: Cyclosilicate structure. Mn2+ and Mn3+ substituting for Al3+ in octahedral sites produce the pink-to-peach color. Cs is often elevated in morganite compared to other beryl varieties (reflecting Cs-rich pegmatite environments). Li may occupy channel sites.
chemical_profile.chemistry_notes: Manganese is the primary chromophore. Mn2+ gives pink; Mn3+ gives deeper pink to purplish. Cesium-rich Cs-beryl (pezzottaite) is related but is a distinct species (Cs-dominant). Irradiation produces orange tones; heat treatment removes these to give cleaner pink. Fe contamination produces brownish-orange overcast.

crystallography.twinning: Rare
crystallography.crystallography_notes: Typically short prismatic to tabular hexagonal crystals, reflecting the Cs-enriched pegmatite environment which produces broader, flatter crystal habits. Prismatic faces often striated. Crystals can be large in Cs-Li pegmatites.

physical_properties.physical_notes: Higher SG than other beryls due to Cs enrichment in crystal structure. Good hardness and durability for jewelry use. Generally cleaner (fewer inclusions) than emerald.

color_profiles.name: Rose / Pure Pink Morganite
color_profiles.cause: Mn2+/Mn3+ at moderate concentration; ideal ratio for purest rose-pink.
color_profiles.conditions: Madagascar, California; heat-treated to remove orangey cast.

color_profiles.name: Peach Morganite
color_profiles.cause: Higher Mn with Fe contributions or natural irradiation color centers.
color_profiles.conditions: Common unheated rough; heat treatment removes orange to produce cleaner pink.

color_profiles.name: Deep Pink / Hot Pink Morganite
color_profiles.cause: Higher Mn3+ concentration.
color_profiles.conditions: Rare; most prized morganite color.

appearance.visual_notes: Morganite's soft rose-peach color is inherently gentle and warm — less assertive than ruby, less cold than pink sapphire. Large clean crystals are available. The rose-gold + morganite combination became dominant in contemporary jewelry design in the 2010s.

optical_and_special_effects.fluorescence: Weak to none
optical_and_special_effects.optical_notes: Uniaxial negative. RI approximately 1.570–1.600. Birefringence ~0.008. Typically clean and well-suited for faceting. Pleochroism mild.

geologic_environments: complex pegmatites with lepidolite, tourmaline, spodumene
notable_localities: Anjanabonoina, Fianarantsoa, Madagascar (world class — finest and largest)
Pala district (Pala Chief Mine), San Diego County, California, USA
Urucum and Golconda, Minas Gerais, Brazil
formation_and_occurrence.occurrence_notes: Morganite is primarily a product of lithium-cesium-tantalum (LCT) granitic pegmatites. It requires Mn-rich fluids and a relatively evolved (late-stage) pegmatite environment. Madagascar's Anjanabonoina is the premier locality for gem-quality material. Named after financier J.P. Morgan in 1911 by George Frederick Kunz.

historical_uses: Named in 1911 by George Frederick Kunz (Tiffany & Co. head gemologist) for financier J.P. Morgan
J.P. Morgan donated major mineral collections to AMNH and Harvard
Gemstone use since naming; rose-gold combination popularized post-2010
cultural_associations: Named for J.P. Morgan — reflects early 20th-century patronage of mineralogy
Rose-gold + morganite aesthetic became 2010s jewelry trend
Often positioned as an alternative/complement to pink diamond and pink sapphire
cultural_and_historical.folklore_notes: Morganite is a relatively recently named gem variety (1911) compared to emerald and aquamarine. George Frederick Kunz named it for J.P. Morgan, one of the great mineral collectors of the Gilded Age — whose collections are now at the American Museum of Natural History and Harvard's Mineralogical Museum. The 'morganite + rose gold' combination became a defining aesthetic of 2010s jewelry design, particularly in engagement rings.

interpretive_vibe.vibe_notes: Morganite is the warm, quiet sister of emerald and aquamarine — the peachy pink end of beryl's spectrum. It lacks the drama of other beryls but has a consistent warmth and gentleness. The rose-gold era made it a design archetype.

practical_notes.toxicity: Beryllium-bearing; dust from grinding hazardous. Finished gems safe.
practical_notes.handling_notes: Good hardness and durability. Heat treatment (to remove orange-peach cast) standard and undisclosed. Similar jewelry durability to aquamarine.
practical_notes.care_notes: Clean with warm soapy water. Ultrasonic generally safe. Avoid strong acids and heavy impacts.
treatments_or_synthetics: Heat treatment: standard and accepted; removes orange/peach from irradiated-color centers, giving cleaner pink
Irradiation: sometimes used to produce or deepen orange-pink (less stable)
Synthetic morganite: uncommon commercially
common_confusions: Rose quartz (softer; lower RI; massive habit)
Pink topaz (biaxial; different SG and RI)
Kunzite (spodumene — biaxial; very strong pleochroism; cleaves perfectly)
Pink sapphire (much harder; uniaxial; different RI)
practical_notes.market_notes: Increasingly popular. Large clean stones are available and relatively affordable compared to emerald. Madagascar is the primary source of premium material. Rose-gold setting trend drove significant market growth. Heat treatment universal and accepted.

prompt_fragments: morganite: peach-rose beryl from Cs-Li pegmatite — warm manganese hexagonal form
Madagascar morganite tabular crystal — blush warmth in beryllium silicate
rose-gold + morganite: 2010s design archetype in soft pink beryl

provenance.notes: Morganite is a variety of beryl. mindat_id 2756 is the morganite variety listing.

structural_units: Si4O11 double chains of SiO4 tetrahedra (inosilicate)
Mg/Fe in M1, M2, M3 octahedral sites
chemical_profile.atomic_structure_notes: Double-chain inosilicate (amphibole). Two single pyroxene-like chains are cross-linked to form a double chain with composition Si4O11. This broader ribbon accommodates larger cations (Ca) at the M4 site. Nephrite is not a single mineral but a solid solution between tremolite (Mg-dominant: Ca2Mg5Si8O22(OH)2) and actinolite (Fe-dominant: Ca2Fe5Si8O22(OH)2). The felt-like interlocking fibrous texture of nephrite provides its legendary toughness.
chemical_profile.chemistry_notes: Color and properties vary along the tremolite–actinolite solid solution: pure tremolite is white to colorless; increasing Fe (actinolite component) produces progressively deeper green. The spinach-green of classic nephrite reflects intermediate-to-Fe-rich composition. Minor Cr can produce vivid green. White 'mutton-fat' nephrite is tremolite-dominant and highly prized in Chinese tradition.

crystallography.unit_cell_notes: Tremolite: a ≈ 9.84 Å, b ≈ 18.05 Å, c ≈ 5.28 Å, β ≈ 104.7°; varies with Fe content
crystallography.twinning: Simple twinning on {100} known; rarely visible in massive nephrite
crystallography.crystallography_notes: As a gem material, nephrite is almost always a fine-grained to fibrous interlocking aggregate — individual crystals are rarely visible to the naked eye. Elongate fibres and blades cross-link in a felt-like microstructure that is directly responsible for nephrite's exceptional toughness, widely considered the toughest natural stone. Bladed and prismatic crystals with two cleavages at ~56° and ~124° (characteristic amphibole angle) are found in coarser metamorphic contexts.

cleavage: perfect prismatic {110} at ~56° and ~124° (amphibole cleavage, two directions)
physical_properties.tenacity: very tough (exceptional; interlocking fibrous microstructure makes it among the toughest natural stones)
physical_properties.physical_notes: Lower SG than jadeite (~2.90–3.03 vs. 3.24–3.43), useful for distinction. Mohs hardness slightly lower (6–6.5 vs. 6.5–7). The toughness of nephrite is proverbial — the interlocking fibrous texture means it resists fracture far better than most minerals of similar hardness. Polynesian and Native American cultures exploited this toughness for axe heads and tools.

color_profiles.name: spinach-jade
color_profiles.cause: Moderate to high Fe2+ substituting for Mg in actinolite-rich nephrite; Fe-rich composition.
color_profiles.conditions: Classic nephrite from New Zealand (pounamu), Siberia, China.

color_profiles.name: mutton-fat-jade
color_profiles.cause: Low Fe content; near-pure tremolite composition; fine texture diffuses light.
color_profiles.conditions: Hetian (Hotan) River pebbles, Xinjiang, China — the most prized nephrite in Chinese tradition.

color_profiles.name: celadon-jade
color_profiles.cause: Low Fe content with minor chromophores.
color_profiles.conditions: Pale green nephrite; also prized in Chinese aesthetics.

color_profiles.name: gray-nephrite
color_profiles.cause: Graphite inclusions or specific Fe oxidation states.
color_profiles.conditions: Various localities including British Columbia, Canada.

color_profiles.name: black-nephrite
color_profiles.cause: Very high Fe content and/or carbonaceous inclusions.
color_profiles.conditions: Some Canadian and New Zealand material.

appearance.visual_notes: Nephrite's waxy sheen and subtle translucency are distinctive. White mutton-fat jade has a soft luminosity unlike any other stone. The green varieties can look similar to jadeite but typically show a more subdued, less intense green. The fibrous microstructure is visible under magnification as a tangled felt-like mass.

optical_and_special_effects.fluorescence: Generally inert; some material shows very weak cream or green fluorescence under LW UV
optical_and_special_effects.pleochroism: Minimal in massive aggregate material; individual crystals dichroic (colorless to pale green)
optical_and_special_effects.optical_notes: RI approximately 1.600–1.627 (spot reading ~1.61 on refractometer). Distinguished from jadeite by lower RI and lower SG. The aggregate texture prevents measurement of birefringence in the usual way.

notable_localities: Hetian (Hotan) area, Xinjiang, China — most prized white nephrite; Yurungkash and Karakash rivers yield famous river pebbles
Luodian, Guizhou, China and Qinghai, China
South Island (Te Wai Pounamu), New Zealand — pounamu (greenstone); West Coast rivers and in-situ sources
Polar and Sub-Polar Urals, Russia (Siberian nephrite)
Fraser Valley and Cassiar, British Columbia, Canada (large volumes)
formation_and_occurrence.occurrence_notes: Nephrite forms at lower pressures than jadeite, typically in contact metamorphic or lower-grade regional metamorphic settings at serpentinite margins. Mg-rich fluids metasomatizing serpentinite at contacts with dolomitic marbles or calcareous rocks are a classic formation environment. River pebbles (as in the Hetian area) are prized because weathering reveals quality and shape while polishing the surface.

birthstone_status: Not a formal birthstone; sometimes listed for August or March informally
historical_uses: China: nephrite carving spans at least 7,000–8,000 years from the Neolithic Hemudu and Xinglongwa cultures. The Liangzhu culture (3300–2300 BCE) produced extraordinary ritual jade bi discs and cong tubes of nephrite. The Zhou dynasty codified jade's symbolic role; Confucius enumerated its eleven virtues. Hetian white nephrite (mutton-fat jade) became the imperial standard under the Han and Tang dynasties.

New Zealand: pounamu (greenstone, Māori name for nephrite) is deeply sacred to Māori and holds a unique status in New Zealand law — under the Māori Fisheries Act and subsequent legislation, pounamu in the South Island belongs to Ngāi Tahu iwi. Traditional hei-tiki pendants, mere (short weapons), adzes, and chisels were made from pounamu; it was used as currency and in diplomatic gift exchange. To gift a hei-tiki is to transfer the mana (spiritual power) accumulated in the stone.
Polynesia: nephrite axes and adzes were among the most prized trade goods across the Pacific; New Zealand greenstone traded as far as the Chatham Islands
Mesoamerica: Olmec and Maya peoples used nephrite alongside jadeite; some Olmec 'jade' carvings are nephrite

Neolithic Europe: nephrite axes and adzes circulated widely in exchange networks; Alpine nephrite sources (Monviso, Italy) supplied much of Neolithic Western Europe's jade tools via long-distance trade
Prehistoric Siberia: Baikal-area nephrite was worked by Bronze Age cultures into knives and ornaments
Spanish conquistadors encountered jade curing practices in Mesoamerica — pressing jade to the kidneys for 'piedra de ijada' (loin stone), the origin of the Spanish word 'jade'
cultural_associations: In Chinese culture, jade (yu) is the supreme material — representing heaven, earth, and human virtue. The character 王 (king) became 玉 (jade) with the addition of a single stroke, indicating the closeness of kingship and jade.

Confucius identified 11 virtues in jade: benevolence (soft warm lustre), wisdom (translucency), courage (hardness that does not injure when broken), justice (sharp angles), purity (bright clear sound when struck)
The Jade Emperor (Yù Huáng) is the supreme deity in Chinese folk religion and Taoism; jade signifies divine authority
Chinese jade burial traditions: jade plugs for the nine orifices (eyes, ears, nostrils, mouth, navel, genitals, anus) were placed on the deceased to preserve the body and ensure immortality
Māori hei-tiki (humanoid jade pendants) accumulate mana (spiritual power) through generations; they are among the most spiritually significant objects in Māori culture
The West Coast of New Zealand's South Island is named Te Wai Pounamu (The Waters of Greenstone)
Hetian jade was a key luxury commodity on the ancient Silk Road; the 'Jade Road' predates the Silk Road, with nephrite traded to China from Xinjiang for over 3,000 years
cultural_and_historical.folklore_notes: In Chinese tradition, jade is said to have a 'living' quality — it absorbs the essence of its wearer over time and gives back healing energy. It is said to feel alive in a way that other stones do not. A jade bangle that breaks is said to have 'sacrificed itself' to protect its owner from harm.

interpretive_vibe.vibe_notes: Nephrite is the older jade — the stone that launched millennia of Chinese and Pacific jade culture before jadeite arrived. Its mutton-fat white and spinach green are among the most culturally loaded color-material combinations in human history. It is not the most beautiful stone, but it may be the most meaningful.

practical_notes.toxicity: low
practical_notes.handling_notes: Very tough and durable; suitable for carving, tools, and jewelry. Avoid strong acids that could attack carbonate-bearing matrix. Generally very stable.
practical_notes.care_notes: Clean with warm water and mild soap. Polish with soft cloth. Unlike jadeite, nephrite treatment is less common but wax coating is occasionally used on rough surfaces.
treatments_or_synthetics: Wax or oil surface treatment to enhance color/luster on carved pieces; generally benign and traditional
Dye treatment to enhance green color; detectable by magnification (dye concentrations in fractures)
No polymer impregnation (less common than in jadeite trade)
Simulants include serpentine ('new jade'), green aventurine, chrysoprase, prehnite
common_confusions: Jadeite (higher SG ~3.3, more vitreous luster, higher RI)
Serpentine / bowenite ('new jade' — lower hardness, lower SG)
practical_notes.market_notes: Top-quality Hetian white nephrite (mutton-fat jade) commands extraordinary prices in Chinese markets — large unblemished white pieces can exceed jadeite in value to collectors. New Zealand pounamu has protected status and cultural significance. Canadian nephrite is abundant and forms the basis of a large commercial market.

prompt_fragments: waxy spinach-green nephrite with fibrous felt texture
mutton-fat white jade soft luminosity
pounamu greenstone sacred Pacific stone
ancient jade carving millennia of tradition

provenance.notes: Mindat ID 2878 is for tremolite, the Mg end-member of nephrite's solid solution. Nephrite itself lacks a single IMA mineral species designation as it is a rock material. Cultural history for China and Māori/New Zealand is verified. The Ngāi Tahu status of pounamu under New Zealand law is accurate.

chemical_profile.ideal_formula: SiO2 (approximately 70–75%) with Al2O3, FeO, Fe2O3, MgO, CaO, K2O, Na2O
structural_units: Disordered silicate network — amorphous (no long-range order)
chemical_profile.atomic_structure_notes: Volcanic glass — a supercooled liquid with no crystalline structure. Chemically equivalent to rhyolite or high-silica granite compositions, quenched so rapidly that crystals had no time to form. The SiO2 content is typically 70–75%. Geochemical fingerprinting (trace elements) allows obsidian to be sourced to specific volcanic flows.
chemical_profile.chemistry_notes: Color and optical effects depend on Fe content, gas bubble distribution, and crystallite inclusions. Black obsidian is colored by iron and magnetite. Snowflake obsidian contains spherulites of cristobalite (white). Rainbow obsidian gets its iridescence from nanolaminate magnetite layers. Mahogany obsidian has reddish-brown bands from Fe oxidation.

crystallography.crystallography_notes: No crystalline structure — volcanic glass. Conchoidal fracture with razor-sharp edges is the defining physical feature. Some specimens contain phenocrysts of quartz, feldspar, or pyroxene (not truly porphyritic obsidian — these are xenocrysts). Spherulitic devitrification (cristobalite/feldspar) creates snowflake patterns.

physical_properties.physical_notes: Perfect conchoidal fracture produces razor-sharp edges — among the sharpest natural edges possible. Obsidian blades have been used in surgery and approach monoatomic-edge sharpness, far exceeding surgical steel in edge acuity. Lower hardness than quartz but forms sharper edges due to amorphous structure.

color_profiles.name: Classic Black Obsidian
color_profiles.cause: Iron and titanium oxides (magnetite) distributed throughout glass matrix.
color_profiles.conditions: Most common obsidian; widespread in all volcanic obsidian flows.

color_profiles.name: Rainbow Obsidian
colors: black with rainbow iridescence bands — green
color_profiles.cause: Thin layers of oriented magnetite nanoparticles creating thin-film interference.
color_profiles.conditions: Specific flows — notably Napa Valley, California; Mexico.

color_profiles.name: Snowflake Obsidian
color_profiles.cause: Devitrification spherulites of cristobalite (silica) and feldspar crystallizing post-emplacement.
color_profiles.conditions: Common; widespread in many obsidian flows.

color_profiles.name: Mahogany Obsidian
color_profiles.cause: Iron oxide-rich zones (hematite/goethite) banding through black glass.
color_profiles.conditions: Many localities; common.

color_profiles.name: Apache Tears
color_profiles.cause: Small rounded nodules of obsidian that weather from perlite matrix; translucent in thin section.
color_profiles.conditions: Arizona, Nevada — perlitic volcanic rocks.

appearance.visual_notes: Obsidian is pure volcanic glass — the most visually simple and the most dramatically effective black mineral material. Its jet-black vitreous surface reflects light cleanly. Rainbow varieties produce full spectral color from thin-film magnetite layers. Apache tears appear opaque but transmit brownish light when held up.

optical_and_special_effects.fluorescence: None typically
optical_and_special_effects.chatoyancy: Sheen obsidian — silver or gold sheen from parallel nanoparticle layers
optical_and_special_effects.iridescence: Rainbow obsidian — full spectral iridescence from nanolaminate magnetite interference layers
optical_and_special_effects.optical_notes: RI approximately 1.48–1.51. Isotropic (amorphous glass). Relatively low RI compared to crystalline minerals. Flow banding and bubble trains sometimes visible internally. Conchoidal fracture surfaces show characteristic curved ridges.

geologic_environments: obsidian cliffs and flows in silicic volcanic terrain
notable_localities: Obsidian Cliff, Yellowstone, Wyoming, USA (major Ancestral Puebloan source)
Glass Buttes, Lake County, Oregon, USA
Pachuca and Teotihuacan sources, Hidalgo/Mexico State, Mexico
Jalisco and Michoacán, Mexico (Aztec workshop sources)
Lipari Island, Sicily, Italy (ancient Mediterranean trade source)
Mount Ararat and Nemrut Dağ, Turkey (Neolithic obsidian trade)
Ethiopia, Kenya (East African Rift — important Acheulean and Later Stone Age sources)
formation_and_occurrence.occurrence_notes: Forms in continental and island arc volcanic settings where high-silica magma erupts and cools extremely rapidly — faster than crystallization can occur. Obsidian flows are geologically young (most < 700,000 years — older obsidian devitrifies to crystalline materials). Obsidian sourcing by trace element fingerprinting has revolutionized archaeology — distribution maps reveal ancient trade networks.

historical_uses: Stone tools — blades, knives, scrapers (Lower Paleolithic to historically in some regions)
Surgical blades (modern surgical use — some cardiothoracic surgeons prefer obsidian scalpels)
Mirrors (Aztec; polished obsidian mirrors for divination)
Trade commodity (obsidian exchange networks documented from Neolithic through Bronze Age)
Ritual and ceremonial objects (Aztec, Mesoamerican, and many other cultures)
Arrowheads and projectile points
cultural_associations: Aztec obsidian mirrors — 'Tezcatlipoca' (smoking mirror) — a deity and divination tool
Obsidian blade craft was among the highest artisanal skills in Mesoamerica
Neolithic obsidian trade — Anatolian obsidian reached Egypt and Cyprus
Apache tears — legend of Apache warriors who died in battle; tears of their wives turned to stone
Japanese and Polynesian tool-making traditions
Mexican folk art and lapidary tradition (obsidian carvings of skulls, animals)
cultural_and_historical.folklore_notes: Tezcatlipoca, the Aztec god of night, sorcery, and the north, is symbolized by a black obsidian smoking mirror — his attribute and the means by which he could see all things in the universe including human sin and fate. Obsidian mirrors were used by priests for scrying and divination. The Apache tears legend holds that women of an Apache band wept when their warriors were killed in battle; their tears fell and crystallized as obsidian nodules. In modern metaphysical tradition, obsidian is a 'truth stone' associated with shadow work.

interpretive_vibe.vibe_notes: Obsidian is the void made solid — a black mirror that reflects what you put in front of it. It is simultaneously the sharpest natural material and the darkest. Tezcatlipoca's smoking mirror sees all. Apache tears carry grief transformed into translucent darkness.

practical_notes.toxicity: Non-toxic in handled form. Sharp fractured edges are a serious laceration hazard — handle broken pieces with extreme care.
practical_notes.handling_notes: Conchoidal fracture creates razor-sharp edges that can cause severe cuts. Store with edges protected. Handle knapped pieces as you would a surgical blade. Polish reduces hazard.
practical_notes.care_notes: Wipe with soft cloth. Avoid impacts — brittle and fractures easily. Sharp edges on raw material are a handling hazard.
treatments_or_synthetics: No treatments common for natural obsidian
Glass simulants (black glass, jet glass) are widespread but heavier and with different RI
Some 'obsidian' sold commercially is slag glass or black glass (volcanic glass without natural origin)
common_confusions: Jet (lignite variety — very soft; carving material)
Basalt (crystalline rock; no conchoidal fracture; different texture)
Slag glass (industrial waste glass — similar appearance)
practical_notes.market_notes: Common and affordable as raw material. Carved Mexican obsidian objects (skulls, animals) widely available. Rainbow and sheen obsidian cabochons are popular. Apache tears common in the market. Lapidary-quality material inexpensive.

prompt_fragments: jet-black volcanic glass with conchoidal fracture — razor edge from deep time
rainbow obsidian — full spectral iridescence on absolute black surface
Aztec smoking mirror: Tezcatlipoca sees all in volcanic glass

provenance.notes: Obsidian is a mineraloid volcanic glass — not a true mineral. No fixed composition.

structural_units: Amorphous (opal-A) or paracrystalline silica (opal-CT, opal-C) with water
In precious opal: close-packed spheres of amorphous SiO2 ~150–400 nm diameter
chemical_profile.atomic_structure_notes: Not truly crystalline — opal is amorphous (opal-A) to paracrystalline (opal-CT) hydrated silica. Water content varies from <1% to >21% by weight. In precious (play-of-color) opal, submicroscopic SiO2 spheres are uniformly stacked in a face-centered cubic or hexagonal close-packed array, creating a diffraction grating that diffracts white light into spectral colors.
chemical_profile.chemistry_notes: Water is structurally important — dehydration causes crazing (cracking). Water content affects SG (1.98–2.25) and stability. Fire opal (orange body color) differs in having fewer structural water molecules and often comes from volcanic rhyolite. Opal-CT (cristobalite-tridymite) is the most common variety in sedimentary environments.

crystallography.crystallography_notes: No crystalline structure — amorphous mineraloid. The play-of-color (opalescence) is a diffraction phenomenon from ordered spherical domains, not true crystallographic property. Precious opal has sphere diameters of approximately 150–400 nm arranged in regular arrays.

physical_properties.tenacity: brittle; can craze or crack with dehydration
physical_properties.physical_notes: Very low SG (1.98–2.25) compared to most gemstones — unusually lightweight. Highly sensitive to dehydration — can crack or craze when dried rapidly. Heat, low humidity, and rapid temperature changes all risk stability. Water content varies significantly by type and affects all properties.

color_profiles.name: White Precious Opal
color_profiles.cause: White to light-gray body color from common opal base; play-of-color from sphere diffraction.
color_profiles.conditions: Lightning Ridge, Australia; Coober Pedy, Australia.

color_profiles.name: Black Precious Opal
colors: dark-blue body with vivid spectral play-of-color
color_profiles.cause: Dark body color (from carbon or iron oxide traces) intensifies play-of-color contrast.
color_profiles.conditions: Lightning Ridge, New South Wales, Australia — highest valued precious opal.

color_profiles.name: Crystal Opal
colors: transparent to semi-transparent with spectral fire
color_profiles.cause: Pure to near-pure silica spheres with no body color obscuring transparency.
color_profiles.conditions: Coober Pedy; other Australian fields.

color_profiles.name: Boulder Opal
colors: brown ironstone matrix with opal veins showing spectral color
color_profiles.cause: Thin seams of precious opal in ironstone host; cut with matrix.
color_profiles.conditions: Queensland, Australia.

color_profiles.name: Common Opal (Potch)
color_profiles.cause: Irregular or absent sphere arrangement; no diffraction grating.
color_profiles.conditions: Worldwide; not gem grade.

appearance.visual_notes: Precious opal's play-of-color is unlike any other optical effect in the mineral world — full spectrum visible in shifting patches as the viewing angle changes. The diffraction grating created by ordered spheres splits white light into all visible wavelengths simultaneously.

optical_and_special_effects.fluorescence: Variable; many specimens fluoresce white, green, yellow, or blue-white under UV; some inert
optical_and_special_effects.iridescence: Play-of-color is a diffraction phenomenon (not thin-film iridescence); produces spectral flashes across the full visible range
optical_and_special_effects.optical_notes: RI approximately 1.37–1.47 (lower than most gemstones). Isotropic (amorphous). The play-of-color is caused by Bragg diffraction of white light from periodic stacked silica sphere arrays — different sphere diameters produce different dominant colors: ~150 nm = violet/blue, ~200 nm = green, ~250–400 nm = yellow/orange/red.

genesis: secondary (replacing organic matter — opalized wood, shells)
geologic_environments: sedimentary sequences (Cretaceous inland sea beds in Australia)
volcanic rhyolite and ignimbrite (fire opal, Mexico)
notable_localities: Lightning Ridge, New South Wales, Australia (world's finest black precious opal)
Coober Pedy, South Australia (world's largest opal-producing region)
Ethiopia (Welo/Wollo — recent high-quality hydrophane precious opal)
Slovakia (Červenica — oldest European precious opal mine)
formation_and_occurrence.occurrence_notes: Australian opals form in Cretaceous sedimentary sequences from silica-rich groundwater percolating through sandy rock. Ethiopian opal (hydrophane) forms in volcanic environments. Mexican fire opal comes from rhyolitic volcanics. Over 90% of world precious opal production has historically come from Australia.

birthstone_status: October birthstone (traditional and modern, alongside tourmaline)
historical_uses: Gemstone — prized since Roman antiquity
Talisman and amulet across ancient Europe and the Near East
Symbol of hope, luck, and prophecy in medieval Europe
Australian Aboriginal sacred stone in some traditions
cultural_associations: Romans called it 'opalus' — highest status gemstone, combining all other gem colors
Pliny the Elder described opal as 'ruby fire, amethyst purple, emerald sea-green — all together'
Medieval Europeans considered opal a lucky stone of prophecy and protection
Sir Walter Scott's novel 'Anne of Geierstein' (1829) recast opal as a bad-luck stone — directly crashing the European opal market for decades
Australia — Lightning Ridge opal shaped national identity and economy
Ethiopian and Australian Aboriginal communities have oral traditions about opal formation
cultural_and_historical.folklore_notes: Opal has the most dramatic reputation reversal in gem history. Ancient Romans paid extravagant sums — Pliny wrote that a Roman senator was exiled rather than sell his opal to Mark Antony. Medieval tradition held opal lucky — a stone containing all gem colors in one. Sir Walter Scott's 1829 novel Anne of Geierstein featured an opal that was destroyed by holy water — and Victorian superstition about opal bad luck was born. The European market collapsed for 50 years. The superstition persists despite being entirely literary in origin.

interpretive_vibe.vibe_notes: Opal is the mineral of all light at once. Where diamond bends light and ruby absorbs it, opal diffracts it — splitting white into every visible wavelength simultaneously across a surface that seems to hold living fire inside it.

practical_notes.toxicity: Non-toxic. Crystalline silica hazard not applicable to amorphous opal.
practical_notes.handling_notes: Fragile and dehydration-sensitive. Avoid extreme temperature changes, direct sunlight, low humidity, and heat. Do not leave in dry display cases without moisture control for extended periods.
practical_notes.care_notes: Store in a cloth pouch with a damp cotton ball for humidity maintenance. Avoid ultrasonic cleaners, steam, and harsh chemicals. Clean with barely damp soft cloth. Avoid acids. Opal doublets and triplets need extra care around glue lines.
treatments_or_synthetics: Opal doublets — thin slice of precious opal cemented to ironstone or black potch base
Opal triplets — opal doublet with glass or quartz dome cover
Impregnation with smoke or plastic to darken background (common in boulder opal)
Synthetic precious opal (Gilson opal, Kyocera opal) — uniform play-of-color; lizard-skin pattern under magnification distinguishes
Glass and plastic simulants common in costume jewelry
common_confusions: Labradorite (feldspar — schiller is reflection, not diffraction; different structure)
Glass opal (uniform bubble patterns under magnification)
Synthetic opal (Gilson — columnar 'lizard skin' pattern at magnification)
Rainbow moonstone (labradorite variety — blue adularescence, not full spectral fire)
practical_notes.market_notes: Precious opal is highly variable in value. Black opal from Lightning Ridge is among the most expensive gemstones per carat at top quality. Crystal and white opals more accessible. Boulder opal more affordable. Ethiopian (Welo) opal has disrupted markets since 2008 discovery. Doublets and triplets are legitimate but must be disclosed. Synthetic opal mass-market.

prompt_fragments: black opal from Lightning Ridge — volcanic spectral fire on dark sea
white precious opal with rolling green-blue spectral patches
boulder opal in ironstone matrix — veins of spectral light

other_ids.note: Not a mineral species; no Mindat mineral ID assigned

chemical_profile.ideal_formula: SiO2 (replacing original wood cellulose and lignin)
structural_units: SiO4 tetrahedra in chalcedony (cryptocrystalline quartz) or macrocrystalline quartz framework
Original wood cell walls replaced molecule by molecule
chemical_profile.atomic_structure_notes: Not a mineral; a biogenic silicified fossil. The replacing silica is typically chalcedony (cryptocrystalline quartz), opal-CT, or macrocrystalline quartz. Silicification occurs when silica-rich groundwater percolates through buried wood, precipitating SiO2 while the organic material dissolves — permineralization preserves the macroscopic cell structure (rings, vessels, rays) in exquisite detail.
chemical_profile.chemistry_notes: Color is entirely controlled by trace element impurities in the replacing silica: Fe2O3 (hematite) gives red/orange/brown; Fe(OH)3 (limonite/goethite) gives yellow; MnO2 gives black; chromium gives green; copper gives blue-green. The original wood provides no surviving chemical signal — only its structural ghost remains.

crystallography.crystal_class: cryptocrystalline to macrocrystalline — not applicable at macro scale
crystallography.crystallography_notes: At the microscopic scale, petrified wood consists of chalcedony (fibrous or granular microcrystalline quartz), sometimes with drusy macrocrystalline quartz in voids. At the macro scale, the wood grain, annual rings, bark texture, and cellular anatomy are often perfectly preserved. This structural duality — crystalline mineral at the micro scale, biological form at the macro scale — is its defining character.

physical_properties.physical_notes: Fully silicified specimens approach the hardness of chalcedony (~6.5–7) and are very durable. Partially replaced specimens may be softer and more porous. The preservation of wood structure sometimes creates planes of weakness along grain boundaries.

color_profiles.name: red-orange-petrified-wood
color_profiles.cause: Fe2O3 (hematite) in the replacing chalcedony; oxidizing conditions during silicification.
color_profiles.conditions: Common in Triassic-age material (e.g., Arizona); oxidizing burial environments.

color_profiles.name: yellow-brown-petrified-wood
color_profiles.cause: FeO(OH) goethite/limonite; partially hydrated iron oxides.
color_profiles.conditions: Widespread; most common color range.

color_profiles.name: black-petrified-wood
color_profiles.cause: MnO2 (pyrolusite) or carbonaceous material preserved in low-oxygen conditions.
color_profiles.conditions: Anaerobic burial environments; some Eocene and Cretaceous localities.

color_profiles.name: gray-petrified-wood
color_profiles.cause: Low trace element content in pure silica replacement; minimal chromophores.
color_profiles.conditions: Clean silica-rich groundwaters; some Pacific Northwest material.

color_profiles.name: blue-green-petrified-wood
color_profiles.cause: Cu or Cr in the replacing silica.
color_profiles.conditions: Rare; specific localities with Cu-mineralized groundwaters.

color_profiles.name: agatized-wood
color_profiles.cause: Multiple silica precipitation events with varying trace element concentrations, producing banding analogous to agate.
color_profiles.conditions: Slow, multi-episode silicification in a fluctuating geochemical environment.

appearance.visual_notes: The preserved wood grain and annual rings are the defining visual feature — the stone tells a biological story. Cross-sections are among the most visually striking mineral-art objects, showing perfect tree anatomy in glowing chalcedony. Color zonation often follows the original wood structure.

optical_and_special_effects.fluorescence: Variable; some material fluoresces green or yellow under UV (chalcedony component)
optical_and_special_effects.chatoyancy: Rare; some fibrous chalcedony replacements show weak chatoyancy
optical_and_special_effects.optical_notes: Optically equivalent to chalcedony: RI ~1.53–1.54, uniaxial positive (fibrous chalcedony), birefringence low. The biological macro-structure is not an optical effect but a structural one.

geologic_environments: volcanic ash-rich fluvial and lacustrine deposits
floodplain and swamp deposits buried under ash falls
notable_localities: Petrified Forest National Park, Arizona, USA (Triassic Chinle Formation; Araucarioxylon arizonicum)
Blue Forest, Eden Valley, Wyoming, USA (Eocene; standing fossil stumps)
Ginkgo Petrified Forest, Washington State, USA (Miocene; Ginkgo and many species)
Lesvos Petrified Forest, Greece (Miocene; UNESCO GeoPark)
Florissant Fossil Beds, Colorado, USA (Eocene stumps)
Eastern Cape, South Africa (Glossopteris-era Permian logs)
Yellowstone National Park, Wyoming, USA (standing petrified forests on Specimen Ridge)
formation_and_occurrence.occurrence_notes: Petrified wood forms best when wood is rapidly buried (reducing microbial decay) in silica-rich environments, particularly where volcanic ash is dissolving in groundwater or where hydrothermal silica is present. Silicification can occur over thousands to millions of years. Permineralization preserves cell walls while silica fills cell lumens; in complete replacement, all organic material is gone.

historical_uses: Indigenous peoples of the American Southwest used petrified wood for tools (scrapers, projectile points) due to its hardness and conchoidal fracture — treated identically to chert
Navajo, Hopi, and other Southwestern nations incorporated petrified wood into cultural practices; the Petrified Forest area was known and visited for thousands of years before European contact

Petrified Forest National Park (established 1906) was one of the first US federal land protections specifically for paleontological/geological resources, partly because collectors were removing massive quantities of material
Egyptian petrified forest near Cairo (Oligocene age) was known to ancient Egyptians
Lesvos, Greece: the petrified forest has been known since antiquity; Theophrastus may have referenced it
cultural_associations: Popular in mid-20th century American Southwest tourism and 'roadside geology' culture — petrified wood bookends, clocks, and tabletops
Symbol of geological deep time and the connection between biology and mineralogy
cultural_and_historical.folklore_notes: Many Indigenous traditions of the American Southwest regard petrified wood as sacred — the bones of ancient beings or the material remains of primordial forests. The Navajo creation narrative describes a primordial world with vast forests; the scattered logs of the Petrified Forest are explained in some accounts as the remains of a giant's arrow shafts.

interpretive_vibe.vibe_notes: Petrified wood is the most legible deep-time object — a tree that became a stone, grain by grain, over millions of years, while preserving every ring and vessel. It is the bridge between biology and mineralogy, and one of the most emotionally resonant materials in the natural world.

practical_notes.toxicity: low
practical_notes.handling_notes: Durable once fully silicified; treat like chalcedony. Partially replaced specimens may be fragile along original grain planes.
practical_notes.care_notes: Clean with warm water and mild soap. Polished slabs are very stable. Avoid acids on calcite-containing matrix. Do not collect from protected sites (Petrified Forest NP — removal is illegal).
treatments_or_synthetics: Wax or resin impregnation of porous incompletely silicified material
Surface dyeing to enhance color (uncommon but known)
Reconstructed or artificial 'petrified wood' does not exist commercially at scale
common_confusions: Carved wood-grain stone (will not show actual cell anatomy under magnification)
Opalized wood (opal replacement rather than chalcedony)
practical_notes.market_notes: Common material; affordable in most forms. Exceptional specimens with vivid color, complete logs, or rare species preservation command premium prices. Sawn and polished cross-sections are widely sold as decorative items.

prompt_fragments: petrified wood cross-section showing ancient tree rings in chalcedony
rust-red Triassic log silicified in Arizona desert
agatized wood banded multicolor fossil
deep time mineral wood grain preserved in stone

provenance.notes: Petrified wood is not a mineral species; it is a fossil/gem material. No IMA or Mindat mineral ID exists. Mindat has entries for specific named fossil wood types. All geological and formation information is accurate.

chemical_profile.atomic_structure_notes: Isometric structure with Fe in octahedral sites and S2 pairs oriented along <111> directions. The S2 dumbbell pairs (not individual S2- ions) are the unique structural feature distinguishing pyrite from marcasite (same composition, different structure). The d-electron band gap in pyrite is responsible for its semiconductor properties.
chemical_profile.chemistry_notes: Pyrite readily incorporates Au (often in solid solution — 'invisible gold' or submicroscopic inclusions), making it an important ore of gold. As and Co substitution common. Oxidizes readily in moist air (sulfuric acid + iron oxide) — 'acid mine drainage' problem. Famous for 'fool's gold' confusion with native gold.

crystallography.twinning: Iron cross twins (two interpenetrating pyritohedra at 90°) — diagnostic and spectacular
crystallography.crystallography_notes: Classic habit is the cube with striated faces (striations from pyritohedral oscillation). Pyritohedron (12-faced form with pentagonal faces) is distinctive and common. Octahedral and combinational forms also occur. Iron cross twins are among mineralogy's most recognizable specimens. 'Pyrite suns' or 'dollar pyrite' — flat discoidal masses in coal — are a unique habit.

physical_properties.physical_notes: Harder than gold (Au Mohs 2.5–3) — scratching with fingernail or coin distinguishes pyrite from gold immediately. Greenish-black streak vs. gold's golden streak is definitive. Much harder and more brittle than gold — gold is malleable, pyrite is brittle. High SG. Distinctive brassy-yellow color without the warmth of true gold.

color_profiles.name: Classic Brassy Pyrite
color_profiles.cause: Metallic luster from d-band electronic structure of iron sulfide; brass-yellow reflectance.
color_profiles.conditions: All pyrite specimens; the baseline color.

color_profiles.name: Iridescent Tarnished Pyrite
color_profiles.cause: Thin-film iron oxide/hydroxide layer from surface oxidation (FeS2 → Fe oxide + H2SO4).
color_profiles.conditions: Surface-oxidized or weathered specimens.

color_profiles.name: Pyrite Sun / Dollar Pyrite
colors: flat disc with radiating crystal striations
color_profiles.cause: Radial crystal growth in confined space (coal seam cleavage planes).
color_profiles.conditions: Illinois coal mines — a unique flat discoidal habit.

appearance.visual_notes: Pyrite is mineralogy's great impersonator — 'fool's gold' for millennia. The striated faces of pyrite cubes (from oscillation between cube and pyritohedron) are diagnostic. Iron cross twins — two interlocking pyritohedra — are among mineralogy's most architecturally perfect specimens. Pyrite suns (flat radiating discs) are unique and immediately recognizable forms.

optical_and_special_effects.fluorescence: None
optical_and_special_effects.optical_notes: Opaque metallic mineral. No optical transmission effects. High reflectivity from metallic luster.

notable_localities: Navajún, La Rioja, Spain (world-famous perfect cubic crystals — benchmark specimens)
Amelia Courthouse, Amelia County, Virginia, USA (pyritohedra)
Quiruvilca, La Libertad, Peru (pyritohedra and iron crosses)
Carlin Trend, Nevada, USA (disseminated gold in pyrite)
formation_and_occurrence.occurrence_notes: Pyrite is the most abundant sulfide mineral — virtually ubiquitous in reducing sedimentary environments, hydrothermal systems, and ore deposits. Of enormous economic importance as an indicator mineral and host for invisible gold (Carlin-type deposits). Navajún, Spain produces some of the most perfect natural cubic crystals of any mineral species — perfect geometric cubes with minimal imperfections.

historical_uses: Fire-starting (iron pyrite struck against flint)
Source of sulfur dioxide for wine preservation (ancient)
Production of sulfuric acid
Gold ore processing (Carlin-type invisible gold)
'Fool's gold' — cause of countless mining frauds and prospecting mistakes
Marcellus Shale energy extraction
Jewelry and inlay (pre-Columbian Peru, Aztec)
Victorian 'cut steel' jewelry — pyrite as substitute
cultural_associations: 'Fool's gold' — ubiquitous cultural metaphor for deceptive appearance
Inca and pre-Columbian use of pyrite mirrors for divination (alongside obsidian)
Named from Greek 'pyr' (fire) — fire-starting use
Carlin Trend discovery (1965, Nevada) — invisible gold in pyrite revolutionized mining
Captain Martin Frobisher's 1576 expedition: brought 200 tons of pyrite from Baffin Island mistaken for gold
cultural_and_historical.folklore_notes: Pyrite has been 'fool's gold' since antiquity — its brass-yellow metallic luster has deceived prospectors, miners, and explorers throughout history. Captain Martin Frobisher returned from his 1576 Arctic expedition with 200 tonnes of 'ore' that turned out to be pyrite — the English Crown had funded the expedition for nothing. Spanish conquistadors in the Americas repeatedly confused pyrite with gold. In pre-Columbian Peru, pyrite was polished into flat circular mirrors for divination, alongside obsidian. The name comes from Greek 'pyr' (fire) because sparks fly when it is struck against iron or flint.

interpretive_vibe.vibe_notes: Pyrite is the great deceiver — the mineral that convinced explorers they'd found gold and sent fleets home to disgrace. But a perfect Navajún pyrite cube is not deception — it is geometric perfection in the service of iron sulfide chemistry. Iron cross twins are architecture. Pyrite suns are geology's mandalas.

practical_notes.toxicity: Pyrite oxidation in air and moisture produces sulfuric acid ('pyrite disease' or pyrite decay in museum specimens). Arsenal-bearing pyrite can release arsenic. Do not inhale pyrite dust. Handle normally; wash hands after.
practical_notes.handling_notes: Some specimens are unstable and decay over time — especially framboidal and fine-grained pyrite in clay/shale matrix. Stable massive and crystalline specimens are fine. 'Pyrite disease' (chemical degradation releasing sulfuric acid) is a museum conservation issue for certain matrix specimens.
practical_notes.care_notes: Store in low humidity to slow oxidation. Do not expose to moisture over long periods. Clean with dry cloth. Do not use water on unstable matrix specimens.
treatments_or_synthetics: No gem treatments relevant
Some pyrite specimens coated with lacquer for preservation
common_confusions: Gold (much softer; malleable; golden streak; no striations; different density feel)
Chalcopyrite (brass-yellow but iridescent; lower hardness; different streak)
Marcasite (same formula but orthorhombic; different habit and stability)
Arsenopyrite (similar luster but paler; distinct silver-white tint)
practical_notes.market_notes: Abundant and affordable. Navajún cubic crystals command collector premiums for perfect examples. Iron cross twins are prized specimens. Pyrite suns (Illinois) are popular and moderately priced. Generally inexpensive for common material.
