iron cross twin: two pyritohedra intersecting at 90° — mineralogy's signature form
pyrite sun: flat radiating disc of brassy crystals from Illinois coal seam

structural_units: SiO4 tetrahedra linked into a 3D framework
chemical_profile.atomic_structure_notes: Framework silicate / network silicate.
chemical_profile.chemistry_notes: Extremely common silica mineral with many varieties.

crystallography.twinning: Commonly noted in several laws including Dauphine, Brazil, and Japan.
crystallography.crystallography_notes: Often appears as six-sided prisms with pyramidal terminations.

physical_properties.physical_notes: Hard, common, and very durable.

color_profiles.name: rock-crystal
color_profiles.cause: Low impurity content / near-ideal composition.
color_profiles.conditions: Pure quartz.

color_profiles.name: smoky
color_profiles.cause: Color centers associated with irradiation and trace impurities.
color_profiles.conditions: Often linked to irradiated quartz with impurity-related defects.

color_profiles.name: rose
color_profiles.cause: Variety-specific color mechanism; often treated as inclusion/defect related.
color_profiles.conditions: Commonly discussed in pegmatitic or hydrothermal contexts.

color_profiles.name: amethyst-spectrum
color_profiles.cause: Impurity / defect-driven color.
color_profiles.conditions: Variety-specific.

appearance.visual_notes: One of the most visually flexible minerals on earth.

optical_and_special_effects.optical_notes: Some varieties or included forms show special effects.

formation_and_occurrence.occurrence_notes: Occurs across enormous geologic range and is one of the most common crustal minerals.

interpretive_vibe.vibe_notes: Useful as a baseline mineral, a structure mineral, and a palette-shifting species.

practical_notes.toxicity: low
practical_notes.handling_notes: Generally durable, but edges and points chip.
practical_notes.care_notes: Avoid rough impact.
practical_notes.market_notes: Huge variety market.

prompt_fragments: glassy trigonal crystal logic
clear silica prisms
drusy quartz glitter

provenance.notes: Starter example entry.

chemical_profile.atomic_structure_notes: Isostructural with calcite (trigonal, R-3c). Mn2+ occupies the Ca2+ site; the Jahn-Teller distortion of Mn2+ is mild compared to Cu2+. Forms continuous solid solutions toward calcite (manganoan calcite / kutnahorite) and siderite.
chemical_profile.chemistry_notes: The pink-to-red color is intrinsic to Mn2+ in the carbonate structure. Fe substitution darkens toward brown; Ca substitution dilutes the pink toward pale cream. Pure endmember is vivid raspberry-red; natural specimens with Fe/Ca show orange, peach, or pale-pink banding.

crystallography.twinning: Contact twins on {01-12} occasionally reported
crystallography.crystallography_notes: Crystals typically rhombohedral with curved faces (saddle-shaped or cobbled appearance), or curved scalenohedral. Massive and banded (layered) forms are classic; stalactitic, botryoidal, and cleavage rhombs also occur. Crystal faces often display striated or curved surfaces.

physical_properties.physical_notes: Similar hardness and cleavage to calcite but denser (higher SG due to Mn). Reacts with warm dilute HCl but less vigorously than calcite in cold acid. Rhombohedral cleavage and characteristic pink color are definitive for hand-specimen ID.

color_profiles.name: Raspberry / Crystal Rhodochrosite
color_profiles.cause: High Mn content with minimal Fe/Ca dilution; Mn2+ d-d transition.
color_profiles.conditions: Well-crystallized specimens from Sweet Home Mine and Catamarca; transparent gem-quality material.

color_profiles.name: Inca Rose (Banded Stalactitic)
color_profiles.cause: Alternating layers with varying Mn/Ca/Fe ratios; banding reflects changing fluid chemistry.
color_profiles.conditions: Stalactitic or stalagmitic forms from Argentina; polished slabs show concentric pink-white banding.

color_profiles.name: Orange-Brown Rhodochrosite
color_profiles.cause: Higher Fe substitution (toward siderite) diluting and warming the pink.
color_profiles.conditions: Common in many hydrothermal deposits.

appearance.visual_notes: The banded 'Inca Rose' stalactitic form from Argentina — polished to reveal concentric pink-and-white rings — is one of the most recognized ornamental stone patterns in the gem world. Crystallized raspberry-red specimens from Colorado are among the most sought-after collector mineral specimens globally.

optical_and_special_effects.fluorescence: Weak; some specimens show pale pink or orange fluorescence under UV
optical_and_special_effects.optical_notes: Uniaxial negative. RI approximately 1.578–1.816 (birefringence ~0.22 — very high like other calcite-group carbonates). nω = 1.816, nε = 1.578.

host_rocks: ore veins in various host rocks
notable_localities: Sweet Home Mine, Park County, Colorado, USA (world's finest transparent red crystals)
Catamarca Province, Argentina (Inca Rose stalactitic material)
San Luis, Argentina (additional Inca Rose deposits)
N'Chwaning Mine, Northern Cape, South Africa (vivid pink crystals)
Alma district, Colorado, USA (historic polymetallic veins)
formation_and_occurrence.occurrence_notes: Requires Mn-rich hydrothermal fluids. Common in the oxidized portions and high-temperature veins of silver-lead-zinc polymetallic sulfide deposits. The stalactitic/stalagmitic 'Inca Rose' variety forms in open cavities within mines. Argentina is the largest source of ornamental material; Colorado Sweet Home Mine produces the finest gem crystals.

historical_uses: Ornamental stone (Inca Rose carvings and cabs)
Manganese ore (low grade; not primary source)
Used by Inca and pre-Columbian Andean cultures in ornaments
Gem and lapidary use (faceted gems from transparent material, cabochons from banded)
cultural_associations: 'Inca Rose' — traditional association with Argentine national identity
National gemstone of Argentina since 2002
Colorado state mineral of Colorado, USA
Inca mythology linked the stone to petrified tears of ancient rulers — origin of 'Rosa del Inca'
cultural_and_historical.folklore_notes: Argentine tradition holds that rhodochrosite (Inca Rose) was formed from the blood of ancient Inca kings and queens crystallized over time — giving the stone its name 'Rosa del Inca' (Rose of the Inca). Argentina declared it the national gemstone in 2002. The Sweet Home Mine in Colorado produced specimens that broke world records for collector rhodochrosite.

interpretive_vibe.vibe_notes: Rhodochrosite lives between pink and red — the color of living tissue, roses, and sunsets. The Inca Rose banding reads as geological diary — each layer a year of cave drip. The Sweet Home crystals are raspberry-red architectural perfection.

practical_notes.toxicity: Low toxicity; manganese in dust form (prolonged occupational inhalation) is a known neurotoxin (manganism). Normal specimen handling is safe. Do not sand or grind without dust protection.
practical_notes.handling_notes: Soft and brittle with perfect cleavage — handle crystals carefully. Banded ornamental material is durable when polished but avoid impact. Curved crystal faces on fine specimens are irreplaceable.
practical_notes.care_notes: Avoid acids (dissolves). Avoid scratching (easily scratched by quartz, glass). Clean with dry or barely damp soft cloth. Avoid ultrasonic cleaners. Store separately from harder minerals.
treatments_or_synthetics: Stabilized/resin-filled banded material for lapidary use
Synthetic rhodochrosite produced but rarely seen commercially
common_confusions: Rhodonite (MnSiO3 — silicate, harder, pink with black veining)
Rose quartz (much harder; no banding pattern)
Mangano-calcite (paler pink; lower SG; may fluoresce more strongly)
practical_notes.market_notes: Fine transparent crystals (Sweet Home, Colorado) command significant collector prices. Inca Rose banded slabs and cabochons are mainstream gem market. National gemstone of Argentina gives it strong cultural market. Moderate to premium pricing depending on quality.

prompt_fragments: raspberry-red curved rhombohedral crystals on white quartz matrix
Inca Rose banded stalactite — concentric pink and cream rings
warm rose-manganese architecture of Sweet Home Mine

chemical_profile.ideal_formula: SiO2 (with trace Ti, Al, Mn or microscopic inclusions)
chemical_profile.atomic_structure_notes: Trigonal quartz framework. The color mechanism of rose quartz is unusual: unlike most colored quartz varieties (which have point-defect color centers), rose quartz color comes primarily from microscopic inclusions of a pink titanium-bearing mineral (aluminum phosphate hydroxide — dumortierite-related fibers, or possibly bosiite). Some researchers propose color from Al-OH color centers. Rarely forms well-developed crystals (unlike amethyst/citrine); typically massive.
chemical_profile.chemistry_notes: Rose quartz is unusual among quartz varieties in being almost always massive (not crystalline). Well-formed pink quartz crystals (sometimes called 'crystalline rose quartz' or 'pink quartz') may have a different color mechanism (Al-P centers). The fibrous inclusions responsible for asterism in star rose quartz are rutile needles. Color fades on prolonged exposure to direct sunlight.

crystallography.twinning: Brazil-law twinning
crystallography.crystallography_notes: Rose quartz is exceptional among colored quartz varieties in rarely forming well-developed crystals. Almost exclusively massive (no crystal faces). Crystal-clear pink 'rose quartz crystals' sold in the gem market are typically pale amethyst or treated material. True rose quartz is massive, waxy to vitreous, with diffuse internal color.

physical_properties.physical_notes: Typically translucent rather than transparent due to fibrous inclusions causing light scattering (milky quality). Rarely transparent. Chatoyancy and asterism possible when properly oriented. Color fades in prolonged direct sunlight.

color_profiles.name: Classic Rose Quartz
color_profiles.cause: Microscopic pink fibrous inclusion of Ti-bearing Al-phosphate hydroxide mineral distributed through massive quartz; optical scattering.
color_profiles.conditions: Massive pegmatitic rose quartz worldwide.

color_profiles.name: Star Rose Quartz
color_profiles.cause: Rutile needle inclusions in three orientations within quartz produce asterism when cut as cabochon.
color_profiles.conditions: Sri Lanka (Maingkha), Brazil — massive material with oriented rutile silk.

color_profiles.name: Deep Rose / Intense Pink
color_profiles.cause: Higher concentration of color-bearing inclusions.
color_profiles.conditions: Some Madagascar and Brazil material; less common.

appearance.visual_notes: Rose quartz has a characteristic soft, diffuse, milky-pink quality from the light-scattering fibrous inclusions. It is never facet-grade transparent in classic form. The color is gentle and warm — not vivid like ruby or pink tourmaline but consistently soft. Star rose quartz cut as cabochon shows the six-rayed star most vividly under a point light source.

optical_and_special_effects.fluorescence: Weak blue or orange-pink under UV
optical_and_special_effects.asterism: Star rose quartz — six-rayed star from oriented rutile inclusions; best shown in Sri Lankan and Brazilian material
optical_and_special_effects.optical_notes: Uniaxial positive. RI = 1.544–1.553. Typically cloudy/translucent due to inclusions causing light scattering — chatoyancy and asterism arise from these same inclusions. Color fades on prolonged UV/sunlight exposure.

notable_localities: Governador Valadares and Araçuaí, Minas Gerais, Brazil (world's largest source)
formation_and_occurrence.occurrence_notes: Rose quartz is primarily a pegmatite mineral, occurring in large massive bodies. Brazil's Minas Gerais is the world's largest and most important source, supplying most of the global gem and carving market. Madagascar produces some of the finer pink material.

historical_uses: Carved into beads and figurines in ancient Egypt and Rome
Facial masks in ancient Egyptian burials (rose quartz)
Carving material — spheres, eggs, hearts (modern)
cultural_associations: Universal love-stone in modern metaphysical tradition
Egyptian and Assyrian legend: Aphrodite/Venus stone
Rose quartz spheres and hearts are the most recognizable crystal-healing object globally
Associated with Aphrodite and Isis in ancient traditions
cultural_and_historical.folklore_notes: Rose quartz has one of the most robust modern metaphysical followings of any mineral. Ancient Assyrian and Roman cosmetic recipes include rose quartz facial rollers — a practice that has seen a massive modern revival. Egyptian legend held that rose quartz was a gift from Aphrodite (via Eros) to humanity — used to prevent aging. The association with love and the heart is essentially universal in modern crystal culture.

interpretive_vibe.vibe_notes: Rose quartz is mineral warmth — not vivid, not sharp, not crystalline. It diffuses its pink into the whole mass and softens light into rose. It is the texture of a gentle thing, the color of trust and care.

practical_notes.toxicity: Non-toxic.
practical_notes.handling_notes: Color fades with prolonged direct sunlight. Durable for jewelry and display. Common form as massive carvings is very stable.
practical_notes.care_notes: Store away from direct sunlight for extended periods. Clean with warm water and soft cloth. Ultrasonic safe. Avoid acids (quartz is generally acid-resistant but some surface finishes are not).
treatments_or_synthetics: Dyeing: pale rose quartz sometimes dyed deeper pink
Irradiation: can produce more intense pink
Glass simulants: common in low-end market
Synthetic rose quartz: produced but rare commercially
practical_notes.market_notes: One of the most affordable and widely available gem and display minerals. Dominant in the crystal/wellness market for spheres, eggs, hearts, rollers. Large polished slabs for interior design. Very low cost per kilogram. Star rose quartz (Sri Lanka) commands premium.

prompt_fragments: rose quartz massive — diffuse pink warmth in translucent quartz fog
star rose quartz cabochon — six-rayed asterism in milky blush
Venus stone: Brazil pegmatite pink dispersed through massive silica

provenance.notes: Rose quartz is a variety of quartz. mindat_id 3476 is the rose quartz variety listing. Color mechanism is debated — fibrous inclusion model is current consensus.

structural_units: AlO6 octahedra sharing faces and edges in hexagonal close-packed oxygen framework
chemical_profile.atomic_structure_notes: Ruby is corundum (Al2O3) colored by Cr3+ substituting for Al3+ in the octahedral site. Chromium content in gem ruby typically 0.1–3 wt% Cr2O3. Iron impurities alongside chromium produce orangey hues; high Cr with low Fe gives the purest reds.
chemical_profile.chemistry_notes: Ruby grade requires both red color from Cr3+ and sufficient saturation. The boundary between 'ruby' and 'pink sapphire' is a commercial convention — there is no discrete compositional boundary, only increasing Cr concentration produces deeper red/pink. 'Pigeon's blood' refers to specific Burmese ruby with vivid red + slight blue/purple fluorescence.

crystallography.twinning: Contact and penetration twins on {0001} and {10-11} common.
crystallography.crystallography_notes: Typically prismatic, tabular (hexagonal outline), or barrel-shaped crystals. Alluvial rubies are typically rounded, frosted pebbles. Striated basal faces common. Parting on {0001} and {10-11}.

physical_properties.physical_notes: Second hardest natural mineral (Mohs 9). Extremely durable and abrasion-resistant. No true cleavage but parting planes can affect cutting. High refractive index (~1.76–1.77) gives excellent brilliance.

color_profiles.name: Pigeon's Blood Ruby
colors: pure-red with slight blue fluorescent overtone
color_profiles.cause: Cr3+ at optimal concentration (~0.5–1.5% Cr2O3); strong red fluorescence under UV complements natural redness in daylight.
color_profiles.conditions: Mogok Valley, Myanmar — classic marble-hosted ruby environment.

color_profiles.name: Burmese Ruby (general)
color_profiles.cause: Cr3+ chromophore; trace Fe adds depth.
color_profiles.conditions: Mogok Valley, Myanmar.

color_profiles.name: Thai/Cambodian Ruby
color_profiles.cause: Higher Fe content alongside Cr3+ creates darker, less fluorescent red.
color_profiles.conditions: Basaltic deposits — Thailand, Cambodia, Vietnam.

color_profiles.name: Star Ruby
colors: red body with six-rayed white asterism
color_profiles.cause: Silk inclusions (rutile needles oriented along three crystallographic directions) producing asterism when cut as cabochon.
color_profiles.conditions: Myanmar, Sri Lanka, India.

macro_patterns: fingerprint and needled inclusions under magnification
appearance.visual_notes: Ruby is the red variety of corundum — the intensity of Cr3+ fluorescence in daylight gives Burmese rubies an inner glow that red garnets and spinel cannot replicate. In strong sunlight, a fine Burmese ruby appears to emit light. The six-rayed star in star ruby is one of the most captivating optical effects in gemology.

optical_and_special_effects.fluorescence: Strong red fluorescence under UV (LW and SW) — Cr3+ emission; Burmese/marble-hosted rubies fluoresce more strongly than basalt-hosted Thai rubies (which have higher Fe quenching fluorescence)
optical_and_special_effects.phosphorescence: Some Burmese rubies phosphoresce after UV exposure
optical_and_special_effects.asterism: Six-rayed star in star ruby — from intersecting rutile (silk) needle inclusions parallel to three directions in {0001} basal plane
optical_and_special_effects.pleochroism: Dichroic: orange-red / purple-red (strong); orientation critical in faceting for color optimization
optical_and_special_effects.optical_notes: Uniaxial negative. nω ≈ 1.770, nε ≈ 1.762 (RI ~1.762–1.770). Birefringence ~0.008. Strong fluorescence under UV is diagnostic for distinguishing ruby from red spinel, garnet.

notable_localities: Mogok Valley, Sagaing Region, Myanmar (world's finest — marble-hosted pigeon's blood)
Mong Hsu, Shan State, Myanmar (commercial production; requires heat treatment)
Vietnam (Lục Yên and Quỳ Châu — marble-hosted)
formation_and_occurrence.occurrence_notes: Marble-hosted rubies (Myanmar, Vietnam) tend to have low Fe and strong fluorescence — producing the coveted 'pigeon's blood' appearance. Basalt-hosted rubies (Thailand, Cambodia) have higher Fe that quenches fluorescence and deepens/darkens color. Mozambique has become a leading producer since major deposits discovered at Montepuez.

historical_uses: Gemstone in jewelry — highest value among colored stones historically
Set in royal regalia across Asian and European monarchies
Ruby laser (first working laser, Theodore Maiman, 1960 — used synthetic ruby)
Watch bearings and precision instrument components (synthetic ruby)
Mughal and Hindu ritual objects
cultural_associations: 'Ratnaraj' — Sanskrit for 'king of gems'
Burmese warriors believed rubies made them invulnerable in battle — inserted under skin
Hindu texts: ruby at the navel of Vishnu's crown
European medieval belief: ruby darkens when its owner is in danger
Ruby associated with Mars (Roman), divine protection, and the blood of the gods
The Black Prince's Ruby in the British Imperial State Crown — actually red spinel, not ruby
cultural_and_historical.folklore_notes: Ruby is the 'king of gems' — a title given in Sanskrit texts that has persisted across cultures. Burmese warriors inserted rubies under their skin before battle believing it would make them invincible. Medieval European tradition held that a ruby's color deepened to warn its wearer of approaching danger and paled if the wearer was in mortal peril. The famous 'Black Prince's Ruby' in the British Crown Jewels is actually a large red spinel — a misidentification that held for centuries. The first ruby laser (1960) used chromium-doped synthetic corundum, the same physics as natural ruby's red fluorescence.

interpretive_vibe.vibe_notes: Ruby holds the deep-red that human eyes are tuned to desire — the color of blood, roses, and fire condensed into Mohs-9 corundum. In strong daylight, Burmese pigeon's blood ruby appears to glow from within — the chromium fluorescence adding spectral energy that no mere pigment can produce.

practical_notes.toxicity: Non-toxic.
practical_notes.handling_notes: Extremely durable in gem form. Second hardest natural material. Avoid impacts on girdle (parting planes can cleave). Safe for all normal jewelry use.
practical_notes.care_notes: Clean with warm soapy water and brush; ultrasonic generally safe (avoid for heavily fractured or filled stones). Store separately from other gems to avoid mutual scratching.
treatments_or_synthetics: Heat treatment: universal and accepted by the trade; removes silk, improves color and clarity
Fracture filling with lead glass: used for heavily included material; requires disclosure and special care
Beryllium diffusion: produces color alteration; requires disclosure
Flux-grown synthetic ruby: Chatham, Ramaura — identified by flux inclusions under magnification
Verneuil (flame-fusion) synthetic ruby: curved growth lines, gas bubbles
Hydrothermal synthetic ruby: very close to natural; spectroscopic testing required
common_confusions: Red spinel (isometric — no birefringence; no fluorescence at same intensity; The 'Black Prince's Ruby' in British Crown is spinel)
Pyrope garnet (no birefringence; no UV fluorescence)
Synthetic ruby (requires spectroscopic and inclusion analysis)
practical_notes.market_notes: Ruby is the highest-priced colored gemstone per carat globally. Burmese (Mogok) pigeon's blood rubies with no heat treatment command extreme premiums at auction. Mozambique rubies have emerged as a credible high-value alternative. Treatment disclosure is mandatory in the trade. Synthetic rubies are commodity items in industry.

prompt_fragments: pigeon's blood ruby — chromium fire glowing in Mogok marble
star ruby cabochon with six-rayed silk asterism
vivid red fluorescent corundum — blood of gems

provenance.notes: Ruby is a variety of corundum. mindat_id 3486 is the GIA/mindat entry for ruby variety specifically.

chemical_profile.ideal_formula: Al2O3 (with Fe2+, Ti4+, V3+, Cr3+, etc.)
structural_units: AlO6 octahedra sharing faces and edges in hexagonal close-packed oxygen framework
chemical_profile.atomic_structure_notes: Sapphire is corundum (Al2O3) in any color other than red (which is ruby). Blue color from Fe2+↔Ti4+ intervalence charge transfer between adjacent octahedral sites. Yellow from Fe3+ or color centers. Pink from low Cr3+. Padparadscha from Cr3+ combined with Fe3+ centers. Green from Fe with V.
chemical_profile.chemistry_notes: The color diversity of sapphire reflects the range of trace element chromophores: blue (Fe2+/Ti4+ IVCT), yellow (Fe3+), green (Fe2+), pink/purple (Cr3+/V3+), orange-pink padparadscha (Cr3++iron-related). Beryllium diffusion treatment in the 2000s allowed production of vibrant yellow and orange colors by inducing new color centers.

crystallography.twinning: Twins on {0001} and {10-11} common.
crystallography.crystallography_notes: Same structure as all corundum. Typically prismatic, tabular, or barrel-shaped crystals. Alluvial sapphires (Sri Lanka, Australia) are rounded worn pebbles. Some Sri Lankan sapphires reach impressive sizes. Parting on {0001} and {10-11}.

physical_properties.physical_notes: Second hardest natural mineral (Mohs 9) — equal to ruby. Extremely durable gemstone material. High RI (~1.76–1.77) provides excellent brilliance. No cleavage makes it resistant to splitting during wear.

color_profiles.name: Kashmir Blue Sapphire
color_profiles.cause: Fe2+/Ti4+ IVCT; fine silk inclusions scatter light to produce the famous velvety texture.
color_profiles.conditions: Extreme altitude metamorphic deposits in Paddar, Kashmir, India — now largely exhausted.

color_profiles.name: Burmese / Ceylon Sapphire
color_profiles.cause: Fe2+/Ti4+ charge transfer — less silk than Kashmir; brighter.
color_profiles.conditions: Mogok, Myanmar; Sri Lanka (Ratnapura, Elahera).

color_profiles.name: Padparadscha
color_profiles.cause: Combination of Cr3+ (pink) and iron-related (orange) color centers.
color_profiles.conditions: Extremely rare; Sri Lanka (Ratnapura), Madagascar, Tanzania.

color_profiles.name: Yellow Sapphire
color_profiles.cause: Fe3+ substitution or color centers.
color_profiles.conditions: Sri Lanka, Madagascar, Tanzania.

color_profiles.name: Star Sapphire
colors: black body with six-rayed white asterism
color_profiles.cause: Rutile (silk) needle inclusions in three crystallographic orientations producing asterism under cabochon cut.
color_profiles.conditions: Sri Lanka, Myanmar, India.

color_profiles.name: Parti Sapphire
color_profiles.cause: Zoned distribution of different chromophores (Fe, Ti) during crystal growth.
color_profiles.conditions: Australia (Queensland, New South Wales).

appearance.visual_notes: Kashmir sapphire produces a 'velvety' blue that seems to glow from within — caused by fine silk inclusions scattering light. Star sapphire at its best shows a perfectly centered six-rayed star that moves over the cabochon surface. Padparadscha's salmon-pink-orange is unique and defies category — it must be evaluated in person to appreciate.

optical_and_special_effects.fluorescence: Blue sapphires typically inert to faint under UV; some Sri Lankan sapphires show orange fluorescence; yellow sapphires may fluoresce orange
optical_and_special_effects.asterism: Six-rayed star in star sapphire from three sets of rutile needles in basal plane
optical_and_special_effects.pleochroism: Dichroic: blue sapphire shows blue / blue-green or violet-blue; strong; orientation critical in cutting
optical_and_special_effects.optical_notes: Uniaxial negative. nω ≈ 1.770, nε ≈ 1.762. Birefringence ~0.008. Distinctly lower UV fluorescence than ruby distinguishes it. CR 2% RI dispersion contributes to 'fire' in faceted stones.

geologic_environments: alkali basalt flows (large basalt-hosted stones from Australia, Thailand)
notable_localities: Paddar (Kashmir), Jammu & Kashmir, India (finest — now largely exhausted)
Ratnapura and Elahera, Sri Lanka (Ceylon sapphire)
Queensland and New South Wales, Australia (parti sapphire)
Yogo Gulch, Montana, USA (unique vivid blue)
formation_and_occurrence.occurrence_notes: Kashmir sapphire, from high-altitude glacial deposits in the Zanskar range, is the most sought-after and rarest source. Most Kashmir material was recovered before 1930; the deposits are largely exhausted. Sri Lanka (Ceylon) has produced fine sapphire for over 2,000 years and is the most consistent historical source. Australian sapphires tend to be dark and basalt-hosted with less desirable blue-green or inky tones.

historical_uses: Royal jewelry and regalia across all major civilizations
Symbol of divine favor in medieval Christian tradition
Set in papal rings and bishops' rings (sapphire as stone of heaven)
Mughal jewelry (major collector)
Princess Diana/Kate Middleton engagement ring — most famous sapphire in modern history
Engraved sapphires as talismans
Navigation (blue sapphire as 'guiding star' stone)
cultural_associations: The stone of heaven — blue sky color sacred in Christian, Islamic, and Hindu traditions
Kings wore blue sapphire as a stone of wisdom and divine right
Persian belief: the sky is blue because it rests on a giant blue sapphire
Hindu tradition: Shani (Saturn) rules sapphire
The Logan Sapphire (423 ct) and Star of India (563 ct) in Smithsonian/AMNH
Princess Diana's 12-carat oval Ceylon sapphire engagement ring (now Princess of Wales)
cultural_and_historical.folklore_notes: Ancient Persians believed the sky was blue because the Earth rested on a vast sapphire pedestal whose reflection made the sky blue. Medieval European clergy wore sapphire rings as stones of heaven. The name comes from Greek 'sappheiros' (possibly originally referring to lapis lazuli, not sapphire). India's Vedic astrological tradition gives sapphire to Saturn (Shani) — 'Neelam' — with complex prescriptions for when wearing it is beneficial or harmful. The engagement ring tradition of sapphire received a 20th-century revival through the 1981 Prince Charles/Princess Diana ring.

interpretive_vibe.vibe_notes: Sapphire is the blue of the sky trapped in stone — or in Persian belief, the reason the sky is blue. Kashmir sapphire has the quality of depth without darkness, blue without coldness, velvet without softness. The finest stones seem to emit blue light rather than reflect it.

practical_notes.toxicity: Non-toxic.
practical_notes.handling_notes: Extremely durable. Appropriate for rings and daily-wear jewelry. Heat treatment should be disclosed. Avoid cleaning fractured or filled stones in ultrasonic.
practical_notes.care_notes: Clean with warm soapy water and brush; ultrasonic safe for untreated or heat-treated stones only. Avoid steam cleaning for any filled or surface-treated stones. Store separately.
treatments_or_synthetics: Heat treatment: very common and accepted by trade; improves color and clarity
Beryllium diffusion: produces yellow/orange/padparadscha colors; requires spectroscopic testing
Fracture and cavity filling: used on some stones; requires special care
Flux-grown synthetic sapphire (Chatham, etc.)
Verneuil synthetic sapphire (curved striae, gas bubbles)
Hydrothermal synthetic: close to natural; testing required
common_confusions: Blue spinel (isometric — no birefringence; different RI)
Aquamarine (beryl — biaxial; different RI and SG)
Blue topaz (biaxial; different RI and SG)
practical_notes.market_notes: Fine Kashmir sapphire is among the rarest and most valuable gemstones globally (deposits exhausted). Ceylon (Sri Lanka) sapphire is the most important historical-trade material. Heat treatment universal and accepted when disclosed. Beryllium diffusion requires disclosure. Madagascar has risen to major production status. Star sapphires from Sri Lanka remain popular collector and gem items.

prompt_fragments: velvety Kashmir blue — cornflower sky trapped in Mohs-9 corundum
star sapphire cabochon — six-rayed asterism rolling over blue-gray surface
padparadscha: salmon-lotus at the boundary of pink and orange, Sri Lanka

provenance.notes: Sapphire is a variety of corundum. mindat_id 3530 is the sapphire variety listing.

chemical_profile.atomic_structure_notes: FCC metallic structure isostructural with gold and copper.
chemical_profile.chemistry_notes: Naturally alloys with gold (electrum) and mercury (amalgam). Highest electrical and thermal conductivity of any element.

crystallography.twinning: Rare; spinel-law twins occasionally reported.
crystallography.crystallography_notes: Crystals rare; usually forms arborescent, dendritic, or wire masses. Cubic and scalenohedral crystals occasionally reported from hydrothermal veins.

physical_properties.physical_notes: Second most malleable and ductile metal after gold. Tarnishes readily in air due to hydrogen sulfide forming acanthite (Ag2S) coating. Highest electrical and thermal conductivity of any element.

color_profiles.name: Fresh Native Silver
color_profiles.cause: reflective metallic surface of unoxidized silver
color_profiles.conditions: freshly exposed, unoxidized

color_profiles.name: Tarnished Silver
color_profiles.cause: silver sulfide (acanthite) tarnish layer from H2S exposure
color_profiles.conditions: exposure to sulfur compounds in air

appearance.visual_notes: Fresh specimens show brilliant silver-white metallic luster. Tarnish develops complex gray-black patinas. Kongsberg wire-silver specimens are among the most celebrated native metal mineral specimens in existence.

optical_and_special_effects.fluorescence: none
optical_and_special_effects.iridescence: tarnish iridescence common on oxidized surfaces

geologic_environments: secondary enrichment zones above sulfide deposits
formation_and_occurrence.occurrence_notes: Native silver most commonly forms in the upper oxidation and secondary enrichment zones of polymetallic sulfide deposits. Primary hydrothermal occurrences also known.

historical_uses: coinage and currency (ancient Lydian, Greek, and Roman coins through modern)
jewelry and silverware
photography (silver halide emulsions, pre-digital era)
antibacterial applications (colloidal silver, wound dressings)
electrical contacts and conductors
mirrors (silver backing)
industrial catalysts
cultural_associations: lunar symbolism across cultures (Moon = silver, Sun = gold)
purity and cleanliness
werewolf mythology (silver bullet)
alchemy (Luna — the moon metal)
aristocratic tableware and status
cultural_and_historical.folklore_notes: Silver has been mined since at least 3000 BCE in Anatolia. The Laurion mines near Athens funded Athenian naval power. Kongsberg, Norway produced extraordinary arborescent silver specimens prized by collectors since the 17th century. In mythology, silver is the metal of the moon — opposite gold's solar nature. Silver bullets are a recurring folkloric remedy against supernatural evil.

interpretive_vibe.vibe_notes: Silver is gold's lunar twin — cooler, more mysterious, marked by its tendency to darken and tarnish over time into complex gray-black patinas.

practical_notes.toxicity: metallic silver is non-toxic; silver compounds and soluble salts can be toxic; chronic ingestion of colloidal silver causes argyria (permanent blue-gray skin discoloration)
practical_notes.handling_notes: Handle with clean dry hands or cotton gloves. Tarnishes from skin oils and sulfur compounds. Soft and easily scratched.
practical_notes.care_notes: Store in anti-tarnish bags or with silica gel. Clean tarnish with appropriate silver polish or dilute ammonia solution. Avoid rubber contact (accelerates tarnish).
treatments_or_synthetics: rhodium plating to prevent tarnish on jewelry silver
sterling silver (92.5% Ag) is the most common commercial alloy
fine silver (99.9% Ag) also common for bullion and some jewelry
common_confusions: white metals (tin, lead) superficially similar when tarnished
practical_notes.market_notes: Globally traded precious metal; spot price lower than gold per troy ounce. Collector specimens from Kongsberg and Cobalt highly valued. Industrial demand in electronics and photography significant.

prompt_fragments: twisted wire silver on black matrix
brilliant silver-white metallic gleam fading to tarnished gray
arborescent silver branches like frozen lightning

chemical_profile.atomic_structure_notes: Isostructural with calcite (trigonal R-3c). Zn2+ occupies the divalent cation site. Forms solid solutions toward siderite (Fe), rhodochrosite (Mn), and otavite (Cd).
chemical_profile.chemistry_notes: Color is determined by trace substitution: Co gives blue-lavender (cobaltian smithsonite), Cu gives green or teal-blue (cuprian smithsonite), Mn gives pink, Fe gives yellow-brown, Cd gives colorless to pale yellow. Pure ZnCO3 is white to colorless. Each color variant has its own collector following.

crystallography.twinning: Rare
crystallography.crystallography_notes: Rarely forms well-developed crystals; typically botryoidal, stalactitic, massive, or as crusts with rounded surfaces. Botryoidal form with translucent pearl-like surfaces is classic and most recognizable. Earthy and cellular forms also occur.

physical_properties.physical_notes: Notably heavy for a non-metallic mineral (SG 4.3–4.5) due to Zn. The pearly botryoidal surfaces are lustrous and translucent, giving an almost ceramic quality. Effervesces in dilute HCl.

color_profiles.name: Cobaltian Smithsonite (Blue / Lavender)
color_profiles.cause: Co2+ substituting for Zn2+.
color_profiles.conditions: Tsumeb, Namibia; Laurion, Greece; Kelly Mine, New Mexico.

color_profiles.name: Cuprian Smithsonite (Green / Teal)
color_profiles.cause: Cu2+ substituting for Zn2+.
color_profiles.conditions: Arizona, New Mexico deposits; any Cu-rich zinc deposit oxidation zone.

color_profiles.name: Manganian Smithsonite (Pink)
color_profiles.cause: Mn2+ substituting for Zn2+.
color_profiles.conditions: Various zinc deposits with Mn.

color_profiles.name: White / Gray Smithsonite
color_profiles.cause: Near-pure ZnCO3 with minimal chromophore substitution.
color_profiles.conditions: Most common form; widespread.

color_profiles.name: Yellow / Brown Smithsonite
color_profiles.cause: Fe2+/Fe3+ substitution or iron oxide staining.
color_profiles.conditions: Fe-bearing zinc deposits.

appearance.visual_notes: Smithsonite is primarily known as a secondary zinc carbonate with exceptional color range and unusual pearlescent botryoidal habit. Cobalt-bearing blue smithsonite from Tsumeb and copper-bearing green from Arizona are collector favorites. The pearly luster of botryoidal forms is distinctive.

optical_and_special_effects.fluorescence: Some specimens fluoresce pale blue, green, or white under UV
optical_and_special_effects.optical_notes: Uniaxial negative. RI approximately 1.621–1.849 (high birefringence like other calcite-group members).

geologic_environments: oxidized zones of sphalerite-bearing polymetallic sulfide deposits
fractures and cavities in limestone above zinc ore
host_rocks: any host rock in oxidized zone of zinc deposit
notable_localities: Kelly Mine, Socorro County, New Mexico, USA (cobaltian lavender-blue)
Broken Hill, New South Wales, Australia
formation_and_occurrence.occurrence_notes: Classic zinc supergene mineral. Forms when oxidizing groundwaters rich in CO2 react with sphalerite (ZnS) and zinc-bearing fluids in carbonate host environments. Named after James Smithson, the British mineralogist and founder of the Smithsonian Institution (who also studied zinc minerals).

historical_uses: Zinc ore (minor source in oxidized deposits)
Gem and lapidary use (cabochons from colored botryoidal material)
Collector mineral
cultural_associations: Named after James Smithson (1765–1829) — founder of the Smithsonian Institution
Smithsonite specimens formed part of Smithson's own mineral collection
cultural_and_historical.folklore_notes: Smithsonite is named for James Smithson — chemist, mineralogist, and benefactor of the Smithsonian Institution. He actually studied calamine (a historical term covering hemimorphite and smithsonite). The mineral was formally named for him posthumously by François Sulpice Beudant in 1832.

interpretive_vibe.vibe_notes: Smithsonite is quiet but extraordinary — a carbonate that refracts light through translucent botryoidal skins in a dozen colors. The cobalt-blue variety from Tsumeb has a quality like polished sky.

practical_notes.toxicity: Zinc is an essential trace element; high concentrations in dust form warrants caution. Cadmium substitution in Cd-rich smithsonite specimens warrants extra care.
practical_notes.handling_notes: Relatively brittle; handle botryoidal masses carefully. Soft surfaces can be marred.
practical_notes.care_notes: Avoid acids. Clean with dry cloth. Do not soak in water. Store away from harder minerals.
treatments_or_synthetics: May be stabilized with resin for lapidary use
common_confusions: Hemimorphite (zinc silicate — harder; different crystal habit)
practical_notes.market_notes: Colored specimens (cobalt-blue, green, lavender) command collector premiums. White/gray masses are inexpensive. Lapidary-quality botryoidal material moderately priced.

prompt_fragments: pearlescent botryoidal smithsonite domes — periwinkle blue cobalt
apple-green cuprian smithsonite over gossan matrix
secondary zinc carbonate translucent skin on limestone

chemical_profile.ideal_formula: SiO2 (with Al3+ + radiation-induced hole color centers)
chemical_profile.atomic_structure_notes: Trigonal quartz framework. Color from Al3+ substituting for Si4+ in tetrahedral sites, combined with natural gamma irradiation creating trapped-hole color centers (Al-O-Al or Al-O-Si electron hole centers). The color is stable at low temperatures but anneals (destroys) at ~300°C — used to produce amethyst or pale quartz from smoky by heating.
chemical_profile.chemistry_notes: Color requires both aluminum impurity (very small amount — ~1 ppm Al) AND natural irradiation (alpha or gamma from radioactive minerals in host rock or from cosmic rays). Smoky quartz from granites and pegmatites near radioactive minerals (monazite, zircon, uraninite) is most common. Irradiation of clear quartz containing Al produces artificial smoky quartz (used in industry). 'Morion' is the very darkest, nearly opaque black variety.

crystallography.twinning: Brazil and Dauphiné law twinning common
crystallography.crystallography_notes: Identical prismatic hexagonal form to other quartz varieties. Large clear smoky quartz crystals are common — some of the largest quartz crystals known are smoky (multi-kilogram specimens from Alps, Brazil, Colorado). Terminations often complete and well-formed. Phantoms (growth zones) visible in some specimens.

physical_properties.physical_notes: Standard quartz properties. Large transparent crystals common. Color bleaches on heating above ~300°C. Cosmic-ray irradiation can produce smoky color in surface quartz over geologic time — some high-altitude smoky quartz is naturally irradiated by cosmic rays.

color_profiles.name: Classic Smoky Quartz
color_profiles.cause: Al-hole color centers from moderate irradiation dose.
color_profiles.conditions: Widespread in granites, pegmatites, and alpine veins.

color_profiles.name: Cairngorm
color_profiles.cause: Al-hole color centers with slight Fe contribution; lower irradiation dose.
color_profiles.conditions: Cairngorm Mountains, Scotland — traditional Scottish variety.

color_profiles.name: Morion
color_profiles.cause: Very high irradiation dose; very dense color center population.
color_profiles.conditions: High radiation environments; some Colorado, European specimens.

appearance.visual_notes: Smoky quartz produces the most varied depth range of any colored quartz — from barely-there smoke-gray to absolute black. Large faceted smoky quartz gems (hundreds to thousands of carats) are obtainable and affordable. The phantoms (ghost crystal growth zones) visible inside larger crystals add visual complexity.

optical_and_special_effects.fluorescence: None typically
optical_and_special_effects.optical_notes: Uniaxial positive. RI = 1.544–1.553. Standard quartz optics. Color anneals at ~300°C.

geologic_environments: alpine fissure veins (classic occurrence in Alps, Himalayas)
notable_localities: Pike's Peak and Lake George, Colorado, USA (large dark smoky crystals with amazonite)
Swiss Alps (Grimsel, Zermatt — alpine fissure veins)
Virgen del Pilar mine, Catamarca, Argentina
formation_and_occurrence.occurrence_notes: Smoky quartz is common worldwide wherever granitic rocks contain trace aluminum (universal) and natural radioactive minerals. Alpine fissure veins in the Swiss and Austrian Alps are famous for large, clear, well-formed smoky crystals deposited by hydrothermal solutions. The Cairngorm Mountains of Scotland historically supplied the Scottish Highland jewelry tradition. Colorado produces spectacular smoky crystals with amazonite feldspar.

historical_uses: Carved into bowls, figures, and jewelry in Roman and Chinese antiquity
Scottish Highland jewelry tradition — cairngorm in kilt pins and brooches
Art Deco smoky quartz in bold settings
Buddhist meditation beads (China)
Inuit and Arctic peoples carved smoky quartz
cultural_associations: Scottish national gem tradition — cairngorm in Highland dress
Smoky quartz as 'Morion' in medieval European gem records
Victorian mourning jewelry used dark stones including morion
Japanese tradition: smoky quartz (kurosuishō) as a meditation stone
cultural_and_historical.folklore_notes: Cairngorm quartz from the Cairngorm Mountains of Scotland is an iconic national gemstone tradition — large brown-gray crystals were set in the handles of Highland dirks, kilt pins, and traditional brooches. Victorian mourning jewelry used dark stones including morion (black smoky quartz) alongside jet. In Celtic tradition, smoky quartz was a stone of the gods of the underworld. Chinese Daoist tradition associated smoky quartz with the yin principle.

interpretive_vibe.vibe_notes: Smoky quartz is where quartz goes dark — radiation absorbed in aluminum defects, the landscape of granite made visible in crystal. Phantoms within large stones show the history of growth: clear to smoky to clearer again, time made visible.

practical_notes.toxicity: Non-toxic. Some radioactive-source material from high-radiation zones may have trace radioactivity but nowhere near hazardous for normal handling.
practical_notes.handling_notes: Durable standard quartz. Color stable in normal use. Avoid heating above 300°C (destroys color). Alpine vein crystals sometimes have delicate terminations worth protecting.
practical_notes.care_notes: Clean with warm soapy water. Ultrasonic safe. Avoid heat.
treatments_or_synthetics: Irradiation: clear quartz artificially irradiated to produce smoky color — common; disclosed in professional settings
Heating: to remove or modify color (yields pale or colorless quartz)
Synthetic smoky quartz: produced industrially
common_confusions: Morion vs. black tourmaline (elongated crystals; different habit)
Obsidian (no crystals; lower hardness; different occurrence)
practical_notes.market_notes: Inexpensive and widely available. Large faceted gems very affordable. Alpine specimens (Swiss, Colorado) command collector premium for quality. Most market material is from Brazil. Dark morion specimens rarer and slightly more prized.

prompt_fragments: large smoky quartz prism — alpine gray crystal from radiation alchemy
morion quartz near-black opacity — shadow crystallized
phantom smoky quartz: clear-to-dark growth zones visible in transparent gray

provenance.notes: Smoky quartz is a variety of quartz. mindat_id 3684 is the smoky quartz variety listing.

structural_units: S8 crown-shaped ring molecules packed in orthorhombic lattice
chemical_profile.atomic_structure_notes: Stable alpha-sulfur consists of S8 ring molecules arranged in an orthorhombic framework. Converts to monoclinic beta-sulfur above 96°C.
chemical_profile.chemistry_notes: Stable orthorhombic polymorph (alpha-sulfur) at surface conditions. Minor impurities of Se and As contribute to color variation. Strong diagnostic odor when scratched.

crystallography.twinning: Contact twins occasionally reported.
crystallography.crystallography_notes: Well-formed bipyramidal crystals common from sedimentary and volcanic deposits. Crystals often striated on prism faces. Converts to monoclinic beta-sulfur above 96°C — handle below this temperature.

physical_properties.physical_notes: Very low melting point (~113°C). Poor thermal conductor — can crack if heated suddenly (thermal shock). Strong diagnostic odor when scratched or struck. Excellent electrical insulator.

color_profiles.name: Volcanic Fumarolic Sulfur
color_profiles.cause: sublimation deposits with minor impurities from volcanic gases
color_profiles.conditions: active volcanic fumaroles

color_profiles.name: Sedimentary / Biogenic Sulfur
color_profiles.cause: bacterial reduction of sulfates (Desulfovibrio spp.)
color_profiles.conditions: cap rock of salt domes, evaporitic sediments

color_profiles.name: Orange-Red Variety
color_profiles.cause: arsenic or selenium impurities
color_profiles.conditions: specific localities with elevated impurity content

textures: earthy to powdery in massive form
appearance.visual_notes: Vivid sulfur-yellow color is highly diagnostic. Fine bipyramidal crystals from Sicily are among the most visually distinctive native element specimens. Surface luster characteristically resinous to waxy.

optical_and_special_effects.fluorescence: weak yellow-white under UV (some specimens)
optical_and_special_effects.optical_notes: Transparent crystals show resinous internal luster. Biaxial positive optical character.

geologic_environments: volcanic crater rims and fumarole fields
salt dome cap rock (Gulf Coast USA, Poland, Sicily)
notable_localities: Agrigento (Girgenti), Sicily, Italy (historically most famous crystals)
Gulf Coast salt domes, Louisiana and Texas, USA
Kawah Ijen, Java, Indonesia (active artisanal mining)
formation_and_occurrence.occurrence_notes: Two principal modes: fumarolic sublimation in volcanic settings and biogenic bacterial reduction of sulfate in evaporitic/salt dome environments. Sicilian sedimentary deposits historically yielded the finest collector crystals.

historical_uses: gunpowder formulation (sulfur, charcoal, potassium nitrate)
fumigation and pest control (antiquity to modern)
sulfuric acid production (Frasch process, contact process)
rubber vulcanization (19th century, Goodyear)
fungicides and pesticides
matches and pyrotechnics
bleaching of textiles and paper
wine preservation (sulfur dioxide)
cultural_associations: brimstone (biblical term — fire and brimstone)
hellfire imagery in Christian tradition
alchemical sulfur as one of three primes (tria prima: sulfur, mercury, salt)
volcanic deity offerings
Japanese traditional firework ingredient
cultural_and_historical.folklore_notes: Sulfur (brimstone) is one of the oldest known mineral substances — referenced in ancient Vedic texts, Homeric epics, and the Bible. The biblical 'fire and brimstone' likely references volcanic sulfur. Alchemy designated sulfur as one of the three primes representing the soul. Kawah Ijen, Indonesia, continues artisanal sulfur mining under conditions of extraordinary volcanic intensity — miners carry glowing blue-flaming sulfur on their backs through sulfurous fog.

interpretive_vibe.vibe_notes: Sulfur smells like the beginning and the end of the world. At Kawah Ijen, miners carry glowing blue flames of burning sulfur on their backs in the dark — one of Earth's most alien industrial scenes.

practical_notes.toxicity: low direct toxicity from mineral contact; sulfur dust is combustible and explosive in air; burning sulfur produces SO2 (severe respiratory irritant and toxic gas); prolonged exposure to SO2/H2S in volcanic environments is dangerous
practical_notes.handling_notes: Do not heat specimens — risk of thermal shock cracking and production of toxic SO2 gas. Handle brittle crystals carefully. Avoid creating dust. Store away from heat sources.
practical_notes.care_notes: Store away from heat and direct sunlight (above 96°C alters crystal structure to monoclinic). Clean with soft dry brush only — avoid water and acids. Do not steam clean.
treatments_or_synthetics: industrial sulfur produced by Frasch process (hot water injection into underground deposits)
by-product sulfur recovered from petroleum refining (Claus process)
common_confusions: orpiment (arsenic trisulfide — similar yellow, but higher luster, different streak)
wulfenite (tabular, orange-yellow, but denser and different crystal habits)
yellow fluorite (harder, cubic, different crystal habit)
yellow calcite (effervesces in HCl, different crystal habits)
practical_notes.market_notes: Fine crystallized specimens from Sicily (Agrigento/Caltanissetta) highly prized by collectors. Volcanic specimens from Ijen and Vulcano also collected. Industrial sulfur is a major commodity chemical.

prompt_fragments: vivid sulfur-yellow bipyramidal crystals on white matrix
volcanic fumarole encrustation in acid yellow and orange
glowing brimstone — ancient and alien

other_ids.note: Sunstone is a trade/variety name; parent mineral varies (oligoclase, labradorite, or orthoclase depending on source). No single Mindat ID covers all sunstones.

structural_units: TO4 framework tetrahedra in 3D feldspar framework
metallic platelet inclusions (hematite, goethite, or native copper)
chemical_profile.atomic_structure_notes: Sunstone is defined by its inclusions, not a distinct feldspar composition. The optical effect (aventurescence) comes from oriented metallic platelets — hematite or goethite in oligoclase sunstone, or native copper in Oregon labradorite sunstone — oriented parallel to crystallographic planes in the feldspar host.
chemical_profile.chemistry_notes: Oregon sunstone is a labradorite-composition plagioclase (An45–An68) with native copper inclusions. Norwegian sunstone is oligoclase with hematite platelets. Indian and other commercial sunstone is typically oligoclase with hematite or goethite inclusions. Copper-bearing Oregon sunstone is unique globally.

crystallography.twinning: Polysynthetic albite and pericline twinning in plagioclase varieties.
crystallography.crystallography_notes: The metallic inclusions responsible for aventurescence are aligned parallel to {001} or other crystallographic planes, which is why the schiller is directional.

physical_properties.physical_notes: Two cleavage directions typical of feldspars. Oregon sunstone ranges from colorless through yellow, orange, red, and deep copper-red/green. The more copper present, the deeper and more saturated the color and the stronger the aventurescence.

color_profiles.name: oregon-sunstone-copper
color_profiles.cause: Native copper inclusions (platelets and dispersed copper) provide both color saturation and metallic aventurescent schiller.
color_profiles.conditions: Oregon, USA (Lake County — Ponderosa Mine and associated claims). The copper content varies by growth zone, creating color-zoned and bicolor stones unique globally.

color_profiles.name: norway-sunstone
color_profiles.cause: Hematite platelet inclusions oriented parallel to {001} in oligoclase matrix.
color_profiles.conditions: Tvedestrand district, Norway. Feldspar pegmatites.

color_profiles.name: india-sunstone
color_profiles.cause: Goethite and hematite platelet inclusions in oligoclase.
color_profiles.conditions: India; common commercial sunstone.

color_profiles.name: colorless-clear-oregon
color_profiles.cause: Very low copper content; body color near-colorless.
color_profiles.conditions: Oregon; facetable transparent material without strong color.

appearance.visual_notes: Aventurescence in sunstone appears as a glittering, spangling metallic flash — brighter and more dramatic than the soft glow of adularescence in moonstone. Oregon sunstone is the only known feldspar with native copper inclusions, giving it a uniquely warm, saturated color. The metallic sparkle in Norwegian sunstone has a coppery-gold quality.

optical_and_special_effects.asterism: Rare 4-ray star sunstone reported in Oregon material.
optical_and_special_effects.iridescence: Aventurescence: metallic glitter and schiller from oriented hematite, goethite, or native copper platelets. More dramatic than moonstone adularescence — appears as individual flashing points or a broad metallic sheen depending on inclusion density and size.
optical_and_special_effects.pleochroism: Oregon sunstone shows moderate to strong pleochroism: colorless to pale yellow to deep orange-red depending on crystal orientation.
optical_and_special_effects.optical_notes: Aventurescence differs from adularescence (moonstone) and labradorescence (labradorite) in mechanism: it is reflection from individual metallic platelet surfaces, not thin-film interference. Oregon sunstone's pleochroism is notable and important for cutting orientation. The copper gives it an absorption spectrum distinct from hematite-bearing sunstone.

geologic_environments: basaltic lava flows (Oregon sunstone — in basalt flows, not pegmatites)
notable_localities: Oregon, USA (Lake County — Ponderosa Mine and Glass Buttes; labradorite with native copper; official state gemstone of Oregon since 1987)
Norway (Tvedestrand, Aust-Agder — oligoclase with hematite; original European sunstone)
India (Rajasthan — oligoclase/labradorite with hematite/goethite; large commercial source)
Canada (Labrador — some sunstone adjacent to labradorite occurrences)
formation_and_occurrence.occurrence_notes: Oregon sunstone occurs uniquely in basaltic lava flows of the Miocene age. The copper precipitated as native metal within the crystallizing feldspar. This formation in basalt (rather than pegmatite) is the primary geological distinction of Oregon sunstone.

historical_uses: Norwegian sunstone historically collected and used as ornamental stone
Oregon sunstone designated official state gemstone of Oregon in 1987

Viking sunstone (sólarsteinn): some researchers propose that Viking navigators used a transparent birefringent crystal — possibly calcite (Iceland spar) or cordierite — as a navigation tool; rarely associated with feldspar sunstone
cultural_associations: Associated with the sun, solar energy, and warmth across modern crystal traditions
Oregon sunstone: symbol of Pacific Northwest geology
cultural_and_historical.folklore_notes: The term 'sólarsteinn' (sunstone) from Norse sagas refers to a navigational crystal, which has generated scholarly debate. Most evidence points to calcite (Iceland spar) or cordierite as the actual navigational stone, not feldspar sunstone — the overlap of the trade name with the saga reference is a modern confusion.

interpretive_vibe.vibe_notes: Sunstone has a grounded, warm, solar quality that contrasts with the cool lunar depth of moonstone. The aventurescent glitter reads as scattered light, like sunlight on moving water or the scatter of a copper fire. Oregon sunstone, with its native copper and rich saturated color, is among the most texturally and chromatically complex feldspars.

practical_notes.toxicity: low
practical_notes.handling_notes: Safe for handling. Two cleavage directions mean it can chip if struck. Moderate hardness (6–6.5) makes it vulnerable to scratching with daily wear.
practical_notes.care_notes: Do not use ultrasonic or steam cleaners. Clean with damp cloth. Avoid hard knocks. Remove before physical activity.
treatments_or_synthetics: Glass with copper aventurine (goldstone/aventurine glass) is a common imitation widely confused with sunstone
Goldstone is man-made glass with copper particles — not a mineral
Some commercial 'sunstone' is dyed or treated material
Synthetic sunstone exists as lab-grown feldspar but is not widespread commercially
common_confusions: goldstone (aventurine glass — man-made copper-glass imitation extremely common and widely mislabeled)
practical_notes.market_notes: Oregon sunstone is the prestige variety; fine deep-red and bicolor stones command premium prices. The Ponderosa Mine offers public dig opportunities. Norwegian sunstone is less common commercially. Most inexpensive commercial sunstone is Indian oligoclase. Goldstone (glass) is pervasively sold as sunstone; buyers should verify.

prompt_fragments: warm copper-orange metallic glitter in transparent feldspar
solar fragment scattered-light aventurescence
Oregon native copper feldspar with deep bicolor red-green pleochroism

provenance.notes: No single Mindat ID covers all sunstones; oligoclase ID used as primary reference. Goldstone confusion documented — important for market notes.

structural_units: SiO4 tetrahedra linked into a 3D framework
parallel fibrous microcrystalline silica replacing amphibole fibers
residual goethite and iron oxide along fiber axes
chemical_profile.atomic_structure_notes: Tiger's eye is a pseudomorph: silica replaced the original crocidolite (riebeckite variety, a blue sodic amphibole) while preserving the fibrous structure. The parallel iron oxide fibers (mainly goethite) provide color and the chatoyant effect.
chemical_profile.chemistry_notes: Bulk composition is predominantly SiO2. Residual iron as goethite (FeO(OH)) or hematite (Fe2O3) within the silica fiber matrix drives both color and optical effect. In hawk's eye (unoxidized form), original crocidolite iron is less altered.

crystallography.unit_cell_notes: Microcrystalline; the macroscopic fibrous texture is inherited from the crocidolite precursor, not a crystallographic feature of the quartz itself.
crystallography.crystallography_notes: The fibrous appearance is a pseudomorphic replacement texture. Parallel silica fibers occupy the former crocidolite fiber positions, with iron oxide concentrated along fiber boundaries and axes.

physical_properties.physical_notes: Silky luster on polished surfaces is characteristic. Lower hardness than typical quartz reflects the fibrous, replacement origin. Fibrous texture gives specimens a directional feel — sheen changes with orientation.

color_profiles.name: tiger-eye-classic
color_profiles.cause: Goethite (iron oxyhydroxide) inclusions within parallel silica fibers, replacing oxidized crocidolite.
color_profiles.conditions: Oxidized pseudomorph; most common commercially available form.

color_profiles.name: hawk-eye
color_profiles.cause: Partial or unoxidized crocidolite fibers preserved or less fully replaced; blue color from original riebeckite amphibole.
color_profiles.conditions: Less oxidized form where blue crocidolite color partially persists.

color_profiles.name: ox-eye
color_profiles.cause: Further oxidation of goethite to hematite shifts color toward red-brown.
color_profiles.conditions: More strongly oxidized specimens.

color_profiles.name: pietersite
color_profiles.cause: Brecciated combination of tiger's eye, hawk's eye, and jasper fragments.
color_profiles.conditions: Brecciated and recemented material; Namibia and China.

appearance.visual_notes: Chatoyancy (cat's eye effect) is the defining visual feature. The golden-brown silky shimmer moves across the surface as the viewing angle shifts. The parallel fiber orientation is always visible in cross-section.

optical_and_special_effects.chatoyancy: Strong; classic golden-brown cat's eye effect from parallel goethite/iron oxide fibers within the silica matrix. Effect is best in cabochon cut.
optical_and_special_effects.optical_notes: Chatoyancy in tiger's eye is caused by light reflection from the oriented parallel fibers of silica with included iron oxide. The effect is most pronounced when the c-axis of the fibrous mass is oriented perpendicular to the line of sight. Hawk's eye shows the same chatoyant mechanism with a blue-gray base.

geologic_environments: ancient iron formations with amphibolite veins
notable_localities: South Africa (Northern Cape Province — Griquatown area; primary and largest source)
formation_and_occurrence.occurrence_notes: Tiger's eye forms when crocidolite (blue asbestos) in banded iron formation sequences is replaced by silica-rich fluids. The replacement is pseudomorphic — silica fills the space of the amphibole fibers while iron oxidizes to goethite, preserving the parallel fibrous texture.

historical_uses: Popular ornamental stone for carvings and amulets in 19th and 20th century
Used in Victorian jewelry
Carved into scarabs, figurines, and decorative objects in South African craft traditions
cultural_associations: Associated with courage, focus, and vision in many modern crystal traditions
The 'eye' motif connects it to concepts of vigilance and protection across cultures
cultural_and_historical.folklore_notes: The cat's eye effect has long been associated with the protective gaze of predatory animals. Tiger's eye has been used as a protective talisman against the evil eye in various traditions.

interpretive_vibe.vibe_notes: Tiger's eye has a quality of focused, watchful warmth — it looks like something is looking back. The chatoyancy gives it movement and life. The amber-gold color range places it in hot, dry, predatory aesthetic territory.

practical_notes.toxicity: SAFETY NOTE: Fully pseudomorphed tiger's eye specimens are generally considered safe for handling — the original crocidolite (blue asbestos) fibers are encased in silica. HOWEVER: cutting, grinding, sanding, or polishing dry tiger's eye can release hazardous silica dust and potentially residual fibrous material. Do NOT sand, polish, or cut without proper respiratory protection (P100 minimum). Lapidaries must use wet cutting and proper dust containment. The hazard is from the dust, not from holding polished specimens.
practical_notes.handling_notes: Polished finished specimens safe for everyday handling and wearing. Do not cut, sand, or grind without proper wet-cutting equipment and respiratory protection.
practical_notes.care_notes: Avoid prolonged water exposure (can affect silky sheen). Avoid harsh chemicals. Clean with soft damp cloth.
treatments_or_synthetics: Some tiger's eye dyed red or blue to simulate hawk's eye or ox-eye forms
Heat treatment can alter color
Synthetic tiger's eye does not exist commercially at scale
common_confusions: other chatoyant minerals (chrysoberyl cat's eye is much rarer and more valuable)
practical_notes.market_notes: Extremely common in the gem and mineral market. South Africa is the overwhelming dominant source. Most commercial material is inexpensive. High-quality chatoyancy and clean color command modest premiums. Pietersite is more valuable than standard tiger's eye.

prompt_fragments: golden silky chatoyant cat's eye effect in amber-brown fibrous silica
predator-eye desert surveillance mineral with shimmering fiber bands
pseudomorph after crocidolite with iron oxide golden shimmer

provenance.notes: Toxicity section is critical — asbestos precursor mineral requires careful communication. Polished specimens safe; dust is the hazard.

chemical_profile.simplified_formula: Complex Na/Ca boron cyclosilicate with variable Y and Z cations
structural_units: Si6O18 six-membered rings of SiO4 tetrahedra (cyclosilicate)
YO6 and ZO6 octahedral chains parallel to c-axis
chemical_profile.atomic_structure_notes: Cyclosilicate (ring silicate). Six-membered Si6O18 rings are linked by BO3 triangles and chains of octahedra into a complex, non-centrosymmetric framework. The formula site X accommodates Na, Ca, K, or vacancy; Y accommodates Fe2+, Mg, Li, Al, Mn2+; Z is dominantly Al with minor Fe, Mg, Cr. The W site carries OH, F, or O at the base of the structure.
chemical_profile.chemistry_notes: Boron is an essential element — tourmaline is one of very few silicates that requires B. The enormous compositional range across X, Y, Z, V, and W sites produces more IMA-approved species than any other mineral group (~40+ species). Cu2+ in the Y-site produces the coveted Paraíba neon-blue color. Mn produces pink (rubellite/elbaite). Fe2+ produces deep blue (indicolite) and Fe-rich schorl black.

crystallography.unit_cell_notes: a ≈ 15.9–16.0 Å, c ≈ 7.1–7.2 Å depending on composition; Z = 3
crystallography.twinning: Rarely twinned; knee-shaped twins known but uncommon
crystallography.crystallography_notes: Trigonal symmetry means tourmaline crystals lack a center of symmetry — one end of a c-axis prism is morphologically and structurally different from the other (hemimorphic). This causes piezoelectric and pyroelectric properties. Prism faces are typically striated parallel to the c-axis — a hallmark identification feature. Cross-sections are characteristically rounded-triangular to pseudo-hexagonal.

physical_properties.physical_notes: SG varies considerably with composition: Fe-rich schorl reaches ~3.2–3.3, while Li-Al-rich elbaite is ~3.0–3.1 and Mg-dominant dravite ~3.0. The broad hardness and SG ranges reflect compositional diversity across species. Pyroelectric and piezoelectric due to non-centrosymmetric structure.

color_profiles.name: schorl
color_profiles.cause: High Fe2+ and Fe3+ content; Fe2+–Fe3+ intervalence charge transfer.
color_profiles.conditions: Most common tourmaline species; Fe-rich pegmatites and granites worldwide.

color_profiles.name: rubellite
color_profiles.cause: Mn2+ and Mn3+ in the Y-site of elbaite.
color_profiles.conditions: Li-rich granitic pegmatites; Brazil (Minas Gerais), Madagascar, Nigeria, Afghanistan.

color_profiles.name: indicolite
color_profiles.cause: Fe2+ and Fe3+ in elbaite (Li-Al-rich end member) producing blue hues.
color_profiles.conditions: Gem-quality indicolite from Brazil, Nigeria, Mozambique.

color_profiles.name: verdelite
color_profiles.cause: Fe2+ and mixed Fe2+/Fe3+ in elbaite; Cr3+ in chrome tourmaline (dravite/uvite).
color_profiles.conditions: Widespread in pegmatites; chrome tourmaline from Tanzania and Kenya is vivid Cr-green.

color_profiles.name: paraiba-tourmaline
color_profiles.cause: Cu2+ (and Mn for violet tones) in the Y-site; extremely rare Cu-bearing elbaite.
color_profiles.conditions: Originally from Paraíba, Brazil (Batalha mine discovered 1989); also Nigeria and Mozambique.

color_profiles.name: watermelon-tourmaline
color_profiles.cause: Growth zoning: Mn-rich (pink) core crystallized before Fe-richer (green) outer zones.
color_profiles.conditions: Li-rich pegmatites; classic from Maine USA, Brazil, Afghanistan.

color_profiles.name: dravite
color_profiles.cause: Mg-dominant Y-site composition; Fe minor.
color_profiles.conditions: Metamorphic rocks, marbles, schists; classic dravite from Dobrova, Slovenia (type locality).

color_profiles.name: colorless-achroite
color_profiles.cause: Very low Fe, Mn, and other chromophores in elbaite.
color_profiles.conditions: Rare; gem-quality achroite from Elba, Italy (type locality for elbaite).

common_forms: long striated prisms with rounded-triangular cross-sections
appearance.visual_notes: The striated prism faces running parallel to the long axis (c-axis) are diagnostic in hand specimens. Cross-sections showing rounded triangular outline are highly characteristic. Watermelon tourmaline slices are immediately recognizable. Schorl is ubiquitous as jet-black prismatic crystals in pegmatites globally.

optical_and_special_effects.fluorescence: Generally inert to weak; some pink/red elbaite may show weak orange or red under LW UV
optical_and_special_effects.chatoyancy: Cat's-eye tourmaline known; parallel tube inclusions or growth channels along c-axis
optical_and_special_effects.pleochroism: Strong to very strong in most colored varieties. Dichroic. Indicolite: dark blue vs. pale blue/colorless along c vs. perpendicular. Rubellite: deep red vs. pale pink. Green varieties: dark green vs. yellow-green. Pleochroism is one of the strongest in the gem world for indicolite and rubellite.
optical_and_special_effects.optical_notes: Uniaxial negative. RI varies by composition: ~1.614–1.666 (range across species). Birefringence ~0.014–0.040. Strong pleochroism means gem cutters orient stones to show best color face-up. Paraíba tourmaline shows intense neon saturation visible even in low light.

notable_localities: Minas Gerais, Brazil (elbaite, rubellite, indicolite, watermelon — world's premier locality)
Ogun and Ebonyi states, Nigeria (Paraíba-type Cu-bearing tourmaline)
Pala district, San Diego County, California, USA (elbaite, rubellite)
Mount Mica and Dunton, Maine, USA (watermelon, elbaite)
formation_and_occurrence.occurrence_notes: Tourmaline's requirement for boron means it is enriched in B-bearing magmatic systems, especially evolved granitic pegmatites. It is one of the last minerals to crystallize in pegmatitic sequences and can tolerate a wide range of hydrothermal temperatures. It is also a robust detrital mineral in sediments due to its hardness.

birthstone_status: Rubellite and pink tourmaline recognized for October
historical_uses: Dutch traders in the 18th century imported tourmaline from Ceylon (Sri Lanka) and noted that a charged crystal would attract and then repel ash from a pipe — earning the name 'aschentrekker' (ash drawer), reflecting its pyroelectric property
Empress Dowager Cixi of China was a major collector of pink tourmaline (rubellite) from California's San Diego County mines in the early 1900s
Used as a gemstone in India for centuries; pink and blue varieties from Sri Lanka known as 'rubies' and 'sapphires' historically
cultural_associations: Paraíba tourmaline is one of the most coveted gem discoveries of the 20th century, first found in 1989 by Heitor Dimas Barbosa after years of digging
Rainbow tourmaline slices used in modern mineral art and jewelry for color zoning display
cultural_and_historical.folklore_notes: Tourmaline was called the 'electric stone' after its pyroelectric properties were described by scientists in the 18th century. It was used to clean tobacco pipes by Dutch sailors because of its ash-attracting properties when heated.

interpretive_vibe.vibe_notes: Tourmaline is the most compositionally diverse gem mineral group on Earth — it can be literally any color, making it a universal chromatic reference. Watermelon tourmaline cross-sections are one of the most visually iconic mineral forms. Paraíba's neon glow is among the most intense colors in the gem world.

practical_notes.toxicity: low
practical_notes.handling_notes: Durable; conchoidal fracture means edges chip. Handle faceted stones carefully. Acicular specimens are delicate.
practical_notes.care_notes: Safe for ultrasonic in most cases unless heavily included or fracture-filled. Warm soapy water suitable. Avoid prolonged heat (pyroelectric effect, though not damaging at normal temperatures).
treatments_or_synthetics: Heat treatment: used to improve color in some rubellite and brownish elbaite; not always detectable
Irradiation: used to produce pink from colorless or pale material; may fade
Synthetic tourmaline: not commercially significant
Coating: occasionally used on pale stones
Fracture filling: rare but known
common_confusions: Paraíba vs. neon apatite, synthetic spinel
Green tourmaline vs. emerald, tsavorite garnet
Schorl vs. black tourmaline vs. hornblende, aegirine
practical_notes.market_notes: Paraíba tourmaline (Cu-bearing) commands extraordinary prices — top specimens far exceed diamond per carat. Rubellite, indicolite, and chrome tourmaline are premium collector gems. Schorl is inexpensive and abundant.
